```mermaid
    Vout[Output Voltage Vo measured between Wiper C and Ground B] --- C
    Vout --- B
    
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#ffd966,stroke:#996600
    style Vsrc fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style Vout fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style R1 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style R2 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style GND fill:#666666,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:** The resistance element runs from Terminal A (left) to Terminal B (right). The wiper at C taps a fraction of the total resistance. The excitation source $V_s$ is connected across A–B, and the output is taken between the wiper C and ground (B).

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Block Diagram of a Potentiometric Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Measurand: Linear Displacement x OR Angular Rotation theta] --> ACT[Mechanical Actuator: Shaft or Slider Rod]
    ACT --> WP[Potentiometer Transducer with Wiper]
    WP --> EX[Stable DC Excitation Source Vs 5V to 24V]
    
    WP -- Tapped Voltage Vo --> SIG[Signal Conditioning: Buffer Amplifier High Zin]
    SIG --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    ADC --> PROC[Microcontroller or PLC]
    PROC --> DISP[Display OR Control Actuator]
    
    style M fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style ACT fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style WP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style EX fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style SIG fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style ADC fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style PROC fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style DISP fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
```

> **Why the buffer amplifier?** Its purpose is to provide a high input impedance to the potentiometer's output node, *minimizing the loading effect* described in Derivation 2. An op-amp voltage follower (unity-gain buffer) with input impedance $\geq 10^{12}\,\Omega$ is the standard KTU-recommended solution.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Equivalent Circuit of a Loaded Potentiometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VS[Source Vs] -- Positive terminal --> A
    A --> R1seg[R1 = x times Rtotal]
    R1seg --> WIP[Wiper Node C]
    WIP --> R2seg[R2 = 1 minus x times Rtotal]
    R2seg --> B
    B -- Negative terminal --> GND[Return to Source]
    
    WIP --> RL[Load Resistance RL]
    RL --> GND
    
    VOM[Voltmeter reads Vo across RL] --- WIP
    VOM --- GND
    
    style VS fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style R1seg fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style R2seg fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style RL fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style WIP fill:#ffd966,stroke:#996600
    style VOM fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style GND fill:#666666,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading:** This shows that $R_L$ is in **parallel** with $R_1$ (the segment between terminal A and the wiper). The parallel combination is then in **series** with $R_2$ across the source $V_s$. The voltmeter reads the voltage across $R_L$, which equals the voltage across the parallel combination $R_1 \parallel R_L$.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Transfer Characteristic (Output Voltage vs. Wiper Position)

> Since Mermaid cannot natively render XY plots, this is represented as a **decision-table** form showing the key landmark points of the ideal vs. loaded characteristic.

| Wiper position $x$ | $V_o$ ideal (V) | $V_o$ loaded with $R_L = 10 R_{total}$ (V) | $V_o$ loaded with $R_L = R_{total}$ (V) | Loading Error (%) at $R_L = R_{total}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 (at A) | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 0.1 | $0.10 V_s$ | $0.0976 V_s$ | $0.0909 V_s$ | 9.09 |
| 0.2 | $0.20 V_s$ | $0.1905 V_s$ | $0.1667 V_s$ | 16.67 |
| **0.5 (mid-stroke — worst case)** | $0.50 V_s$ | $0.4444 V_s$ | $0.3333 V_s$ | **33.33 (max)** |
| 0.8 | $0.80 V_s$ | $0.7619 V_s$ | $0.6667 V_s$ | 16.67 |
| 0.9 | $0.90 V_s$ | $0.8780 V_s$ | $0.8182 V_s$ | 9.09 |
| 1.0 (at B) | $1.00 V_s$ | $1.00 V_s$ | $1.00 V_s$ | 0.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The S-shaped deviation of the loaded curve from the ideal straight line is the **defining visual signature** of the loading effect. It is *symmetric about $x = 0.5$* and *pinches to zero* at both ends.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a potentiometer as a resistance transducer. List its main types based on the construction of the resistance element.**

**Model Answer:**

A potentiometer is a passive, three-terminal variable resistance transducer that converts a mechanical displacement (linear or angular) into a proportional output voltage by acting as an adjustable voltage divider. The wiper mechanically taps a fraction of the total resistance.

**Main types based on the resistance element:**

1. **Wire-Wound Potentiometer** — Nichrome or constantan wire wound on a former. Used in industrial position feedback. Resolution is finite (depends on number of turns).
2. **Carbon-Film (or Cermet) Potentiometer** — A carbon or cermet track deposited on a phenolic substrate. Used in consumer electronics. Resolution is effectively infinite.
3. **Conductive Plastic Potentiometer** — A plastic track loaded with conductive particles. Used in precision servo systems. Very long life ($\sim 10^7$ cycles) and excellent resolution.

*(Examiner allocates ~1 mark for definition, ~2 marks for types.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the concept of loading effect in a potentiometer. What is the design rule to minimize it?**

**Model Answer:**

**Loading Effect:** When a finite load resistance $R_L$ is connected across the output of a potentiometer, the load draws current and distorts the ideal voltage-divider action. The output voltage becomes *non-linear* and is no longer a linear function of wiper position.

**Mathematical form of the loaded output:**
$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{1 + x(1 - x) \cdot \dfrac{R_{total}}{R_L}}$$

**Design Rule to Minimize Loading Error:** Choose the load resistance to be at least **10 times** the total potentiometer resistance, i.e., $R_L \geq 10 R_{total}$. This restricts the loading error at mid-stroke to less than **2.4%**.

*(Examiner allocates ~1.5 marks for explanation, ~1.5 marks for design rule.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE module questions for Module 2 offer internal choice. Below are **two alternative 14-mark questions** on potentiometers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a linear potentiometer as a displacement transducer. Derive the output voltage expression for both **no-load and loaded** conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear potentiometer transducer has a total resistance of **5 kΩ** and is excited by a **20 V** DC source. The wiper is positioned at **40%** of the full range. The output is connected to a measuring instrument with input resistance of **20 kΩ**. Calculate:
  - (i) Output voltage under no-load condition. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) Output voltage under loaded condition. **(3 marks)**
  - (iii) Maximum possible loading error (in %) and the wiper position at which it occurs. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Theory and Derivation:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
A linear potentiometer consists of a straight resistance element of total resistance $R_{total}$ across which a stable DC excitation $V_s$ is applied. The wiper, mechanically linked to the measurand, slides along the element and divides $R_{total}$ into $R_1$ and $R_2$ as described in Section 3.1. The output voltage at the wiper is proportional to the displacement.

**No-load derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, $V_o = V_s \cdot x$, where $x$ is the fractional wiper position.

**Loaded derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.2, the loaded output is:
$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{x}{1 + x(1 - x) \cdot \dfrac{R_{total}}{R_L}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $R_{total} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_s = 20 \text{ V}$, $x = 0.4$, $R_L = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**(i) No-load output voltage:**
$$V_o = 20 \cdot 0.4 = \mathbf{8.00 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Loaded output voltage:**

Compute the correction term:
$$x(1 - x) \cdot \frac{R_{total}}{R_L} = (0.4)(0.6) \cdot \frac{5}{20} = 0.24 \cdot 0.25 = 0.06$$

$$V_o = 20 \cdot \frac{0.4}{1 + 0.06} = 20 \cdot \frac{0.4}{1.06} = \frac{8.00}{1.06} \approx \mathbf{7.547 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Maximum loading error:**

The maximum error occurs at $x = 0.5$. Using the formula:
$$\epsilon_{max} = \frac{R_{total}}{4 R_L + R_{total}} = \frac{5}{4(20) + 5} = \frac{5}{85} \approx 0.0588$$

$$\epsilon_{max}\% = \mathbf{5.88\%}$$

The wiper position at which this maximum error occurs is **x = 0.5 (mid-stroke)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of potentiometric transducers used in instrumentation. Explain the **resolution** and **sensitivity** of a potentiometer. How does the wire-wound type differ from the conductive-plastic type in terms of resolution? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotary wire-wound potentiometer has a total resistance of **10 kΩ**, is excited by **15 V**, has **500 turns**, and a maximum rotation of **300°**. Calculate:
  - (i) Sensitivity in V/° and in V/rad. **(3 marks)**
  - (ii) Resolution expressed as a percentage of full scale. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The smallest angular displacement it can mechanically resolve. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

**Classification (3 marks):** Potentiometric transducers are classified as:
1. **Linear (Translational) Potentiometers** — for linear displacement.
2. **Rotary (Angular) Potentiometers** — single-turn and multi-turn, for angular displacement.
3. Based on track material: **Wire-wound**, **Carbon-film / Cermet**, **Conductive Plastic**, and **Hybrid (e.g., Hybritron®)**.

**Resolution (2 marks):** Resolution is the smallest *fractional* change in input that produces a measurable change in output. For a wire-wound pot with $N$ turns, $R_{es} = 1/N$ (finite). For a conductive-plastic pot, the track is continuous (no discrete steps), so resolution is theoretically infinite, limited in practice only by contact noise ($\sim 0.1\%$).

**Sensitivity (2 marks):** Sensitivity is the rate of change of output voltage per unit mechanical input. For a linear pot, $S = V_s/L$ [V/m]. For a rotary pot, $S = V_s/\theta_{max}$ [V/rad]. It is a *constant* independent of wiper position for the ideal (unloaded) case.

**Wire-Wound vs. Conductive Plastic — Resolution:** Wire-wound pots have *finite* resolution (e.g., $0.1\%$ for a 1000-turn pot) because the wiper transitions from turn to turn in discrete steps, producing a *staircase* output. Conductive plastic pots have *effectively infinite* resolution because the resistance element is a smooth, continuous film — output is a *smooth ramp*, with no steps.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $R_{total} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_s = 15 \text{ V}$, $N = 500$ turns, $\theta_{max} = 300°$.

**(i) Sensitivity in V/°:**
$$S = \frac{V_s}{\theta_{max}} = \frac{15}{300} = \mathbf{0.05 \text{ V/°}}$$

**Sensitivity in V/rad:** Converting 300° to radians:
$$300° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 5.236 \text{ rad}$$

$$S = \frac{15}{5.236} = \mathbf{2.865 \text{ V/rad}}$$

**(ii) Resolution (% of FSO):**
$$R_{es} = \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 = \mathbf{0.2\% \text{ of FSO}}$$

**(iii) Smallest resolvable angular displacement:**
$$\Delta \theta_{min} = \frac{\theta_{max}}{N} = \frac{300°}{500} = \mathbf{0.6°}$$

Correspondingly, the smallest resolvable output step:
$$\Delta V_{min} = \frac{V_s}{N} = \frac{15}{500} = \mathbf{30 \text{ mV}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dimensional analysis of $x(1-x)$:** Many students compute $(0.4)(0.6)$ correctly but then multiply by $R_{total}/R_L$ in the wrong units (e.g., mixing $\Omega$ and $k\Omega$). *Always normalize* the ratio to a dimensionless number.
> 2. **Confusing the position of the load:** A load at the wiper introduces loading error; a load across the entire source (between A and B) does not affect the wiper voltage (it just increases source current). Draw the circuit before writing equations.
> 3. **Forgetting the mid-stroke maximum:** Many students compute loading error at the *given* $x$ instead of at $x = 0.5$. When the question asks for the **maximum** loading error, the answer is *always* at $x = 0.5$.
> 4. **Unit mixing in sensitivity:** $V_s / \theta_{max}$ requires $\theta$ in *radians* for SI units. For practical engineering, $V_s/\theta$ in V/° is also accepted by KTU — but be **consistent**.
> 5. **Resolution of wire-wound vs. plastic:** Students frequently swap these. Memorize: **wire-wound → finite (stepped output); plastic/film → infinite (smooth output).**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (save this for the night before the exam):**

- ✅ A **potentiometer** is a **passive, 3-terminal** resistive transducer working on the **voltage-divider principle**.
- ✅ The wiper divides $R_{total}$ into $R_1$ and $R_2$ such that $R_1 + R_2 = R_{total}$.
- ✅ **Ideal (no-load) output:** $V_o = V_s \cdot x$ — perfectly linear, where $x$ is the fractional wiper position.
- ✅ **Loaded output:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_s \cdot x}{1 + x(1-x) \cdot R_{total}/R_L}$ — non-linear, deviates most at mid-stroke.
- ✅ **Maximum loading error** occurs at $x = 0.5$ (mid-stroke), and equals $\dfrac{R_{total}}{4R_L + R_{total}}$ as a fraction.
- ✅ **Design rule to limit error below 2.4%:** Choose $R_L \geq 10 R_{total}$.
- ✅ **Sensitivity** is a *constant* for an ideal pot: $S = V_s/L$ (linear) or $S = V_s/\theta_{max}$ (rotary).
- ✅ **Resolution** of wire-wound pots: $1/N$, where $N$ = number of wire turns. **Resolution of film/plastic pots:** effectively infinite.
- ✅ **Types by motion:** Linear (translational), Rotary (angular), Multi-turn (helical).
- ✅ **Types by element:** Wire-wound (nichrome/constantan), Carbon-film, Cermet, Conductive plastic, Hybrid.
- ✅ **Use a unity-gain buffer (op-amp voltage follower)** in the signal-conditioning stage to present a high $Z_{in}$ load and *eliminate* the loading effect.
- ✅ **Zero-order sensor** — output is directly proportional to input (no integration or differentiation).
- ✅ **Applications:** Throttle position sensor, joystick, liquid-level float, valve-position transmitter, flight-control surface feedback, robotic joint angle.
- ✅ **Common power ratings:** **2 W** and **5 W** — never exceed $V_s^2/R_{total}$.
- ✅ **Major limitation:** Loading error (solved by buffer), finite resolution of wire-wound types (solved by plastic types), and mechanical wear (solved by hybrid types).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain Gauge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strain Gauge

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Strain Gauge** (also called a *strain gage*) is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a proportional change in electrical resistance. It operates on the fundamental principle of the **Piezoresistive Effect** combined with the geometric effect of dimensional change under applied stress.

Mathematically, a strain gauge transduces a mechanical input quantity (strain $\varepsilon$, dimensionless, often expressed in microstrain $\mu\varepsilon$) into an electrical output quantity (change in resistance $\Delta R$ or bridge output voltage $V_o$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Terminology):**
> A strain gauge is a device whose electrical resistance changes when it is subjected to mechanical strain. The ratio of fractional change in resistance to the applied strain is defined as the **Gauge Factor (GF)**, which is the single most important figure of merit for any strain gauge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain ($\varepsilon$)** is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of change in length to original length: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$. In engineering practice, strain is commonly expressed in **microstrain ($1\,\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$)**. Typical values encountered in practical measurements range from $10\,\mu\varepsilon$ to $2000\,\mu\varepsilon$.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a long, thin rubber band connected in an electrical circuit with a small battery and an ammeter.

- When you **stretch** the rubber band, it becomes **longer and thinner**, making it harder for electricity to flow. The ammeter reading drops.
- When you **compress** (squeeze) the rubber band, it becomes **shorter and fatter**, allowing more current. The ammeter reading rises.

A **strain gauge behaves exactly like this rubber band**, except it is made of a metallic foil or semiconductor material (not rubber), and it is bonded firmly to the surface of a structural element (a beam, a shaft, a bridge) whose strain we wish to measure. As the structure deforms under load, the gauge deforms identically, producing a tiny but measurable change in resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy Summary Table**

| Real-World Object | Physical Quantity | Strain Gauge Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Rubber band being stretched | Elongation | Tensile strain $\varepsilon > 0$ |
| Rubber band being compressed | Contraction | Compressive strain $\varepsilon < 0$ |
| Change in current flow | Electrical signal | Change in resistance $\Delta R$ |
| Ammeter deflection | Indication | Output voltage $V_o$ of Wheatstone bridge |

---

## 1.3 Working Principle - The Two Coupled Effects

The change in resistance of a strain gauge arises from **two simultaneous physical effects**:

1. **Geometric Effect (Dominant in Metallic Gauges):**
   The wire or foil element changes its length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ when strained. Since $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$, any change in geometry directly alters $R$.

2. **Piezoresistive Effect (Dominant in Semiconductor Gauges):**
   The resistivity $\rho$ of the material itself changes when subjected to mechanical stress. This effect is extremely pronounced in semiconductors like silicon and germanium, giving them much higher sensitivity than metals.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard resistance of a typical strain gauge is **$120\,\Omega$**, **$350\,\Omega$**, or **$1000\,\Omega$**, with $350\,\Omega$ being the most common in industrial applications (per ISO and Vishay Micro-Measurements standards).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic - Step-by-Step Working

- **Step 1 — Mechanical Coupling:**
  The strain gauge is firmly bonded to the test specimen using a special adhesive (e.g., cyanoacrylate or epoxy). This ensures the gauge elongates or contracts by exactly the same amount as the surface it is mounted on.

- **Step 2 — Strain Transfer:**
  When the host structure experiences a load $F$, it produces strain $\varepsilon$ on its surface. This strain is faithfully transferred to the active grid of the gauge.

- **Step 3 — Resistance Modulation:**
  The active grid (a serpentine pattern of fine wire or foil) experiences the strain, which causes its length $L$ to change by $\Delta L$, its cross-sectional area $A$ to change by $\Delta A$, and (in semiconductors) its resistivity $\rho$ to change by $\Delta \rho$. The net result is a fractional change in resistance $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$.

- **Step 4 — Signal Conditioning:**
  This small change in resistance ($\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ is typically of the order $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$) is converted into a usable voltage signal using a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit, often in conjunction with an **amplifier** and **filter**.

- **Step 5 — Display / Recording:**
  The amplified output voltage $V_o$ is calibrated against known strain values, allowing direct readout of strain, stress ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$), force, pressure, or any other derived mechanical quantity.

---

## 2.2 Why is a Wheatstone Bridge Used?

A single strain gauge produces a resistance change of the order of **fractions of an ohm**, which is impossible to measure directly with a voltmeter. Charles Wheatstone (1843) devised a null-deflection bridge that converts this tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable output voltage $V_o$. The bridge also provides inherent **temperature compensation** when multiple active gauges are used.

For a single active strain gauge $R_g$ in one arm of a balanced Wheatstone bridge, with the other three arms being fixed precision resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_g}{R_g + R_3} - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$$

At initial balance, all four resistances are equal to $R$, and $V_o = 0$. When the gauge resistance changes by $\Delta R$, the bridge becomes unbalanced and produces a non-zero output voltage proportional to strain.

---

## 2.3 Types of Strain Gauges (KTU High-Yield Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects you to be able to draw and describe at least these three types.**

### (a) Bonded Metallic Strain Gauges
- **Wire Type:** A fine resistance wire (Constantan, Nichrome, or Isoelastic alloy) of diameter $25\,\mu m$ to $50\,\mu m$ is arranged in a grid pattern and bonded to a thin carrier (paper or epoxy backing).
- **Foil Type:** A thin metallic foil (thickness $3\,\mu m$ to $5\,\mu m$) is photo-etched into a serpentine grid pattern. Foil gauges have better heat dissipation, higher fatigue life, and are the modern industry standard.

### (b) Unbonded Strain Gauges
The resistance wire is stretched between two insulated supports but is **not** bonded to the specimen. Strain is transmitted mechanically through a flexible frame. Used mainly in transducer elements (e.g., load cells, pressure sensors) for high-strain applications up to $\pm 5\%$.

### (c) Semiconductor Strain Gauges
Made from doped silicon or germanium strips bonded to a flexible backing. They exploit the strong piezoresistive effect, yielding Gauge Factors of **100 to 200** (compared to ~2.0 for metals). They are highly sensitive but suffer from temperature sensitivity and nonlinearity.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these equations verbatim. Every numerical problem in KTU is built from these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Standard Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | Fundamental resistance of a conductor | $R$ in $\Omega$, $\rho$ in $\Omega\cdot$m |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Definition of linear strain | Dimensionless (often $\mu\varepsilon$) |
| 3 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\Delta r / r}{\Delta L / L}$ | Poisson's ratio (lateral strain / axial strain) | Dimensionless; $\nu \approx 0.3$ for metals, $\approx 0.28$ for Constantan |
| 4 | $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | **Gauge Factor** (most important equation) | $G_F \approx 2.0$ for metallic, $100$ to $200$ for semiconductor |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | Linear sensitivity relation | Used in bridge output calculations |
| 6 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Output of quarter-active Wheatstone bridge | $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage (typically $5\,\text{V}$ or $10\,\text{V}$) |
| 7 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Output of half-active Wheatstone bridge | When 2 active gauges, adjacent arms |
| 8 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | Output of full-active Wheatstone bridge | Maximum sensitivity, all 4 arms active |
| 9 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's law (stress–strain relation) | $E$ = Young's modulus (e.g., $200\,\text{GPa}$ for steel) |
| 10 | $F = \sigma \cdot A_c$ | Axial force from stress | $A_c$ = cross-sectional area |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In numerical problems, students often confuse the bridge output formula for different configurations. **Always identify whether the bridge is quarter, half, or full active** before plugging values. Mixing up the factor $\dfrac{1}{4}$, $\dfrac{1}{2}$, or $1$ in front of $V_{ex}$ is the most common mark-losing mistake in KTU boards.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Strain gauges are the backbone of modern experimental stress analysis and form the sensing element in numerous industrial transducers:

- **Load Cells:** Used in weighing scales, platform balances, and force testers. Full-bridge configuration gives highest accuracy.
- **Pressure Transducers:** A diaphragm with bonded gauges converts pressure into strain.
- **Torque Meters on Rotating Shafts:** Gauges mounted at $45^{\circ}$ to the shaft axis measure torsional strain.
- **Structural Health Monitoring of Bridges, Aircraft, and Dams:** Provides real-time load and fatigue data.
- **Accelerometers and Vibration Sensors:** Inertial mass on a beam with strain gauges produces signal proportional to acceleration.
- **Biomedical Devices:** Micro-strain gauges in orthodontic braces, prosthetic implants, and respiration monitors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Gauge Factor from First Principles

**Statement:** Derive the expression for Gauge Factor of a strain gauge, starting from the fundamental resistance equation of a conductor.

**Given:**
- A strain gauge is essentially a long, thin electrical conductor of length $L$, uniform cross-sectional area $A$, and intrinsic resistivity $\rho$.
- The resistance of this conductor is $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$.

**To Derive:** The relationship $G_F = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$ in terms of Poisson's ratio $\nu$ and fractional change in resistivity $\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$.

---

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental resistance equation.**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides to convert multiplication into addition.**

This is a standard mathematical trick used in deriving sensitivity relations for transducers with multiplicative constitutive equations.

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate both sides with respect to the straining variable.**

Since strain $\varepsilon$ is the independent variable that drives all changes, we differentiate with respect to $\varepsilon$. For small incremental changes, the differentials $dR, d\rho, dL, dA$ may be replaced by finite increments $\Delta R, \Delta \rho, \Delta L, \Delta A$.

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the definition of linear strain $\varepsilon$.**

By definition, axial strain is the fractional change in length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{dL}{L}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dL}{L} = \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Relate the change in cross-sectional area to axial strain via Poisson's ratio.**

For a conductor with a circular cross-section of radius $r$, the area is $A = \pi r^2$. Differentiating:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \frac{dr}{r}$$

By definition, Poisson's ratio relates the lateral (radial) strain to the axial strain:

$$\nu = -\frac{dr / r}{dL / L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dr}{r} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

Substituting back:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot (-\nu \cdot \varepsilon) = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign in Poisson's ratio reflects the physical fact that when a wire is stretched axially ($\varepsilon > 0$), it contracts laterally ($dr < 0$), and vice versa.

**Step 6 — Substitute all fractional changes into the logarithmic derivative.**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon - (-2\nu \cdot \varepsilon)$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu) \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange into the standard Gauge Factor form.**

Divide both sides by $\varepsilon$:

$$\frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} + (1 + 2\nu)$$

Define Gauge Factor:

$$\boxed{\,G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}\,}$$

**Step 8 — Interpret the two terms.**

- The term $1 + 2\nu$ is the **geometric contribution** to the gauge factor. For most metals, $\nu \approx 0.3$, so this gives $\approx 1.6$.
- The term $\dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ is the **piezoresistive contribution**.
  - For metallic gauges (Constantan, Nichrome): piezoresistive effect is negligible ($\approx 0$), so $G_F \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1.6$ to $2.0$.
  - For semiconductor gauges (silicon): piezoresistive effect dominates, giving $G_F$ in the range of $100$ to $200$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Output Voltage of a Quarter-Active Wheatstone Bridge

**Given:** A Wheatstone bridge with four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$, where $R_4$ is the active strain gauge and $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ (initially balanced). Excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across the bridge.

**Step 1 — Write the bridge output voltage as the difference of the two divider voltages.**

$$V_o = V_B - V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} - V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $R_4 = R + \Delta R$ and $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$.**

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{R + R + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{R + R} \right]$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left[ \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Bring to a common denominator.**

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left[ \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \right]$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \left[ \frac{2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} \right]$$

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the small-signal approximation $\Delta R \ll R$.**

Since $\Delta R$ is of the order of milliohms while $R$ is hundreds of ohms, the term $\Delta R$ in the denominator is negligible:

$$2R + \Delta R \approx 2R$$

Thus:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of strain using the Gauge Factor relation $\Delta R = R \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$.**

$$\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **single most important working equation** in the strain gauge unit of any KTU 2024 paper. The output voltage is directly proportional to: (i) the excitation voltage, (ii) the gauge factor, and (iii) the strain.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bridge Configuration Comparison

| Configuration | Active Gauges | Output Voltage $V_o$ | Sensitivity Factor | Temperature Compensation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quarter Bridge** | 1 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | $\dfrac{1}{4}$ | Requires **dummy gauge** in adjacent arm |
| **Half Bridge** (adjacent arms) | 2 | $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ | Built-in, partial |
| **Half Bridge** (opposite arms) | 2 | $V_o = 0$ (no useful output) | $0$ | Useless configuration |
| **Full Bridge** | 4 | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | $1$ | Built-in, full |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Full-bridge configuration is always preferred in high-precision industrial applications** (e.g., commercial load cells) because it provides the maximum sensitivity (factor of 1 versus $\frac{1}{4}$), automatic temperature compensation, and cancellation of nonlinearity errors.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A strain gauge with nominal resistance $R = 350\,\Omega$ and gauge factor $G_F = 2.1$ is bonded to a steel structural member. The member is subjected to a stress $\sigma = 70\,\text{MPa}$. Young's modulus of steel is $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$. The gauge is connected in one arm of a quarter-active Wheatstone bridge with excitation $V_{ex} = 5\,\text{V}$. Find (a) the strain, (b) the fractional change in resistance, and (c) the bridge output voltage.

**Solution:**

**(a) Strain calculation** using Hooke's law $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{70 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} = 350\,\mu\varepsilon$$

**(b) Fractional change in resistance** using $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 2.1 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4} = 7.35 \times 10^{-4}$$

The absolute change in resistance:

$$\Delta R = R \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = 350 \times 7.35 \times 10^{-4} = 0.257\,\Omega$$

**(c) Bridge output voltage** for quarter-active bridge:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \times 7.35 \times 10^{-4} = 9.1875 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V} \approx 0.919\,\text{mV}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that even a $70\,\text{MPa}$ stress produces a bridge output of less than $1\,\text{mV}$. This is why KTU problems universally expect the output to be **amplified** by an instrumentation amplifier before display — a typical amplifier gain is 100 to 1000.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Strain Gauge Measurement System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mechanical Input Force F or Pressure P] --> B[Test Specimen Beam Shaft or Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Strain epsilon produced on surface]
    C --> D[Bonded Strain Gauge deforms identically]
    D --> E[Change in Resistance Delta R]
    E --> F[Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
    F --> G[Bridge Output Voltage Vo in mV]
    G --> H[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100 to 1000]
    H --> I[Filter and Signal Conditioning]
    I --> J[ADC and Microprocessor]
    J --> K[Digital Display or Recorder]
    F -.-> L[Excitation Voltage Source Vex 5V to 10V regulated]
    L --> F
    F -.-> M[Calibration and Temperature Compensation Network]
    M --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The mechanical input drives a chain of energy conversions: Force $\rightarrow$ Strain $\rightarrow$ Resistance change $\rightarrow$ Voltage change $\rightarrow$ Amplified and digitized signal. The excitation source and compensation network are auxiliary subsystems that feed the bridge.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Tree: Classification of Strain Gauges

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SG[Strain Gauges] --> BMG[Bonded Metallic Gauges]
    SG --> UBG[Unbonded Gauges]
    SG --> SCG[Semiconductor Gauges]
    BMG --> WT[Wire Type Grid Pattern Diameter 25 to 50 micrometer]
    BMG --> FT[Foil Type Photo etched serpentine Thickness 3 to 5 micrometer]
    UBG --> UBG1[Wire stretched between insulated supports]
    UBG --> UBG2[Used in high strain transducers up to 5 percent]
    SCG --> SCG1[Doped Silicon or Germanium]
    SCG --> SCG2[Piezoresistive effect dominant]
    SCG --> SCG3[Gauge Factor 100 to 200]
    WT --> WT1[Gauge Factor approximately 2.0]
    FT --> FT1[Gauge Factor approximately 2.0]
    FT --> FT2[Industry standard modern]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Wheatstone Bridge Configurations and Output Scaling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WB[Wheatstone Bridge Selection] --> Q1{How many active gauges?}
    Q1 -->|1 gauge| QB[Quarter Active Bridge]
    Q1 -->|2 gauges| Q2{Adjacent or opposite arms?}
    Q1 -->|4 gauges| FB[Full Active Bridge]
    Q2 -->|Adjacent| HB[Half Active Bridge Opposite Strain]
    Q2 -->|Opposite| NA[No Useful Output Reject Configuration]
    QB --> QB1[Output Vo equals Vex by 4 times Delta R by R]
    HB --> HB1[Output Vo equals Vex by 2 times Delta R by R]
    FB --> FB1[Output Vo equals Vex times Delta R by R]
    FB --> FB2[Maximum sensitivity factor 1]
    HB --> HB2[Sensitivity factor 0.5]
    QB --> QB3[Sensitivity factor 0.25]
    QB3 --> TC1[Requires dummy gauge for temperature compensation]
    HB2 --> TC2[Built in partial temperature compensation]
    FB2 --> TC3[Built in full temperature compensation]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Temperature Compensation Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TC[Temperature Drift Problem Apparent Strain] --> STC{Solution Strategy}
    STC --> S1[Self Temperature Compensated Gauge]
    STC --> S2[Dummy Gauge Method]
    STC --> S3[Half or Full Bridge with Adjacent Dummy]
    S1 --> S1D[Choose gauge alloy with zero TCR at operating temp]
    S2 --> S2D[Identical gauge mounted on unstrained piece in adjacent arm]
    S3 --> S3D[Drift cancels because both arms change by same Delta R due to T]
    S2D --> S2R[Bridge remains balanced under temperature change]
    S3D --> S3R[Most common method used in KTU lab experiments]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Note:** The dummy gauge method is the practical technique you will use in the Transducers and Measurements Laboratory. You mount a "dummy" strain gauge on an unstrained piece of the same material, and connect it in the adjacent arm of the Wheatstone bridge. Since both arms see the same temperature-induced $\Delta R$, the bridge output remains at zero when there is no mechanical strain — only true mechanical strain produces a non-zero $V_o$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define Gauge Factor. Explain its significance in strain gauge measurements.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Gauge Factor ($G_F$)** of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance to the applied mechanical strain:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

**Significance:**
- It is the **sensitivity** or **figure of merit** of the strain gauge. A higher $G_F$ means a larger output for the same strain.
- For metallic strain gauges (Constantan, Nichrome), $G_F \approx 2.0$, and the geometric effect ($1 + 2\nu$) dominates.
- For semiconductor strain gauges, $G_F = 100$ to $200$, and the piezoresistive effect dominates.
- Knowing $G_F$ allows the designer to compute the bridge output voltage: $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ (quarter bridge).
- It determines the choice of gauge for a given application — high-sensitivity semiconductor gauges for small strains, robust foil gauges for harsh industrial environments.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between bonded and unbonded strain gauges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Bonded Strain Gauge | Unbonded Strain Gauge |
|---|---|---|
| **Mounting** | Firmly cemented to the test specimen using adhesive | Wire is stretched between supports; not bonded to specimen |
| **Strain Range** | Low to medium (up to $0.3\%$ to $0.5\%$) | High (up to $\pm 5\%$) |
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate to high | Moderate |
| **Construction** | Wire or foil grid on paper/epoxy carrier | Tensioned wire on insulated frame |
| **Applications** | Stress analysis, load cells, pressure sensors | Force/pressure transducers, accelerometers |
| **Fatigue Life** | Limited by adhesive | Better for cyclic loading |
| **Hysteresis** | Present due to bonding | Lower hysteresis |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the Gauge Factor of a strain gauge starting from the fundamental resistance equation. Explain why semiconductor strain gauges have a much higher gauge factor than metallic ones.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the resistance equation** of a uniform conductor:

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Take natural logarithm** to convert to additive form:

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate** with respect to strain:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute axial strain** $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} = \varepsilon$ and lateral strain $\dfrac{\Delta A}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon$ (using Poisson's ratio for circular cross-section):

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange** by dividing by $\varepsilon$:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Why semiconductors have much higher $G_F$:**

For **metallic** gauges (Constantan, Nichrome, Isoelastic): the resistivity change $\Delta \rho$ under stress is very small, so $\dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} \approx 0$. The geometric term $1 + 2\nu$ alone gives $G_F \approx 1.6$ to $2.0$.

For **semiconductor** gauges (silicon, germanium): the piezoresistive term $\dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ is very large (typically 100 to 200 times the geometric term) because the band structure of semiconductors is highly sensitive to mechanical strain. Therefore, $G_F$ for semiconductor gauges is in the range of **100 to 200**, which is roughly **50 to 100 times higher** than metallic gauges.

---

**(b) A strain gauge of resistance $350\,\Omega$ and gauge factor $2.0$ is bonded to a steel cantilever beam. A quarter-active Wheatstone bridge is used with excitation voltage $10\,\text{V}$. When a load is applied, the bridge output is $2.5\,\text{mV}$. Determine (i) the strain, (ii) the change in resistance, and (iii) the stress in the beam if Young's modulus is $200\,\text{GPa}$.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R = 350\,\Omega$
- $G_F = 2.0$
- $V_{ex} = 10\,\text{V}$
- $V_o = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$
- $E = 200 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$
- Quarter-active bridge: $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$

**(i) Strain calculation:**

Using $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{4 \cdot V_o}{V_{ex} \cdot G_F} = \frac{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 2.0} = \frac{0.01}{20} = 5 \times 10^{-4} = 500\,\mu\varepsilon$$

**(ii) Change in resistance:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon = 2.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta R = R \times 10^{-3} = 350 \times 10^{-3} = 0.35\,\Omega$$

**(iii) Stress calculation** using Hooke's law $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\sigma = 200 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 100 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa} = 100\,\text{MPa}$$

**Summary of results:**
- Strain: $\varepsilon = 500\,\mu\varepsilon$
- $\Delta R = 0.35\,\Omega$
- Stress: $\sigma = 100\,\text{MPa}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of a Wheatstone bridge with an active strain gauge in one arm. Derive the expression for its output voltage.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (Mermaid rendered):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excitation Source Vex] --> B[Node A Top Left]
    A --> C[Node C Top Right]
    B --> D[Node B Bottom Left]
    C --> E[Node D Bottom Right]
    D --> F[Arm R1]
    D --> G[Arm R3 with strain gauge]
    E --> H[Arm R2]
    E --> I[Arm R4 fixed]
    F --> H
    G --> I
    H --> J[Output Vo between B and D]
    I --> J
```

The bridge consists of four resistive arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arranged in a diamond configuration. An excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across two opposite nodes, and the output voltage $V_o$ is measured across the other two nodes.

**Working Principle:**
- When the bridge is **balanced** ($R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$), $V_o = 0$.
- When the active strain gauge $R_3$ experiences strain, its resistance changes by $\Delta R$, unbalancing the bridge and producing a non-zero $V_o$.

**Derivation of Output Voltage:**

The voltage at node B (divider across $R_3$ and $R_4$):

$$V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3 + \Delta R}{R_3 + \Delta R + R_4}$$

The voltage at node D (divider across $R_1$ and $R_2$):

$$V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Output voltage:

$$V_o = V_B - V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \left[ \frac{R_3 + \Delta R}{R_3 + \Delta R + R_4} - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right]$$

Setting $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ (initially balanced) and applying $\Delta R \ll R$:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

In terms of strain: $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$

---

**(b) Compare quarter, half, and full-active Wheatstone bridge configurations with respect to (i) number of active gauges, (ii) output voltage expression, (iii) sensitivity, and (iv) temperature compensation. A load cell uses a full bridge with $R = 350\,\Omega$ and $G_F = 2.1$. Find the output voltage for a strain of $1000\,\mu\varepsilon$ with $V_{ex} = 10\,\text{V}$.** **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Quarter Bridge | Half Bridge | Full Bridge |
|---|---|---|---|
| Active Gauges | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Output $V_o$ | $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | $V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ |
| Sensitivity Factor | $0.25$ | $0.50$ | $1.00$ |
| Temperature Compensation | Needs dummy gauge | Built-in (partial) | Built-in (complete) |
| Nonlinearity | Present | Reduced | Cancelled |

**Numerical Calculation:**

For full bridge: $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$

Given: $V_{ex} = 10\,\text{V}$, $G_F = 2.1$, $\varepsilon = 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}$

$$V_o = 10 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3} = 21 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 21\,\text{mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** Compare this $21\,\text{mV}$ output for full bridge with the $0.525\,\text{mV}$ you would get for a quarter bridge under identical conditions — a 40× improvement! This is precisely why commercial load cells universally use full-bridge configurations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the geometric term $1 + 2\nu$ in the gauge factor derivation** — KTU expects the full 3-term expression. Just stating $G_F = 2.0$ without derivation costs you 4 out of 7 marks in Part (a).
> 2. **Wrong Wheatstone bridge factor** — Using $\frac{1}{4}$ for a full bridge is the #1 error. Always check how many active gauges are present and where they are placed (adjacent vs. opposite arms).
> 3. **Unit errors in strain** — Strain is dimensionless but is conventionally expressed in $\mu\varepsilon$. Converting $500\,\mu\varepsilon$ to $500$ (forgetting the $10^{-6}$) will give absurd answers and lose full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply $\Delta R \ll R$ approximation** — This is what makes the linear formula $V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$ valid. If the problem gives a large strain ($> 1\%$), the approximation breaks down and you must use the full nonlinear expression.
> 5. **Mixing up $V_o$ and $V_{ex}$** — $V_{ex}$ is the DC excitation across the bridge; $V_o$ is the tiny differential output. They differ by 3 to 4 orders of magnitude.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strain Gauge**

- **Strain Definition:** $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ (dimensionless, often in $\mu\varepsilon$).
- **Poisson's Ratio:** $\nu = -\dfrac{\text{lateral strain}}{\text{axial strain}}$; for metals $\nu \approx 0.3$, for Constantan $\nu \approx 0.28$.
- **Gauge Factor Formula:** $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ — the **most important equation** of this module.
- **Metallic Gauge Factor:** $G_F \approx 2.0$ (geometric effect only).
- **Semiconductor Gauge Factor:** $G_F = 100$ to $200$ (piezoresistive effect dominant).
- **Sensitivity Relation:** $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$.
- **Standard Gauge Resistances:** $120\,\Omega$, $350\,\Omega$, $1000\,\Omega$ (350 most common).
- **Quarter Bridge Output:** $V_o = (V_{ex} / 4) \cdot (\Delta R / R) = (V_{ex} / 4) \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$.
- **Half Bridge Output:** $V_o = (V_{ex} / 2) \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ (active gauges in adjacent arms, opposite strain).
- **Full Bridge Output:** $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ — maximum sensitivity, used in load cells.
- **Types of Strain Gauges:** Bonded (wire, foil), Unbonded, Semiconductor.
- **Temperature Compensation:** Use dummy gauge in adjacent arm OR choose self-temperature-compensated gauge OR use full-bridge configuration.
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ — converts measured strain to stress.
- **Piezoresistive Effect:** Change in resistivity $\rho$ under applied stress; small in metals, dominant in semiconductors.
- **Bonded vs. Unbonded:** Bonded (firmly cemented, used for static/dynamic strain on real structures); Unbonded (free wire on frame, used in transducer elements).
- **Foil vs. Wire:** Foil is photo-etched from a thin metallic sheet; better heat dissipation, higher fatigue life, industry standard.
- **Typical Strain Range:** $10\,\mu\varepsilon$ to $2000\,\mu\varepsilon$ in practical engineering measurements.
- **Typical Output:** Order of millivolts — always requires an instrumentation amplifier.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermistor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermistor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermistor (Thermal Resistor):** A thermistor is a **two-terminal, solid-state, temperature-sensitive semiconductor transducer** whose electrical resistance varies predictably and significantly with changes in its body temperature. It belongs to the class of **passive resistive temperature transducers** and is fabricated from sintered metallic oxides (e.g., oxides of manganese, nickel, cobalt, iron, or copper) doped to achieve a highly temperature-dependent resistivity.

The two principal classes recognized by KTU 2024 scheme are:

- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) Thermistor:** Resistance *decreases* as temperature rises. The most common type, used for precision measurement, control, and compensation.
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) Thermistor:** Resistance *increases* as temperature rises. Used in overcurrent protection, motor starting, and self-regulating heater circuits.

A special sub-class is the **CTR (Critical Temperature Resistor)** which exhibits an extremely sharp resistance change at a specific critical (Curie-like) temperature, used in fire-alarm and thermal protection circuits.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Faucet"

Imagine a garden hose where the **nozzle opening automatically shrinks when the water gets too hot and expands when the water cools down**:

- In an **NTC thermistor**, the "nozzle" is the conductive path inside the semiconductor. As temperature **rises**, more charge carriers (electrons/holes) are thermally excited across the semiconductor's bandgap, *widening* the conductive channel — so resistance **falls** and current flows more easily.
- In a **PTC thermistor**, the crystalline grain boundaries within a doped barium-titanate ceramic become *resistive* at higher temperatures, *choking* the flow.

So a thermistor is essentially a **temperature-to-resistance converter** — by simply measuring its resistance, we indirectly measure the temperature of the medium in thermal contact with it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from RTD:** An RTD uses a *pure metal* (Pt, Ni, Cu) with a small, *linear* positive coefficient. A thermistor uses a *ceramic semiconductor* with a large (typically **−3 % to −6 % per °C**), highly *non-linear* negative coefficient. This is why thermistors offer **10×–100× higher sensitivity** than RTDs, but over a narrower range (typically **−55 °C to +150 °C** for NTC).

## 1.3 Standardized Physical & Electrical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Operating range (NTC bead) | **−55 °C to +150 °C** | $T_{op}$ |
| Operating range (NTC disc) | **−40 °C to +300 °C** | $T_{op}$ |
| B-constant (material) | **3000 K – 5000 K** | $B$ |
| Nominal resistance (at 25 °C) | **1 kΩ, 10 kΩ, 100 kΩ** | $R_{25}$ or $R_0$ |
| Temperature coefficient (α) | **−3 % to −6 % per °C** | $\alpha_T$ |
| Dissipation constant | **1 mW/°C – 10 mW/°C** | $\delta$ |
| Thermal time constant | **1 s – 60 s** | $\tau$ |
| Curie temperature (PTC) | **60 °C – 180 °C** | $T_c$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Physics — Why Does Resistance Change?

The conduction in a thermistor is governed by **semiconductor physics**, not metallic conduction. The conductivity $\sigma$ of an intrinsic semiconductor follows the Arrhenius-type exponential law:

$$\sigma = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(\frac{-E_g}{2kT}\right)$$

where $E_g$ is the bandgap energy (~0.1–1 eV for metal oxides) and $k$ is Boltzmann's constant. Since $R \propto 1/\sigma$:

$$R \propto \exp\left(\frac{E_g}{2kT}\right)$$

This is the **fundamental origin** of the thermistor's exponential response.

## 2.2 The B-Parameter Equation (Standard Model)

The most widely used engineering model expresses resistance as a function of absolute temperature:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical B-Parameter Equation:**
> $$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$
> where $R_T$ = resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (K), $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (K, usually **298.15 K = 25 °C**), and $B$ is the **material-specific beta constant** in Kelvin.

**Logical Derivation of Use-Cases:**

1. **To find resistance at a new temperature** → solve $R_T$ directly using the $B$-equation.
2. **To find temperature from a measured resistance** → invert logarithmically:
$$T = \left[\frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{B}\ln\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right)\right]^{-1}$$
3. **To identify $B$ experimentally** → measure $R_1$ at $T_1$ and $R_2$ at $T_2$:
$$B = \frac{\ln(R_1/R_2)}{(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)}$$

## 2.3 Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (α)

Differentiating $R_T$ with respect to $T$:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{B}{T^2} \quad \text{(per °C or per K)}$$

This is **negative** for NTC and is **inversely proportional to $T^2$** — meaning the thermistor is *more sensitive at low temperatures* and *less sensitive at high temperatures*. This non-linearity is the chief engineering trade-off.

## 2.4 Steinhart–Hart Equation (High-Accuracy Model)

For precision applications (better than ±0.1 °C over a wide range), the cubic Steinhart–Hart model is used:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steinhart–Hart Equation:**
> $$\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R_T) + C \left[\ln(R_T)\right]^3$$
> where $A$, $B$, $C$ are device-specific calibration constants, typically supplied by the manufacturer as a 3-point or 4-point fit over the operating range.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_T = R_0 \exp\left[B\left(\tfrac{1}{T} - \tfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Standard NTC resistance vs. temperature |
| 2 | $T = \left[\tfrac{1}{T_0} + \tfrac{1}{B}\ln(R_T/R_0)\right]^{-1}$ | Inversion — find $T$ from $R_T$ |
| 3 | $B = \ln(R_1/R_2) \div (1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ | Experimental $B$ determination |
| 4 | $\alpha_T = -B/T^2$ | Temperature coefficient (% per K) |
| 5 | $\frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln R + C (\ln R)^3$ | Steinhart–Hart (precision) |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{rise} = P \cdot \delta^{-1} \cdot t$ | Self-heating temperature rise (transient) |
| 7 | $P = I^2 R_T = V^2 / R_T$ | Power dissipated in thermistor |
| 8 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_s}{R_s + R_T}$ | Voltage-divider output |
| 9 | $\tau = \rho V c / \delta$ | Thermal time constant (response speed) |
| 10 | $f(x) = a x + b x^2 + c x^3$ | Polynomial linearization approximation |

> **Symbols Glossary:** $R_T$ = resistance at temp $T$; $R_0$ = nominal resistance at $T_0 = 25\,°C = 298.15\,K$; $B$ = beta constant (K); $\alpha_T$ = tempco (K⁻¹); $\delta$ = dissipation constant (mW/°C); $\tau$ = thermal time constant (s); $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat, $V$ = volume.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical:** Digital thermometers, patient monitoring probes.
- **Automotive:** Engine coolant, intake-air, oil-temperature sensing.
- **Industrial:** HVAC duct sensors, 3D-printer hot-end thermistors (e.g., **NTC 100 kΩ B = 3950**).
- **Consumer Electronics:** Battery-pack protection (NTC on Li-ion cells), inrush current limiters (NTC at power-on).
- **Appliance:** Rice cookers, water heaters, refrigerator defrost control (PTC).
- **Compensation:** Used in *series* with copper coils to compensate for copper's positive temperature drift.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — B-Parameter Equation from Arrhenius Conduction

**Step 1.** Start with intrinsic semiconductor conductivity:
$$\sigma = \sigma_\infty \cdot \exp\left(\frac{-E_a}{2kT}\right)$$

where $E_a$ is the activation energy of the dopant/metal-oxide, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

**Step 2.** Resistivity is the reciprocal of conductivity:
$$\rho = \frac{1}{\sigma} = \frac{1}{\sigma_\infty} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{2kT}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Resistance of a uniform specimen of length $\ell$ and cross-section $A$ is:
$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot \ell}{A} = \frac{\ell}{A \sigma_\infty} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E_a}{2kT}\right)$$

**Step 4.** Define a device constant $K = \ell/(A \sigma_\infty)$ and a beta constant $B = E_a/(2k)$:
$$R_T = K \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{T}\right)$$

**Step 5.** To anchor to a known reference point $(T_0, R_0)$:
$$R_0 = K \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{T_0}\right) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K = R_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 6.** Substitute $K$ back into Step 4:
$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{B}{T_0}\right) \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{T}\right) = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

This is the **canonical B-equation**, valid for NTC thermistors. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Coefficient $\alpha_T$

**Step 1.** Write $R_T$ in differential form. Take the natural log:
$$\ln R_T = \ln R_0 + B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:
$$\frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = B \cdot \frac{d}{dT}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = B \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{T^2}\right) = -\frac{B}{T^2}$$

**Step 3.** By definition of the temperature coefficient of resistance:
$$\alpha_T = \frac{1}{R_T} \cdot \frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{B}{T^2}$$

**Numerical Insight:** For $B = 3950\,\text{K}$ and $T = 298.15\,\text{K}$ (25 °C):
$$\alpha_T = -\frac{3950}{(298.15)^2} = -\frac{3950}{88{,}893} \approx -0.0444\;\text{K}^{-1} = -4.44\,\%\,\text{per °C}$$

This confirms the **large negative sensitivity** characteristic of NTC thermistors at room temperature. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Self-Heating Temperature Rise

**Step 1.** Electrical power dissipated in the thermistor body:
$$P = I^2 R_T$$

**Step 2.** Heat conducted away to the surroundings is governed by the dissipation constant $\delta$ (mW/°C) — the power required to raise the thermistor's body temperature by 1 °C *above ambient* in steady state:
$$P = \delta \cdot (T_{body} - T_{amb})$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the steady-state self-heating rise:
$$\Delta T_{sh} = T_{body} - T_{amb} = \frac{P}{\delta} = \frac{V^2}{\delta \cdot R_T}$$

**Step 4.** For transient heating (thermal capacity $C_{th} = m c$):
$$T_{body}(t) - T_{amb} = \frac{P}{\delta}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{C_{th}}{\delta}$$

**Engineering Implication:** To keep self-heating below 0.1 °C, the sensor current should be limited such that $P \le 0.1 \cdot \delta$. This dictates the choice of excitation voltage in a Wheatstone-bridge design. ∎

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An NTC thermistor has $R_{25} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $B = 3950\,\text{K}$. Find its resistance at $T = 100\,°C = 373.15\,\text{K}$, and the temperature coefficient at 25 °C and at 100 °C.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Resistance at 100 °C:**

Using the B-equation with $T_0 = 298.15\,\text{K}$:
$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{373.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}$$

$$= 0.0026798 - 0.0033540 = -0.0006742\;\text{K}^{-1}$$

Exponent argument: $B \cdot \Delta = 3950 \times (-0.0006742) = -2.6632$

$$R_{100} = 10{,}000 \times e^{-2.6632} = 10{,}000 \times 0.0698 = 698\,\Omega$$

**Part (b) — Temperature coefficient at 25 °C:**
$$\alpha_{25} = -\frac{3950}{(298.15)^2} = -0.0444\;\text{K}^{-1} \approx -4.44\,\%/\text{K}$$

**Part (c) — Temperature coefficient at 100 °C:**
$$\alpha_{100} = -\frac{3950}{(373.15)^2} = -\frac{3950}{139{,}241} = -0.0284\;\text{K}^{-1} \approx -2.84\,\%/\text{K}$$

> **Observation:** The sensitivity at 25 °C (~−4.4 %/K) is **1.56× higher** than at 100 °C (~−2.8 %/K), confirming the $1/T^2$ fall-off. This is why thermistors are preferred for *low-to-mid* temperature measurements rather than high temperatures. ∎

## 3.5 Voltage Divider Output (Line-of-Thought)

For a thermistor in a simple voltage divider with fixed series resistor $R_s$:

**Step 1.** The output voltage across the fixed resistor $R_s$ (tapped across the thermistor–source junction) is:
$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_T}$$

**Step 2.** As $T$ rises, $R_T$ (NTC) **decreases**, so the share of $V_{in}$ across $R_s$ **increases** → $V_{out}$ rises monotonically with $T$.

**Step 3.** The non-linearity of $V_{out}(T)$ is *less* severe than $R_T(T)$ because the divider partially linearizes the response — a useful KTU exam observation.

**Step 4.** Optimal linearity is obtained when $R_s \approx R_T$ at the **midpoint** of the operating temperature range.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thermistor Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["THERMAL DOMAIN"]:::hot --> nodeB["Medium Temp T_inf"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Thermistor Body"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Self Heating P_eq"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Body Temp T_b"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["ELECTRICAL DOMAIN"]:::cool
    nodeF --> nodeG["R_T Output"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Wheatstone Bridge / Voltage Divider"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Signal Conditioner Amplifier"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["ADC / Display / Controller"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Final Reading in Degree C"]

    classDef cool fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2471a3,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Temperature T Rising"]:::heat --> nodeB["Charge carrier density n increases"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Conductivity sigma rises"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Resistivity rho falls"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["R_T falls exponentially"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Voltage divider V_out rises"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["ADC reads higher count"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Display shows higher degree C"]

    classDef heat fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#e67e22,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thermistor Interfacing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        i1["Thermistor NTC R_T"]
        i2["Reference Resistor R_s fixed"]
    end

    subgraph BRIDGE["Bridge / Divider"]
        b1["Wheatstone Bridge or Voltage Divider"]
    end

    subgraph COND["Signal Conditioning"]

    end

    i2 --> b1

```

## 4.4 Mermaid Chart — PTC Switching Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Curie Temperature T_c NOT reached"]:::cold --> nodeB["Low resistance state"]
    nodeA2["T exceeds T_c"]:::hot --> nodeC["Sharp resistance increase 10^3 to 10^6 times"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Normal conduction"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Overcurrent protection activated"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Load operates normally"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Circuit opens protects load"]

    classDef cold fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
    classDef hot fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#cb4337,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Reference Circuit — Wheatstone-Bridge Thermistor Readout

```
              V_ex (5 V)
                │
        ┌───────┴───────┐
        │               │
       R_s1            R_T (NTC)
        │               │
        ├───── V_out ───┤
        │               │
       R_s2            R_s3
        │               │
        └───────┬───────┘
                │
              GND (0 V)

   V_out = V_ex × [ R_s2/(R_s1+R_s2)  −  R_s3/(R_T + R_s3) ]
   Balance (V_out = 0) when R_T = R_s1·R_s3 / R_s2
```
