<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a thermistor. Distinguish between NTC and PTC types with one example application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A thermistor is a temperature-sensitive semiconductor resistor whose resistance varies with temperature in a well-defined manner. **(1 mark)**

- **NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *decreases* with rising temperature; used in digital thermometers, inrush current limiters. **(1 mark)**
- **PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient):** Resistance *increases* sharply beyond a Curie temperature; used in overcurrent protection and motor-start circuits. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the B-constant of a thermistor? Explain its significance with the relevant equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The B-constant is a material-specific parameter (in Kelvin) that characterizes the slope of $\ln R$ versus $1/T$ for an NTC thermistor. **(1 mark)** It is defined by the canonical equation:
$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$
**(1 mark)** A higher B indicates greater temperature sensitivity; it is experimentally determined from two known (R, T) pairs as $B = \ln(R_1/R_2) / (1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the B-parameter equation $R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[B(1/T - 1/T_0)\right]$ for an NTC thermistor starting from the Arrhenius conduction model. State clearly the meaning of each symbol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermistor has $R_{25} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $B = 3500\,\text{K}$. Find (i) its resistance at 80 °C, (ii) the temperature coefficient at 25 °C and at 80 °C, and (iii) the temperature at which its resistance drops to 1 kΩ. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** State the Arrhenius-type conductivity of an intrinsic semiconductor:
$$\sigma = \sigma_\infty \exp(-E_a/2kT)$$

**Step 2 [1 mark]:** Write $R = \ell / (\sigma A)$:
$$R = \frac{\ell}{A \sigma_\infty} \exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{2kT}\right)$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** Define $B \equiv E_a/2k$ and $K \equiv \ell/(A\sigma_\infty)$:
$$R_T = K \exp(B/T)$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Anchor at $(T_0, R_0)$: $K = R_0 \exp(-B/T_0)$.

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Substitute and obtain the canonical form:
$$R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 6 [1 mark]:** Define symbols — $R_T$: resistance at absolute temperature $T$; $R_0$: resistance at reference $T_0$ (298.15 K); $B$: beta constant (K).

**Step 7 [1 mark]:** State that the negative temperature coefficient arises because the exponent $(1/T - 1/T_0)$ becomes negative as $T > T_0$, forcing $R_T < R_0$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Resistance at 80 °C (353.15 K) [2 marks]:**
$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{353.15} - \frac{1}{298.15} = 0.0028316 - 0.0033540 = -0.0005224\,\text{K}^{-1}$$
$$B\Delta = 3500 \times (-0.0005224) = -1.8285$$
$$R_{80} = 5000 \times e^{-1.8285} = 5000 \times 0.1606 = 803\,\Omega \;\; \textbf{[1 U] (KTU: state answer to 3 s.f. — 803 Ω)} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(ii) Temperature coefficient [3 marks]:**

At 25 °C:
$$\alpha_{25} = -\frac{3500}{(298.15)^2} = -\frac{3500}{88{,}893} = -0.03938\;\text{K}^{-1} \approx \mathbf{-3.94\,\%/K} \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

At 80 °C:
$$\alpha_{80} = -\frac{3500}{(353.15)^2} = -\frac{3500}{124{,}715} = -0.02806\;\text{K}^{-1} \approx \mathbf{-2.81\,\%/K} \quad \text{[1.5 marks]}$$

**(iii) Temperature at $R_T = 1000\,\Omega$ [2 marks]:**

Using the inverted B-equation:
$$T = \left[\frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{B}\ln\!\left(\frac{R_T}{R_0}\right)\right]^{-1}$$
$$= \left[\frac{1}{298.15} + \frac{1}{3500}\ln\!\left(\frac{1000}{5000}\right)\right]^{-1}$$
$$= \left[0.0033540 + \frac{\ln(0.2)}{3500}\right]^{-1}$$
$$= \left[0.0033540 + \frac{-1.6094}{3500}\right]^{-1}$$
$$= \left[0.0033540 - 0.0004598\right]^{-1} = \left[0.0028942\right]^{-1} = 345.5\,\text{K} = \mathbf{72.3\,°C} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of an NTC thermistor. Discuss the temperature coefficient of resistance and the concept of self-heating error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NTC thermistor of $R_{25} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $B = 3950\,\text{K}$, dissipation constant $\delta = 5\,\text{mW/°C}$ is used with an excitation of 3.3 V DC in a voltage divider. Compute (i) the voltage across the thermistor at 25 °C, (ii) the steady-state self-heating temperature rise, and (iii) the corrected resistance and the error in indicated temperature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Principle [2 marks]:** An NTC thermistor is a sintered-oxide semiconductor whose resistance decreases exponentially with rising temperature. Charge carriers are thermally generated across the small bandgap of the metal-oxide lattice; as $T$ rises, carrier concentration rises exponentially, lowering resistance.

**Sketch (textual) [2 marks]:** A Wheatstone-bridge circuit with the thermistor in one arm, an instrumentation amplifier, ADC, and a digital display, with self-heating power $P = I^2 R$ dissipated within the bead.

**Temperature coefficient [2 marks]:**
$$\alpha_T = -\frac{B}{T^2}$$

It is negative (NTC), large in magnitude (typically −3 to −6 %/K), and inversely proportional to $T^2$.

**Self-heating error [1 mark]:** The current flowing through the thermistor dissipates Joule heat $P = I^2 R$ which raises the body temperature above ambient, biasing the reading. It is minimized by keeping $P \le 0.1 \cdot \delta$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Voltage across the thermistor (no-load reference) [2 marks]:**

Assuming a simple series circuit (or divider with $R_s = R_{25} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ for maximum linearity at 25 °C):
$$V_{th} = 3.3 \times \frac{R_T}{R_s + R_T} = 3.3 \times \frac{10{,}000}{20{,}000} = \mathbf{1.65\,V} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(ii) Self-heating rise [2 marks]:**
$$P = \frac{V_{th}^2}{R_T} = \frac{(1.65)^2}{10{,}000} = 2.7225 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W} = 0.272\,\text{mW}$$
$$\Delta T_{sh} = \frac{P}{\delta} = \frac{0.272}{5} = \mathbf{0.0545\,°C} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Corrected resistance and error [3 marks]:**

The body temperature is now $T_{body} = 25 + 0.0545 = 25.0545\,°C = 298.2045\,\text{K}$.

Corrected resistance:
$$R_{corrected} = 10{,}000 \times \exp\!\left[3950\left(\frac{1}{298.2045} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)\right]$$
$$= 10{,}000 \times \exp\!\left[3950 \times (0.0033534 - 0.0033540)\right]$$
$$= 10{,}000 \times \exp(3950 \times -6.04 \times 10^{-7})$$
$$= 10{,}000 \times \exp(-0.002386) = 10{,}000 \times 0.99762 = 9976.2\,\Omega$$

The *error* in indicated temperature (assuming the system uses uncalibrated self-heating):
$$\Delta T_{error} = \mathbf{0.0545\,°C}\;\; \text{(equivalent to 0.055 °C bias)} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

The change in resistance due to self-heating: $R_{25} - R_{corrected} = 10{,}000 - 9{,}976 = 23.8\,\Omega$ **(0.24 % reduction)**. **[2 marks for the corrected resistance calculation; 1 mark for stating the error].**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** in the B-equation — this *alone* causes a 100+ K error and zero marks on the relevant step.
> 2. **Using $R = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$** (the metallic-RTD formula) for a thermistor — a *classic* KTU trap. The thermistor equation is **exponential**, not linear.
> 3. **Mixing up the sign of $\alpha$** — NTC has a *negative* $\alpha$ but the *magnitude* is what matters in sensitivity discussions.
> 4. **Ignoring self-heating** in low-$\delta$ / high-current applications — examiners often award a dedicated 1–2 marks for self-heating analysis.
> 5. **Inverting the B-equation incorrectly** — remember the units in $\ln(R_T/R_0)$ are dimensionless; $1/B$ must be in K⁻¹, with $1/T$ in K⁻¹.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thermistor = Thermal Resistor** — a 2-terminal, solid-state, temperature-sensitive semiconductor.
- **NTC** = resistance falls as $T$ rises; **PTC** = resistance rises; **CTR** = sharp transition at Curie point.
- **Canonical equation:** $R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[B\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ — always use **Kelvin** for $T$.
- **B-constant** is device-specific (typically **3000 K – 5000 K**) and is found by a 2-point calibration.
- **Temperature coefficient:** $\alpha_T = -B/T^2$ — large, negative, and inversely proportional to $T^2$.
- **Steinhart–Hart** equation (cubic in $\ln R$) is used for high-precision applications.
- **Self-heating error:** $\Delta T_{sh} = P/\delta$; controlled by limiting $P \le 0.1 \cdot \delta$.
- **Comparison with RTD:** Thermistor — high sensitivity (−3 to −6 %/K), non-linear, narrow range, cheap. RTD — low sensitivity (~0.4 %/K), linear, wide range, expensive.
- **Comparison with Thermocouple:** Thermistor — best for −55 °C to +150 °C with high accuracy; Thermocouple — best for high temperatures (>500 °C) and harsh environments.
- **Common nominal resistances:** 1 kΩ, 5 kΩ, 10 kΩ, 50 kΩ, 100 kΩ — all at 25 °C reference.
- **Standard excitation:** Low-voltage DC (1.25 V – 5 V) with current-limit to minimize self-heating.
- **Linearization techniques:** (1) Series/parallel resistor network, (2) Look-up table in MCU, (3) Piecewise linearization, (4) Software curve fit using $\alpha_T$ correction.
- **KTU-favorite numerical setup:** Given $R_0$, $B$, find $R_T$ at another $T$, and $\alpha$ — practice inverting the B-equation repeatedly.
- **Time constant $\tau$** = thermal mass / dissipation constant — quantifies the response speed of the bead.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Transducer: LVDT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Inductive Transducer: LVDT # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts **linear mechanical displacement** into a corresponding **electrical (AC voltage) signal**. It operates on the principle of **mutual inductance variation** between a primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings, caused by the axial movement of a movable soft-iron armature (core) inside a hollow cylindrical former. It belongs to the family of **variable reluctance / mutual inductance transducers** and is the most widely used transducer for measuring small linear displacements (typically from a few micrometers up to several centimeters) in industrial and laboratory environments. ### 1.2 Constructional Anatomy (Physical Make-up) An LVDT consists of **four** essential parts: | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Primary Winding (P)** | Energized by an AC excitation source; magnetizes the core. | | **Secondary Windings (S1 & S2)** | Two identical coils wound on either side of the primary; connected in **series opposition** (anti-series). | | **Soft-Iron Core (Armature)** | A cylindrical slug that moves linearly; it acts as a low-reluctance path for the magnetic flux. | | **Hollow Former (Bobbin)** | A non-magnetic cylindrical support on which the windings are placed. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Balancing Scale" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuition:** Imagine a **two-pan balance scale**. The two pans are the secondary coils (S1 and S2). The weight (magnetic flux) is distributed by the **movable fulcrum (iron core)**. > - If the fulcrum is **exactly at the center** (null position) → both pans carry equal weight → **net output = 0**. > - If the fulcrum slides **left** → the left pan dips (more flux in S1) → net signal has a **certain phase**. > - If the fulcrum slides **right** → the right pan dips (more flux in S2) → net signal has the **opposite phase**. > > The *direction* of the tilt is revealed by the *phase* of the output voltage, and the *amount* of tilt is revealed by the *magnitude* of the output voltage. This is the heart of the **null-balance principle** used in LVDTs. ## 1.4 Standard Operating Metrics - **Excitation Frequency:** **1 V to 24 V RMS**, at **50 Hz to 20 kHz** (typical **2.5 kHz to 10 kHz**). - **Linearity:** Better than **±0.25 %** of full scale. - **Sensitivity:** Typically **0.5 V/mm to 30 mV/μm** (depends on excitation). - **Resolution:** Practically **infinite** (limited only by the readability of the output meter), since there is **no friction or contact**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. An **AC excitation voltage** $E_p$ is applied to the primary winding P. 2. This produces a **time-varying magnetic flux** $\phi_p$ in the core region. 3. The flux links with the two secondary windings S1 and S2 through the movable iron core. 4. According to **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, EMFs are induced in S1 and S2: $$e_{s1} = -N_{s1}\frac{d\phi_{s1}}{dt} \quad \text{and} \quad e_{s2} = -N_{s2}\frac{d\phi_{s2}}{dt}$$ 5. The secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so the **differential output voltage** is: $$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$$ 6. The position of the iron core determines the magnitude and phase of $e_o$. > [!NOTE] > **Why series opposition?** Because the two secondary EMFs are physically wound in opposite senses. This anti-series connection makes the *null position* give a *zero output*, which is the foundational principle of any **differential transformer**. ## 2.2 The Three Operating Zones | Core Position | Magnetic Flux Linkage | $E_{s1}$ vs $E_{s2}$ | Differential Output $E_o$ | |---|---|---|---| | **At null (center)** | Equal flux in S1 and S2 | $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ | $E_o = 0$ | | **Displaced upward by x** | More flux links S1 | $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ | $E_o > 0$ (in phase with $E_p$) | | **Displaced downward by x** | More flux links S2 | $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ | $E_o > 0$ (180° out of phase with $E_p$) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **must-memorize** summary for KTU board exams. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to keep markdown table syntax intact. | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{s1} = E_o + Kx$ | EMF in secondary 1 (core displaced +x) | | 2 | $E_{s2} = E_o - Kx$ | EMF in secondary 2 (core displaced +x) | | 3 | $E_o^{diff} = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 2Kx$ | **Differential output voltage** (linear in x) | | 4 | $K = \dfrac{dE_o^{diff}}{dx}$ | **Sensitivity** of the LVDT (V/mm) | | 5 | $E_{s}^{avg} = \dfrac{E_{s1} + E_{s2}}{2} = E_o$ | **Sum output** (used for temperature compensation) | | 6 | $e_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_m$ | Primary induced EMF (transformer EMF eqn.) | | 7 | $E_{s} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{s} \, \phi_m$ | Secondary induced EMF | | 8 | $\dfrac{N_{s1}}{N_p} = \dfrac{N_{s2}}{N_p} = \text{turns ratio (n)}$ | Turns ratio for matched secondaries | Where: - $E_o$ = Null-position (residual) EMF, ideally **0** - $K$ = Sensitivity constant (V per unit displacement) - $x$ = Linear displacement of the core from null - $f$ = Excitation frequency in **Hz** - $N_p, N_{s1}, N_{s2}$ = Number of turns in primary, secondary 1, secondary 2 - $\phi_m$ = Maximum magnetic flux in **Weber (Wb)** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aircraft flight-control surface displacement** (high reliability, no friction). - **Hydraulic cylinder piston position** feedback in heavy machinery. - **Profiling of surface roughness** in precision metrology. - **Force and load cells** (when coupled with elastic elements). - **Nuclear reactor rod position** measurement (radiation-tolerant design). - **Robotic arm joint feedback** and CNC machine tool positioning. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Output Voltage Equation ### Assumptions - The two secondary windings S1 and S2 are **identical** (same number of turns, same area). - The iron core moves linearly along the axis of the former. - The magnetic coupling (and hence the induced EMF) is a **linear function** of core position $x$ within the operating range. ### Step 1 — Excitation of the Primary Let the AC supply be: $$e_p = E_p \sin(\omega t)$$ This induces a primary flux $\phi_p$ in the core, where $\omega = 2\pi f$ and $f$ is the excitation frequency. ### Step 2 — Mutual Inductance with the Secondaries When the core is at the **null (center)** position, by symmetry: $$M_1 = M_2 = M_0 \quad \text{(mutual inductances equal)}$$ When the core **moves up by a displacement $x$**: - Flux linking S1 increases $\Rightarrow M_1 = M_0 + \Delta M$ - Flux linking S2 decreases $\Rightarrow M_2 = M_0 - \Delta M$ Assuming **small linear displacement** (so $\Delta M$ is proportional to $x$): $$\Delta M = K_1 \, x$$ Therefore: $$M_1 = M_0 + K_1 x \quad \text{and} \quad M_2 = M_0 - K_1 x$$ ### Step 3 — Induced EMFs in the Secondaries By Faraday's Law of mutual induction: $$e_{s1} = -M_1 \frac{di_p}{dt} \quad \text{and} \quad e_{s2} = -M_2 \frac{di_p}{dt}$$ Since the primary current $i_p$ is sinusoidal: $$i_p = I_p \sin(\omega t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{di_p}{dt} = \omega I_p \cos(\omega t)$$ So: $$e_{s1} = -\omega I_p (M_0 + K_1 x) \cos(\omega t)$$ $$e_{s2} = -\omega I_p (M_0 - K_1 x) \cos(\omega t)$$ ### Step 4 — Differential (Series-Opposition) Output The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so the net output is: $$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$$ Substituting: $$e_o = -\omega I_p \cos(\omega t) \big[(M_0 + K_1 x) - (M_0 - K_1 x)\big]$$ Simplify inside the brackets: $$e_o = -\omega I_p \cos(\omega t) \, [2 K_1 x]$$ $$e_o = -2 \omega I_p K_1 x \cos(\omega t)$$ ### Step 5 — Final Compact Form Let us define the **sensitivity constant** $K = 2 \omega I_p K_1$. Then: $$e_o = -K x \cos(\omega t)$$ The **RMS magnitude** of the output is: $$E_o = K \, x \quad \text{(RMS, in Volts)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **This is the famous linear relation** $E_o \propto x$, which is why LVDT is treated as a linear transducer. The sign of $x$ (i.e., the **direction** of motion) is encoded in the **phase** of the output: a 180° phase shift indicates reversal of motion. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** An LVDT has a sensitivity of **2.5 mV/μm** with a ±5 mm range. The excitation is 6 V RMS at 5 kHz. Calculate the output voltage when the core is displaced by **3 mm** from the null position. ### Solution **Step 1:** Convert the displacement to micrometers. $$x = 3 \text{ mm} = 3000 \, \mu m$$ **Step 2:** Apply the output relation $E_o = K \cdot x$ (linear regime). $$E_o = 2.5 \text{ mV}/\mu m \times 3000 \, \mu m$$ **Step 3:** Compute the result. $$E_o = 7500 \text{ mV} = 7.5 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4:** Cross-check with the full-scale range. $$E_{o,\max} = 2.5 \text{ mV}/\mu m \times 5000 \, \mu m = 12.5 \text{ V (F.S.)}$$ Since $7.5 \text{ V} < 12.5 \text{ V}$, the LVDT is operating within its linear range. ✓ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix | Aspect | At Null Position | Core Displaced Up by x | Core Displaced Down by x | |---|---|---|---| | $M_1$ | $M_0$ | $M_0 + K_1 x$ | $M_0 - K_1 x$ | | $M_2$ | $M_0$ | $M_0 - K_1 x$ | $M_0 + K_1 x$ | | $E_{s1}$ | $E_o$ (ideally 0) | $E_o + Kx$ | $E_o - Kx$ | | $E_{s2}$ | $E_o$ (ideally 0) | $E_o - Kx$ | $E_o + Kx$ | | $E_o^{diff}$ | $0$ | $+2Kx$ (in phase) | $+2Kx$ (180° phase) | | Phase w.r.t. $E_p$ | — | Same (0°) | Opposite (180°) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LVDT Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Excitation Source
5V to 24V RMS
1kHz to 10kHz] -->|ep| P((Primary
Winding P)) P -->|flux link| C{Movable Soft-Iron Core
position x} C -->|coupling1| S1((Secondary
Winding S1)) C -->|coupling2| S2((Secondary
Winding S2)) S1 -->|es1| N[Differential
Junction] S2 -->|es2| N N -->|eo series opposition| PSD[Phase Sensitive
Detector] PSD -->|magnitude| V1[Voltmeter
Magnitude Display] PSD -->|phase| V2[Phase Indicator
Direction Display] C -.->|mechanical
displacement| C ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Cross-Sectional Schematic (Logical Layout) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Former[Hollow Cylindrical Former] direction TB S1top[Secondary Coil S1
Top Section] Pmid[Primary Coil P
Middle Section] S2bot[Secondary Coil S2
Bottom Section] Core[Soft Iron Core
Slides Axially] end Former --> SigOut{Differential Output
es1 minus es2} ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Output Characteristics Plot (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Displacement x negative
Core down] --> R1[Output eo negative
180 deg phase shift] Q2[Displacement x equals 0
Null position] --> R2[Output eo equals 0
No signal] Q3[Displacement x positive
Core up] --> R3[Output eo positive
in phase with ep] ``` > [!NOTE] > The relationship between displacement $x$ and the differential output $E_o$ is **linear** in the central working range, but **non-linear** (curve flattens) near the extreme ends of the stroke. This is the reason LVDTs are specified with a clear **linear range** and a total **mechanical range**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define LVDT and list any four of its salient features. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is a passive inductive transducer used to convert linear displacement into a proportional AC voltage output, based on the principle of variation of mutual inductance between a primary and two secondary windings due to the movement of a soft-iron core. **Four salient features:** 1. **Frictionless operation** — no contact between core and windings ⇒ infinite resolution. 2. **High sensitivity** — typically in the range of mV per μm of displacement. 3. **Linearity** — output voltage is linearly proportional to displacement over a wide range. 4. **Bidirectional measurement** — phase of output indicates direction of displacement. 5. *Rugged construction, high reliability, and inherent immunity to wear.* *(Any four well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)* --- ### Q2. Why is an LVDT called a "differential" transformer? Explain briefly. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The LVDT is called a *differential* transformer because its output is the **algebraic difference** between the EMFs induced in its two secondary windings, which are connected in **series opposition**. $$E_o^{diff} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$ When the core is at the null (center) position, $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$, so the differential output is zero. Any displacement of the core unbalances this difference, producing a non-zero output whose **magnitude** is proportional to displacement and **phase** indicates the direction. This differential (subtractive) arrangement is what gives the LVDT its high linearity, null-referencing capability, and rejection of common-mode disturbances such as temperature drift or supply variations. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat constructional diagram, describe the working principle of an LVDT. Show that the output voltage is a linear function of the core displacement. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Constructional Description:** An LVDT consists of a hollow cylindrical former (made of a non-magnetic material). Three coils are wound on this former: a **primary winding (P)** at the center, and **two identical secondary windings (S1 and S2)** placed symmetrically on either side of the primary. A movable **soft-iron core (armature)** slides along the axis inside the hollow former. The core is mechanically coupled to the object whose displacement is to be measured. **Working Principle:** - The primary is energized by an AC excitation voltage $e_p$. - The AC produces an alternating magnetic flux that links the core and, through it, the two secondaries. - The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so their induced EMFs subtract. - At the **null position** (core at center), the flux linking S1 and S2 is equal, so $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ and the differential output $E_o = 0$. - When the core is displaced by $x$ upwards, more flux links S1, so $E_{s1} > E_{s2}$ and the output becomes **positive and in phase** with $E_p$. - When the core is displaced by $x$ downwards, more flux links S2, so $E_{s2} > E_{s1}$ and the output is **180° out of phase** with $E_p$. **Mathematical Proof (already derived above):** Starting from the mutual-inductance expressions and Faraday's law, we obtained: $$E_o^{diff} = 2 K_1 \omega I_p \, x = K \cdot x$$ Since $K$ is a constant for a given LVDT and excitation, $E_o \propto x$, confirming the linear relationship. (Full derivation in **Section 3.1** may be reproduced in the answer sheet.) --- **(b)** An LVDT has the following specifications: excitation = **5 V RMS at 5 kHz**, sensitivity = **0.2 V/mm**, full-scale range = **±10 mm**. Determine (i) the output voltage at a displacement of **7 mm** from null, and (ii) the maximum possible RMS output voltage. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Given:** - Sensitivity $K = 0.2 \text{ V/mm}$ - Full-scale displacement $x_{max} = 10 \text{ mm}$ **Step 1 — Output at 7 mm displacement:** $$E_o = K \cdot x = 0.2 \text{ V/mm} \times 7 \text{ mm} = 1.4 \text{ V (RMS)}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum possible output (at full-scale):** $$E_{o,\max} = K \cdot x_{max} = 0.2 \text{ V/mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 2.0 \text{ V (RMS)}$$ **Verification of linearity:** Since the LVDT is specified as linear over $\pm 10$ mm, the value 7 mm lies well within the linear range. ✓ **Phase indication:** The phase of $E_o$ (same as or opposite to $E_p$) tells the direction; magnitude $|E_o| = 1.4$ V gives the displacement. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Draw the equivalent circuit of an LVDT referred to the primary side and derive the expression for the differential output voltage. Explain why the output is zero at the null position. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Equivalent Circuit Referred to Primary:** Each secondary is represented as a voltage source $E_{s1}$ or $E_{s2}$ in series with a leakage impedance. When referred to the primary side (through the turns ratio $n = N_s / N_p$), the impedances and the EMF get scaled accordingly. The two secondaries appear in **series-opposition** in the equivalent circuit. Let the referred secondary EMFs be $E_{s1}'$ and $E_{s2}'$ and the referred secondary impedances be $Z_{s1}'$ and $Z_{s2}'$. By the transformer EMF equation (assuming sinusoidal steady state): $$E_{s1}' = j \omega M_1 I_p \quad \text{and} \quad E_{s2}' = j \omega M_2 I_p$$ The differential output (referred to the primary) is: $$E_o' = E_{s1}' - E_{s2}' = j \omega I_p (M_1 - M_2)$$ Substituting $M_1 = M_0 + K_1 x$ and $M_2 = M_0 - K_1 x$: $$E_o' = j \omega I_p \cdot 2 K_1 x = 2 j \omega K_1 I_p x$$ Magnitude: $$\vert E_o' \vert = 2 \omega K_1 I_p \, x = K \cdot x$$ **Null position explanation:** At the null position, $x = 0$, so: $$E_o' = 2 j \omega K_1 I_p (0) = 0$$ This is because the **flux linkage** with S1 and S2 becomes **identically equal** at the geometric center, and since the secondaries are in series opposition, their EMFs exactly cancel, leaving a true zero output (in the ideal case). The residual voltage observed in practice is called the **null voltage** or **residual voltage**, caused by imperfect winding symmetry and harmonic distortions. --- **(b)** Compare LVDT with a **potentiometric transducer** for displacement measurement. List at least **four advantages** of LVDT over a potentiometer. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution | Parameter | LVDT | Potentiometer | |---|---|---| | Principle | Mutual inductance variation | Resistance variation (voltage divider) | | Contact / Friction | **No contact**, frictionless | **Wiper contact**, friction present | | Resolution | Practically **infinite** | Limited by wire pitch / wiper size | | Linearity | Excellent (≈ ±0.25 % F.S.) | Moderate, depends on loading | | Wear and Tear | **Nil** (no moving electrical contact) | **High** (wiper wears out) | | Life | Very long (millions of cycles) | Limited (thousands of cycles) | | Direction Sensing | Yes (via **phase** of output) | Yes (via **sign** of output) | | External Power | AC excitation required | DC reference required | | Sensitivity to Vibration | Low | High (wiper bounce) | | Hysteresis | Negligible | Noticeable | **Four key advantages of LVDT over potentiometer:** 1. **No friction and no contact** ⇒ infinite resolution and long operational life. 2. **No mechanical wear** ⇒ suitable for high-cycle dynamic measurements. 3. **High sensitivity and excellent linearity** over a wide range. 4. **Inherent bidirectional output** (phase-encoded direction). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Don't write "output = $E_{s1} + E_{s2}$"** — the secondaries are in **series opposition**, so the output is the **difference**, not the sum. This is the most common mark-losing error. > - **Don't forget the phase information** — the phase of $E_o$ encodes the *direction* of displacement, not just its magnitude. Many students only discuss magnitude and skip the phase, losing 2–3 marks. > - **Don't write $E_o = Kx$ without defining $K$** — the examiner expects an explicit sensitivity constant with units. > - **Avoid using `|` inside markdown tables** in your answer scripts — prefer `\vert` or `\mid` if reproducing formula tables. > - **Residual null voltage** is an *expected* practical phenomenon, not an "error". Mention it with a brief justification (winding asymmetry, harmonics). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **LVDT** = **L**inear **V**ariable **D**ifferential **T**ransformer; converts linear displacement into AC voltage. - It has **1 primary** and **2 secondaries** (S1, S2) on a **hollow former**; a **soft-iron core** slides inside. - Secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (anti-series) — output is the **differential** EMF. - **At null (center) position:** $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$ ⇒ **output = 0**. - **Displacement $x$ → output** $E_o = K \cdot x$ (linear, RMS value). - **Direction of displacement** is encoded in the **phase** of $E_o$ (same as or 180° out of phase with $E_p$). - **Sensitivity** $K = dE_o / dx$ — typically **mV/μm** or **V/mm**. - **Excitation:** AC voltage (1 V–24 V RMS) at **1 kHz to 20 kHz** (typical 2.5–10 kHz). - **Residual (null) voltage** in real LVDTs is due to winding asymmetry and harmonic distortion. - **Advantages:** Frictionless, infinite resolution, high linearity, bidirectional, no wear, rugged. - **Disadvantages:** Requires AC excitation, sensitive to temperature, limited to moderate displacements, needs a phase-sensitive demodulator for direction. - **Applications:** Aircraft control surfaces, hydraulic pistons, force/load cells, robotics, metrology. - **Most-tested formulas for KTU board exams:** - $E_o = 2K_1 \omega I_p x$ (magnitude form) - $E_o = K \cdot x$ (linear output) - $E_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_m$ (transformer EMF) - $E_{s1} = E_o + Kx$, $E_{s2} = E_o - Kx$

topics.title: Capacitive Transducers: Variable Air Gap, Variable Area, Variable Permittivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitive Transducers: Variable Air Gap, Variable Area, Variable Permittivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Capacitive Transducer

A **Capacitive Transducer** is a passive electronic device that converts a physical measurand (such as displacement, pressure, force, liquid level, or humidity) into a measurable change in electrical capacitance. The fundamental operating principle is governed by the general equation of a parallel-plate capacitor, where any one of the three defining geometric/dielectric parameters is intentionally varied by the input physical quantity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A capacitive transducer is a device in which the measurand causes a change in the value of capacitance, which is then detected by a suitable AC bridge, oscillator circuit, or charge amplifier to produce an electrical output proportional to the input.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Bucket" 

Imagine two flat metal plates (P1 and P2) separated by a small air gap — think of it as a **"water bucket"** where the amount of "electrical water" (charge Q) the bucket can hold depends on three things:

1. **How big is the bucket?** → Area of the plates (**A**)
2. **How close are the bucket walls?** → Distance between plates (**d**)
3. **What material is the bucket made of?** → Dielectric material's permittivity (**ε**)

If you **squeeze** the plates closer (reduce *d*), the bucket gets tighter and holds more charge (capacitance **increases**). If you **slide** one plate out sideways (reduce *A*), the bucket gets smaller and holds less charge (capacitance **decreases**). If you **pour a different liquid** (oil instead of air) between the plates, the dielectric changes and capacitance shifts. This is the **entire principle** of capacitive transduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** A capacitive transducer does not generate electricity on its own. It must be energized by an **AC source** or integrated into an **oscillator/bridge circuit** to produce a usable voltage output. This is a passive transducer.

## 1.3 Fundamental Governing Equation

The general capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is universally given as:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, A}{d}$$

Where the standard metrics and physical constants are:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C$ | Capacitance | **Farads (F)** |
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of free space | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** |
| $\varepsilon_r$ | Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) | **Dimensionless** |
| $A$ | Effective overlapping area of plates | **m²** |
| $d$ | Distance between the plates (air gap) | **m (or mm)** |

## 1.4 Classification of Capacitive Transducers (KTU Board Focus)

Based on the parameter that is varied by the measurand, capacitive transducers are classified into **three** fundamental types — each is a **high-yield 7–14 mark KTU question** topic:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Classes:**
> 1. **Variable Air Gap Type** — $d$ changes (most common; used for pressure, displacement, acceleration).
> 2. **Variable Area Type** — $A$ changes (used for angular/linear displacement, liquid level).
> 3. **Variable Permittivity (Dielectric) Type** — $\varepsilon_r$ changes (used for liquid level, humidity, thickness).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Variable Air Gap Capacitive Transducer

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
The plates of the capacitor remain **stationary in area and dielectric**, but the **separation distance $d$** between them is altered by the measurand. As $d$ changes, $C$ varies in a **non-linear, hyperbolic** manner. This is the most widely deployed configuration in industrial pressure sensors, microphones, and accelerometers.

### 2.1.2 Working Logic — Step by Step
- The input physical quantity (e.g., pressure on a diaphragm) causes one plate to deflect.
- Deflection alters the air-gap thickness $d$.
- The capacitance $C$ changes **inversely and non-linearly** with $d$.
- The change in $C$ is detected by an AC Wheatstone bridge or a relaxation oscillator.
- The bridge imbalance voltage becomes the output signal.

### 2.1.3 Mathematical Formulation

For a single air-gap capacitor with a fixed dielectric (air, $\varepsilon_r = 1$):

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, A}{d}$$

Let initial gap be $d_0$ and initial capacitance be $C_0$:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, A}{d_0}$$

If the gap increases by a small amount $\Delta d$, the new capacitance is $C$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, A}{d_0 + \Delta d}$$

**Sensitivity of the transducer** is defined as the rate of change of $C$ with respect to $d$:

$$S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \, A}{d^2}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that capacitance decreases as gap increases. The magnitude of sensitivity increases as $d$ becomes smaller — hence smaller gaps yield higher sensitivity (a critical design trade-off).

### 2.1.4 Differential (Push-Pull) Air-Gap Configuration
To **linearize** the response and double the sensitivity, two capacitors are connected in a **differential arrangement**: a central movable plate forms $C_1$ and $C_2$ with two fixed outer plates. When the central plate moves by $\Delta d$, $C_1$ increases while $C_2$ decreases, and the bridge produces a linear, amplified differential output.

## 2.2 Type 2 — Variable Area Capacitive Transducer

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
The distance $d$ and dielectric $\varepsilon_r$ are **held constant**, but the **effective overlapping area $A$** between the plates is varied by the measurand. This produces a **linear** relationship between $C$ and the displacement.

### 2.2.2 Working Logic
- One plate is fixed; the other is allowed to slide linearly or rotate angularly.
- Movement changes the overlap area $A$ while keeping the gap $d$ unchanged.
- Capacitance varies **linearly** with displacement: $C \propto A$.
- This linearity is the chief advantage of the variable-area type over the variable-gap type.

### 2.2.3 Mathematical Formulation

For linear displacement of one plate by a distance $x$ (where the plate has width $b$ and fixed gap $d$):

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, b \, (L - x)}{d}$$

The sensitivity is constant and equals:

$$S = \frac{dC}{dx} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, b}{d}$$

For **angular displacement** ($\theta$ in radians) of a rotary plate with mean radius $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, (R_2^2 - R_1^2) \, \theta}{2d}$$

Sensitivity: $S = \dfrac{dC}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon_r \, (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2d}$

## 2.3 Type 3 — Variable Permittivity (Dielectric) Capacitive Transducer

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
The plate area $A$ and gap $d$ are fixed, but the **dielectric material** between the plates (and hence $\varepsilon_r$) changes due to the measurand. The transducer detects changes in physical properties of the dielectric — such as **liquid level, moisture content, or chemical composition**.

### 2.3.2 Working Logic
- A dielectric medium (liquid, gas, or solid) enters or occupies a portion of the capacitor gap.
- The change in $\varepsilon_r$ produces a proportional change in $C$.
- A common application is **liquid-level measurement** in a non-metallic tank: as the liquid level rises, more of the gap is filled with liquid (higher $\varepsilon_r$ ≈ 2–80) replacing air ($\varepsilon_r$ ≈ 1), increasing $C$.

### 2.3.3 Mathematical Formulation

If a dielectric of thickness $t$ (less than total gap $d$) is inserted between plates of area $A$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, A}{\left(d - t\right) + \dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_r}}$$

For a **liquid level sensor** where liquid of permittivity $\varepsilon_{r1}$ fills a height $h$ of the capacitor and air ($\varepsilon_{r2} = 1$) fills the remaining height $(H - h)$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \, b}{\left(\dfrac{H - h}{\varepsilon_{r1}}\right) + \dfrac{h}{1}}$$

(where $b$ is the plate width). The sensitivity is:

$$S = \frac{dC}{dh} = \varepsilon_0 \, b \left(\frac{1}{1} - \frac{1}{\varepsilon_{r1}}\right)$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the table below — it covers ~80% of numerical questions in KTU ESE.**

| S.No. | Transducer Type | Governing Equation | Sensitivity | Linearity | Typical Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Variable Air Gap | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | $\left\vert S \right\vert = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}$ | **Non-linear (hyperbolic)** | Pressure, displacement, acceleration |
| 2 | Differential Air Gap | $\Delta C = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 A \, \Delta d}{d^2 - \Delta d^2}$ | $\dfrac{d(\Delta C)}{d(\Delta d)} = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 A \, d}{(d^2 - \Delta d^2)^2}$ | **Approximately linear for small $\Delta d$** | High-precision pressure sensors |
| 3 | Variable Area (Linear) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r b (L-x)}{d}$ | $\dfrac{dC}{dx} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r b}{d}$ | **Perfectly linear** | Linear displacement, thickness gauges |
| 4 | Variable Area (Angular) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (R_2^2 - R_1^2)\theta}{2d}$ | $\dfrac{dC}{d\theta} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r (R_2^2 - R_1^2)}{2d}$ | **Linear in $\theta$** | Angular displacement, torque |
| 5 | Variable Permittivity | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{(d-t) + t/\varepsilon_r}$ | $\dfrac{dC}{d\varepsilon_r} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A \, t}{[\varepsilon_r(d-t) + t]^2}$ | **Non-linear** | Liquid level, humidity, composition |
| 6 | Liquid Level Sensor | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 b \, \varepsilon_{r1} \, \varepsilon_{r2}}{H}$ (composite) | $S = \varepsilon_0 b \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{r1}}\right)$ | **Linear in $h$** | Tank level measurement |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Automotive:** Capacitive accelerometers (MEMS) — variable air-gap type — are used in airbag deployment systems, stability control, and inertial measurement units.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Capacitive pressure transmitters dominate clean-room semiconductor manufacturing and pharmaceutical applications due to their high accuracy and absence of mercury.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Capacitive touchscreens in smartphones rely on **variable permittivity** (your finger changes the effective dielectric).
- **Oil & Gas:** Capacitive level gauges in cryogenic LNG tanks use the variable-area or variable-dielectric principle for non-contact measurement.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensitivity of Variable Air-Gap Transducer

**Problem Setup:** A parallel plate capacitive transducer has a plate area of $A = 400 \text{ mm}^2$ and an initial air gap of $d_0 = 0.5 \text{ mm}$. Determine:
- (i) The initial capacitance.
- (ii) The sensitivity at the initial position.
- (iii) The change in capacitance for a gap increase of $\Delta d = 0.05 \text{ mm}$.

### Step 1 — Convert Units to SI

The plate area in SI units:

$$A = 400 \text{ mm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

The initial gap in SI units:

$$d_0 = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Initial Capacitance $C_0$

Using the basic capacitance formula with air as dielectric ($\varepsilon_r = 1$):

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{d_0}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{35.416 \times 10^{-16}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C_0 = 7.0832 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 7.08 \text{ pF}$$

> **Textual interpretation:** With the given geometry, the transducer has an initial capacitance of approximately **7 picofarads** — a typical order of magnitude for capacitive sensors.

### Step 3 — Compute Sensitivity at Initial Position

The general sensitivity expression is:

$$S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}$$

Evaluating at $d = d_0$:

$$S_0 = -\frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{(5 \times 10^{-4})^2}$$

$$S_0 = -\frac{35.416 \times 10^{-16}}{25 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$S_0 = -1.4166 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/m} = -14.166 \text{ pF/m}$$

> **Textual interpretation:** The negative sign denotes that **C decreases as d increases**. The magnitude $\vert S_0 \vert = 14.17 \text{ pF/m}$ tells us the capacitance reduces by ~14 pF for every meter of gap increase — equivalently, ~0.0142 pF per 1 mm.

### Step 4 — Approximate Change in Capacitance for $\Delta d = 0.05 \text{ mm}$

For a small displacement, we can use the linear approximation:

$$\Delta C \approx S_0 \cdot \Delta d$$

$$\Delta C = -1.4166 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\Delta C = -7.083 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F} = -0.7083 \text{ pF}$$

### Step 5 — Exact (Non-Linear) Change in Capacitance for Verification

The exact new capacitance is:

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 + \Delta d}$$

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{(5 + 0.5) \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{35.416 \times 10^{-16}}{5.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 6.4393 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 6.439 \text{ pF}$$

The exact change is:

$$\Delta C_{\text{exact}} = 6.439 - 7.083 = -0.644 \text{ pF}$$

> **Textual interpretation:** The exact change ($-0.644 \text{ pF}$) differs from the linearized estimate ($-0.708 \text{ pF}$) by about **9%**, confirming that the hyperbolic response is non-linear even for a modest 10% change in gap. This is precisely why the **differential (push-pull) configuration** is preferred in KTU-grade instrument design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Differential Air-Gap Transducer (Full Derivation)

**Setup:** A differential capacitive transducer has a central movable plate and two fixed outer plates. Initial gap from central plate to each outer plate is $d$. If the central plate moves by a small displacement $x$ towards the upper fixed plate, derive an expression for the differential change in capacitance.

### Step 1 — Define the Two Capacitances

Upper capacitor (gap reduced):

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - x}$$

Lower capacitor (gap increased):

$$C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d + x}$$

### Step 2 — Form the Difference

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - x} - \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d + x}$$

### Step 3 — Algebraic Simplification

Taking common denominator $(d-x)(d+x) = d^2 - x^2$:

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \left[\frac{(d + x) - (d - x)}{d^2 - x^2}\right]$$

$$\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \left[\frac{2x}{d^2 - x^2}\right]$$

$$\boxed{\Delta C = \frac{2 \, \varepsilon_0 \, A \, x}{d^2 - x^2}}$$

### Step 4 — Sensitivity of the Differential Configuration

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$$S_{\text{diff}} = \frac{d(\Delta C)}{dx} = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A (d^2 - x^2) - 2\varepsilon_0 A x(-2x)}{(d^2 - x^2)^2}$$

$$S_{\text{diff}} = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A d^2 - 2\varepsilon_0 A x^2 + 4\varepsilon_0 A x^2}{(d^2 - x^2)^2}$$

$$S_{\text{diff}} = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A d^2 + 2\varepsilon_0 A x^2}{(d^2 - x^2)^2}$$

$$S_{\text{diff}} = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A (d^2 + x^2)}{(d^2 - x^2)^2}$$

### Step 5 — Small-Displacement Linearization

For $x \ll d$, we neglect $x^2$ terms:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A \, x}{d^2}$$

This is a **linear function of $x$** — proving the linearizing benefit of the differential arrangement.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Variable Permittivity with Composite Dielectric

**Setup:** A parallel plate capacitor of plate area $A = 200 \text{ cm}^2$ and total gap $d = 2 \text{ mm}$ has two dielectric slabs of equal thickness $t = 1 \text{ mm}$ placed in series. Slab 1 has $\varepsilon_{r1} = 4$ (glass) and Slab 2 has $\varepsilon_{r2} = 2.5$ (oil). Find the equivalent capacitance.

### Step 1 — Series Capacitance Formula

For dielectrics in series, individual capacitances add reciprocally:

$$\frac{1}{C_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Each Individual Capacitance

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r1} A}{t} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 200 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_1 = \frac{8.854 \times 4 \times 200 \times 10^{-13}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{7083.2 \times 10^{-13}}{10^{-3}}$$

$$C_1 = 70.832 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F} = 7.0832 \text{ nF}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r2} A}{t} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 200 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{8.854 \times 2.5 \times 200 \times 10^{-13}}{10^{-3}} = \frac{4427 \times 10^{-13}}{10^{-3}}$$

$$C_2 = 44.27 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F} = 4.427 \text{ nF}$$

### Step 3 — Combine in Series

$$\frac{1}{C_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{7.0832} + \frac{1}{4.427} \text{ nF}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{C_{\text{eq}}} = 0.1412 + 0.2259 = 0.3671 \text{ nF}^{-1}$$

$$C_{\text{eq}} = \frac{1}{0.3671} = 2.724 \text{ nF}$$

### Step 4 — Verification (Composite Formula)

Using the direct formula for series dielectrics:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\dfrac{t_1}{\varepsilon_{r1}} + \dfrac{t_2}{\varepsilon_{r2}}}$$

$$C = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 200 \times 10^{-4}}{\dfrac{10^{-3}}{4} + \dfrac{10^{-3}}{2.5}}$$

$$C = \frac{17.708 \times 10^{-14}}{0.25 \times 10^{-3} + 0.4 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C = \frac{17.708 \times 10^{-14}}{0.65 \times 10^{-3}} = 27.243 \times 10^{-11} = 2.724 \text{ nF} \checkmark$$

> **Textual interpretation:** Both methods agree — the equivalent capacitance is **2.724 nF**, illustrating how composite dielectrics allow tunable permittivity (the foundation of variable-permittivity transducers).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance Table

| Performance Criterion | Variable Air Gap | Variable Area | Variable Permittivity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linearity** | Poor (hyperbolic) | **Excellent (linear)** | Moderate |
| **Sensitivity** | **Highest (large for small d)** | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Range of measurement** | Small (~mm) | Wide (mm to m) | Moderate |
| **Hysteresis** | Low (with proper design) | Very low | Moderate (dielectric dependent) |
| **Temperature stability** | Good | Good | **Poor (dielectric varies with T)** |
| **Construction complexity** | Simple | Simple | **High (sealed dielectric path)** |
| **Typical Measurand** | Pressure, force, acceleration | Displacement, level | **Level, humidity, composition** |
| **KTU-Examiner Pick** | Most-tested | Often asked as derivation | Frequently on capacitive gauges |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Capacitive Measurement System

The following block diagram depicts the **complete signal-flow chain** from the physical measurand to the electrical output for any capacitive transducer. This block-level view abstracts the physical geometry and focuses on system interactions — strictly compliant with the Mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, plain text labels).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Physical Measurand: Pressure, Displacement, Level, Humidity] --> B[Primary Sensing Element: Variable Capacitor Cx]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Block: AC Excitation Source and Bridge Circuit]
    C --> D[Detector Stage: Phase Sensitive Demodulator or Amplifier]
    D --> E[Output Stage: Linearised Voltage or Current Signal]
    E --> F[Display and Recording: Digital Readout or SCADA]
    F --> G[Calibration Feedback Loop: Reference Capacitor Cref]

    subgraph AC_Excitation [AC Excitation Subsystem]
        H[High Frequency Oscillator 50kHz to 1MHz] --> I[Stable Amplitude Voltage Source]
    end

    subgraph Bridge_Network [Wheatstone Bridge Network]
        J[Reference Arm Capacitor C1] --> K[Unknown Arm Capacitor Cx]
        K --> L[Null Detector Output Vout proportional to deltaC]
    end

    subgraph Output_Conditioning [Output Signal Conditioning]
        M[Rectifier and Filter] --> N[Linearisation Network]
        N --> O[Final Standardised Output 0 to 10V or 4 to 20mA]
    end

    AC_Excitation --> Bridge_Network
    Bridge_Network --> Output_Conditioning
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three Types of Capacitive Transducers

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Type1 [Variable Air Gap Type]
        T1A[Fixed Plate P1] --- T1B[Movable Diaphragm Plate P2]
        T1B --> T1C[Change in Air Gap Distance d]
        T1C --> T1D[Capacitance Cx decreases as d increases]
    end

    subgraph Type2 [Variable Area Type]
        T2A[Fixed Plate P1] --- T2B[Sliding Plate P2]
        T2B --> T2C[Change in Overlap Area A]
        T2C --> T2D[Capacitance Cx decreases as A decreases]
    end

    subgraph Type3 [Variable Permittivity Type]
        T3A[Fixed Plates P1 and P2] --- T3B[Dielectric Medium between Plates]
        T3B --> T3C[Change in Relative Permittivity epsilon r]
        T3C --> T3D[Capacitance Cx changes proportionally to epsilon r]
    end

    Type1 --> Measurand1[Pressure, Force, Acceleration]
    Type2 --> Measurand2[Linear and Angular Displacement, Liquid Level]
    Type3 --> Measurand3[Liquid Level, Humidity, Chemical Composition]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Input Displacement x applied to Central Plate]) --> Move1[Central Plate Shifts by x]

    Move1 --> Branch2[Lower Gap becomes d plus x]

    Branch2 --> C2[Capacitor C2 decreases]
    C1 --> Bridge[Connected to AC Wheatstone Bridge]
    C2 --> Bridge
    Bridge --> Diff[Differential Output proportional to 2 epsilon0 A x divided by d squared]
    Diff --> End([Linearised Analogue Output Voltage])

```

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Physical Construction (Text Representation)

Since Mermaid cannot draw physical components, the construction of the three transducer types is described schematically:

**(a) Variable Air Gap Transducer (Pressure Sensor):**

```
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │  FIXED UPPER PLATE  │  ←── P1 (Stationary)
   │  ●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●  │
   │═════════════════════│  ←── d (Air Gap, Varies with Pressure)
   │  ○○○○○○○○○○○○○○○○○  │
   │  MOVABLE DIAPHRAGM  │  ←── P2 (Deflects under Pressure)
   │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  │
   │  PRESSURE INLET     │
   └─────────────────────┘
        Output: Cx = ε0A/d
```

**(b) Variable Area Transducer (Displacement Sensor):**

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   │  FIXED PLATE P1 (Width b)    │
   │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │  ←── P1
   │══════════════════════════════│
   │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  │  ←── P2 (Slides)
   │  MOVING PLATE P2             │
   │        ◄──── x ────►         │
   │  Effective Area A = b × (L-x)│
   └──────────────────────────────┘
        Output: Cx = ε0 εr b (L−x)/d
```

**(c) Variable Permittivity Transducer (Liquid Level):**

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   │  FIXED PLATE P1 (Outer)      │
   │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │  ←── P1
   │══════════════════════════════│
   │▒▒▒▒▒ AIR (εr = 1) ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│
   │▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│
   │▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒│

   │══════════════════════════════│
   │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  │  ←── P2 (Fixed)
   └──────────────────────────────┘
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a capacitive transducer and list its three types based on the parameter varied. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **capacitive transducer** is a device that converts a physical quantity (such as displacement, pressure, or liquid level) into a measurable change in electrical capacitance. The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is given by:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

The three types based on the varied parameter are:

1. **Variable Air Gap Type** — distance $d$ varies.
2. **Variable Area Type** — overlapping plate area $A$ varies.
3. **Variable Permittivity Type** — dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ varies.

### Q2. Why is the differential configuration preferred over the single-plate air-gap capacitive transducer? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The differential configuration is preferred for three reasons:

1. **Linearity:** A single-plate air-gap transducer exhibits a **non-linear (hyperbolic)** $C$-$d$ relationship. The differential arrangement yields a **linearized** output for small displacements, since $\Delta C \approx \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 A x}{d^2}$, which is a linear function of displacement $x$.
2. **Double Sensitivity:** The output is proportional to the **difference** of two capacitances, effectively doubling the sensitivity.
3. **Temperature Compensation:** Common-mode errors (temperature drift, supply variations) cancel out since both capacitors experience identical environmental conditions.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the sensitivity of a parallel-plate **variable air-gap** capacitive transducer. Explain why the response is non-linear and how the differential configuration overcomes this limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel-plate capacitive transducer has plates of area $300 \text{ mm}^2$ separated by an air gap of $1 \text{ mm}$. Calculate:
- (i) The initial capacitance.
- (ii) The sensitivity at the initial gap.
- (iii) The percentage change in capacitance when the gap is increased by 0.1 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation of Sensitivity — 7 Marks**

**Step 1:** Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor (air dielectric):

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

**Step 2:** Sensitivity is defined as the rate of change of $C$ with respect to the gap $d$:

$$S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}$$

**Step 3:** The negative sign indicates $C$ decreases as $d$ increases. The magnitude of sensitivity is **inversely proportional to $d^2$** — meaning smaller gaps produce exponentially higher sensitivity. This is also why the response is **non-linear (hyperbolic)**.

**Step 4:** For a differential arrangement, the central movable plate forms $C_1$ and $C_2$ with two fixed outer plates. The differential output is:

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A \, x}{d^2 - x^2}$$

**Step 5:** For $x \ll d$ (small displacements), $x^2$ is negligible, giving:

$$\Delta C \approx \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2} \cdot x$$

This is **linear in $x$**, confirming linearization through the differential topology.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**

$$A = 300 \text{ mm}^2 = 300 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$d_0 = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}, \quad \Delta d = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

**Part (i): Initial Capacitance**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{26.562 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-3}} = 2.6562 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 2.66 \text{ pF}$$

**Part (ii): Sensitivity at Initial Gap**

$$S_0 = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0^2} = -\frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^{-4}}{(10^{-3})^2}$$

$$S_0 = -\frac{26.562 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-6}} = -2.6562 \times 10^{-8} \text{ F/m} = -26.56 \text{ pF/m}$$

**Part (iii): Percentage Change in Capacitance**

New gap: $d_{\text{new}} = d_0 + \Delta d = 1.1 \text{ mm} = 1.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_{\text{new}}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 3 \times 10^{-4}}{1.1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{26.562 \times 10^{-16}}{1.1 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.4147 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 2.41 \text{ pF}$$

Percentage change:

$$\% \Delta C = \frac{C_{\text{new}} - C_0}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{2.41 - 2.66}{2.66} \times 100$$

$$\% \Delta C = \frac{-0.25}{2.66} \times 100 = -9.40\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A 10% increase in air gap produced a **9.40% decrease** in capacitance — confirming the **non-linear, inverse proportionality** between $C$ and $d$.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **variable-area** capacitive transducer used for displacement measurement. Derive the expression for capacitance and sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two parallel plates of area $250 \text{ mm}^2$ are separated by a $0.5 \text{ mm}$ air gap. A dielectric slab of thickness $0.25 \text{ mm}$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 5$ is introduced into the gap. Calculate the new capacitance and the percentage increase in capacitance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Variable-Area Transducer — 7 Marks**

**Construction:** Two rectangular plates of width $b$ and length $L$ are mounted parallel with a fixed air gap $d$. One plate is **stationary**, the other is **free to translate** linearly or rotate.

**Working:** When the movable plate slides by a distance $x$, the effective overlap area becomes:

$$A_{\text{eff}} = b \times (L - x)$$

The capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r b (L - x)}{d}$$

**Sensitivity:**

$$S = \frac{dC}{dx} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r b}{d}$$

The sensitivity is **constant** (independent of $x$), which is the principal advantage of this type — output is a **perfectly linear function** of displacement.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**

$$A = 250 \text{ mm}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$d = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$t = 0.25 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\varepsilon_r = 5$$

**Step 1 — Initial Capacitance (air only):**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{22.135 \times 10^{-16}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 4.427 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance with Dielectric Slab Inserted:**

The capacitor now has two regions in series:
- Dielectric region of thickness $t = 0.25 \text{ mm}$, $\varepsilon_r = 5$
- Air region of thickness $(d - t) = 0.25 \text{ mm}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{(d - t) + \dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_r}}$$

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4} + \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{5}}$$

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{22.135 \times 10^{-16}}{2.5 \times 10^{-4} + 0.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{22.135 \times 10^{-16}}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 7.378 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 7.378 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Increase:**

$$\% \Delta C = \frac{C_{\text{new}} - C_0}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{7.378 - 4.427}{4.427} \times 100$$

$$\% \Delta C = \frac{2.951}{4.427} \times 100 \approx 66.66\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Inserting the dielectric slab **more than doubled** the capacitance (a 66.66% increase) — confirming that permittivity is a powerful tuning parameter in capacitive transducer design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Unit Blunder:** Students frequently substitute plate area in mm² without converting to m². This single error wipes out 3–4 marks. Always convert: $1 \text{ mm}^2 = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$.
> 2. **Forgetting $\varepsilon_0$:** Some students drop the $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ constant. This is a guaranteed **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Sign Error in Sensitivity:** Writing $S = +\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d^2}$ instead of the negative form. The negative sign is critical — the examiner expects it.
> 4. **Mixing Up Series vs Parallel Formulas:** For variable permittivity problems, students wrongly use the parallel combination formula. **Always use series (sum of reciprocals) when dielectrics are stacked along the field.**
> 5. **Skipping Derivations in 7-mark sub-parts:** Partial differentiation steps (especially in differential air-gap problems) must be shown explicitly. A "final answer only" approach typically receives 3 out of 7 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- **Master Equation:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — memorize in SI units.
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$ — commit to memory.
- **Three types of capacitive transducers:** Air Gap, Area, Permittivity — know the **one-word identification** for any KTU MCQ.
- **Air Gap:** Hyperbolic response, **highest sensitivity**, used for pressure/acceleration.
- **Area:** **Linear** response, used for displacement, lower sensitivity than air gap.
- **Permittivity:** Used for level/humidity, sensitive to **temperature** (drawback).
- **Differential configuration** is the **most exam-favorite** configuration: it linearizes, doubles sensitivity, and rejects common-mode errors.
- **Differential capacitance formula:** $\Delta C = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0 A x}{d^2 - x^2}$ — derive it cold in under 90 seconds.
- **Series vs Parallel dielectrics:** Use series formula when slab is **along the field direction** (slab fills part of the gap).
- **Capacitance is always in Farads (F)**, but values are typically **pF** (picofarads) — remember $1 \text{ pF} = 10^{-12} \text{ F}$.
- **KTU favorite numerical:** Plate area in $\text{cm}^2$ or $\text{mm}^2$, gap in mm, dielectric in $\varepsilon_r$ — always convert to SI before calculation.
- **Advantages to memorize:** High sensitivity, good frequency response, low power, low temperature coefficient (with proper design), works for static and dynamic measurements.
- **Disadvantages to memorize:** Stray capacitance problems, requires **shielded cables**, non-linear in air-gap type, low output (requires amplification).
- **Real-world link:** Smartphone touchscreens = variable permittivity; MEMS accelerometers = variable air gap; tank level gauges = variable area or permittivity.
- **Examiner trap:** If asked "which type is most sensitive?" — answer **variable air gap** (justified by $S \propto 1/d^2$). If asked "which is most linear?" — answer **variable area** (justified by $C \propto A$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Piezoelectric # Piezoelectric Transducers — A KTU 2024 Premium Study Module ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Piezoelectric Effect (KTU Standard Definition):** > The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain crystalline materials to generate an internal electric charge (and consequently a measurable potential difference across their faces) when subjected to a mechanical stress, and conversely, to undergo a mechanical deformation when an external electric field is applied across them. Materials exhibiting this property are termed **piezoelectric materials**, and the device constructed from such a crystal, used to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy (or vice versa), is called a **piezoelectric transducer**. The term "piezo" originates from the Greek word *piezein*, meaning **"to press"** or "to squeeze." The phenomenon was first discovered by **Jacques Curie and Pierre Curie in the year 1880** during their experiments on the crystallographic properties of quartz, tourmaline, and Rochelle salt. ### 1.2 Two Manifestations of the Piezoelectric Effect | Effect Type | Input | Output | KTU Notation | Practical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Direct (Generator) Effect** | Mechanical Force / Stress | Electric Charge / Voltage | **d** coefficient | Force, pressure, vibration sensors | | **Converse (Motor) Effect** | Electric Voltage | Mechanical Strain / Displacement | **d** coefficient (reciprocal) | Actuators, ultrasonic transducers, speakers | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** The same piezoelectric coefficient **d** governs BOTH the direct and converse effects. This reciprocity is a direct consequence of the **Maxwell relations** of thermodynamics and is frequently asked as a 3-mark question. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Squishy Matchstick Box" Intuition Imagine a small matchbox wrapped in a thin aluminum foil that is connected to a sensitive micro-ammeter via two wires. - **Step 1 (Direct Effect):** You press the top of the matchbox with your finger. The internal structure of the box is deformed asymmetrically, causing a tiny displacement of the foil surfaces — and the meter shows a brief kick. **The "press" produced electricity.** - **Step 2 (Converse Effect):** Now you apply a small battery voltage across the foil. The matchbox actually **physically bulges or contracts** very slightly because of the electric field pushing on the charged internal dipoles. **The "voltage" produced motion.** A piezoelectric crystal behaves exactly like this matchbox: a tiny, exquisitely sensitive "press-to-electricity" and "electricity-to-press" converter. The Greek origin of the word "piezo" is precisely the **"press"** you apply with your finger. ### 1.4 Essential Physical Constants & Material Properties > [!NOTE] > **Universal Physical Constants for Piezoelectric Analysis:** > - **Vacuum Permittivity:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** > - **Relative Permittivity of Quartz:** $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.6$ (varies with cut) > - **Curie Temperature of Quartz:** $T_c = 573^\circ\text{C}$ (above which piezoelectricity vanishes) > - **Curie Temperature of PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate):** $T_c \approx 350^\circ\text{C}$ ### 1.5 Classification of Piezoelectric Materials > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Watch-List:** Examiners frequently test material classification. Memorize at least one example from EACH of the following three categories: **A. Naturally Occurring (Single-Crystal) Piezoelectric Materials** - **Quartz (SiO₂):** Highest mechanical Q-factor, excellent long-term stability, used in precision oscillators and accelerometers. - **Rochelle Salt (NaKC₄H₄O₆·4H₂O):** Very high piezoelectric coefficient, but extremely sensitive to humidity and temperature (rarely used in modern industrial transducers). - **Tourmaline:** Naturally polarized, exhibits strong pyroelectric and piezoelectric behavior. **B. Synthetic (Polycrystalline) Piezoelectric Ceramics — Industrial Workhorses** - **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT):** Most widely used piezoelectric ceramic globally; very high $d_{33}$ values (300–600 pC/N). - **Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃):** First discovered synthetic piezoelectric ceramic. - **Lead Titanate (PbTiO₃):** High Curie temperature, used in high-temperature applications. **C. Polymeric Piezoelectric Materials (Modern & Flexible)** - **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride):** A flexible polymer film; can be moulded into arbitrary shapes for wearable sensors and acoustic pickups. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Does Piezoelectricity Exist? — The Crystallographic Origin > [!NOTE] > **The 'Why' Behind Piezoelectricity:** > Piezoelectricity is fundamentally a **crystallographic asymmetry** phenomenon. In a non-piezoelectric crystal (such as NaCl), the centers of positive and negative charge coincide, forming a **centrosymmetric lattice**. In a piezoelectric crystal (such as quartz — SiO₂), the lattice is **non-centrosymmetric**: the center of positive charge (Si⁴⁺ ions) and the center of negative charge (O²⁻ ions) **do not coincide** even at rest, forming a permanent **electric dipole**. - When the crystal is **mechanically stressed**, these dipoles are displaced asymmetrically, generating a net surface charge density $\sigma_p$ on the crystal faces — this is the **Direct Piezoelectric Effect**. - When an **external electric field E** is applied, the dipoles re-align, causing an asymmetric lattice strain — this is the **Converse Piezoelectric Effect**. ### 2.2 Symmetry Constraint — The 32 Crystal Classes Rule Of the **32 distinct crystallographic point groups**, exactly **20 classes are non-centrosymmetric**. Of these 20, **10 classes exhibit a unique polar axis** and are therefore **pyroelectric**, while the remaining **20 non-centrosymmetric classes (including all 10 pyroelectric ones)** exhibit piezoelectricity. Hence, **piezoelectricity is a more general property than pyroelectricity** — a frequently confused KTU 2024 conceptual point. ### 2.3 The Piezoelectric Constitutive Equations (Tensor Form) The behaviour of a piezoelectric crystal is fully described by two coupled linear constitutive equations: $$D = d \cdot T + \varepsilon^T \cdot E$$ $$S = s^E \cdot T + d_t \cdot E$$ Where the symbols carry the following engineering meaning: - $D$ = Electric displacement (C/m²) - $T$ = Applied mechanical stress (N/m²) - $E$ = Applied electric field (V/m) - $S$ = Mechanical strain (dimensionless) - $d$ = **Piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N or m/V) - $d_t$ = Transpose of $d$ (converse effect coefficient) - $\varepsilon^T$ = Permittivity at constant stress - $s^E$ = Elastic compliance at constant electric field ### 2.4 The Two Fundamental Piezoelectric Coefficients > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Favourite:** The two coefficients **d** and **g** are inversely related through permittivity, and both are routinely tested. **A. Piezoelectric Charge Coefficient (d):** $$d = \frac{\text{Charge generated per unit area}}{\text{Applied force}} \quad \text{(Units: C/N or m/V)}$$ **B. Piezoelectric Voltage Coefficient (g):** $$g = \frac{\text{Electric field developed}}{\text{Applied mechanical stress}} \quad \text{(Units: V·m/N or m²/C)}$$ **C. The Fundamental Relationship:** $$g = \frac{d}{\varepsilon^T} = \frac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r}$$ ### 2.5 The Three Common Piezoelectric Modes (KTU Board Favourite) | Mode | Symbol | Input Axis | Output Axis | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Longitudinal** | $d_{33}$ | Force along poling (3) axis | Charge along poling (3) axis | Stack actuators, force sensors | | **Transverse** | $d_{31}$ | Force along (1) axis, perpendicular to poling | Charge along (3) axis (poling) | Bimorphs, bending actuators | | **Shear** | $d_{15}$ | Shear stress | Shear charge | Shear-mode accelerometers, ultrasonics | ### 2.6 The Complete KTU Formula Sheet (Print & Memorize) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL MARKDOWN NOTE:** Per the KTU-Premium-Engine protocol, all absolute-value bars and conditionals in the following table are rendered using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve table syntax integrity. No vertical pipes are used inside cell contents. | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | $Q$ = charge (C), $d$ = coefficient (C/N), $F$ = force (N) | Charge generated by direct effect | | 2 | $V_o = \dfrac{Q}{C_p} = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$ | $V_o$ = output voltage, $C_p$ = crystal capacitance | Open-circuit output voltage | | 3 | $C_p = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$ | $A$ = electrode area, $t$ = crystal thickness | Parallel capacitance of the crystal | | 4 | $V_o = \dfrac{d \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} \cdot F$ | Substitute $C_p$ in eq. (2) | Output voltage in terms of dimensions | | 5 | $S_V = \dfrac{V_o}{F} = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t}$ | Voltage sensitivity (V/N) | Sensor figure of merit | | 6 | $S_Q = \dfrac{Q}{F} = d$ | Charge sensitivity (C/N) | Sensor figure of merit | | 7 | $g = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$ | $g$ = voltage coefficient (V·m/N) | Material voltage coefficient | | 8 | $\tau = R_L \cdot C_p$ | $\tau$ = time constant, $R_L$ = load resistance | Low-frequency response limit | | 9 | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = stiffness, $m$ = seismic mass | Resonant frequency (accel. mode) | | 10 | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_L C_p}$ | Low-cutoff frequency | Lower edge of measurement band | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Vibration monitoring in turbine blades and helicopter gearboxes. - **Medical:** Ultrasound imaging probes (transmit–receive mode), blood-pressure sensors, hearing aids. - **Automotive:** Knock sensors in IC engines, airbag accelerometers, tyre pressure monitoring. - **Consumer Electronics:** Microphones, piezo buzzers, touch-sensitive screens, quartz wristwatch timing. - **Industrial NDT:** Ultrasonic flaw detection, thickness gauging, sonar systems. - **Precision Metrology:** Atomic force microscopy (AFM) cantilever deflection sensing. > [!NOTE] > **Why PZT dominates industry:** It has a piezoelectric coefficient $d_{33}$ approximately **100× larger** than natural quartz (≈ 300 pC/N vs. 2.3 pC/N), giving vastly superior signal levels at the cost of slightly lower temperature stability. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I — Output Voltage of a Piezoelectric Transducer Under Purely Mechanical Loading **Statement:** Derive the open-circuit output voltage $V_o$ of a piezoelectric crystal when an axial force $F$ is applied through a pair of electrodes of area $A$ separated by crystal thickness $t$. **Starting Point — Direct Piezoelectric Effect (in 33-mode):** The charge generated on the crystal face is given by the fundamental constitutive relation: $$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$ The charge produces a voltage across the crystal, which acts as a parallel-plate capacitor with capacitance: $$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$ The output voltage is charge divided by capacitance: $$V_o = \frac{Q}{C_p}$$ Substituting the expression for $Q$: $$V_o = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_p}$$ Substituting the expression for $C_p$: $$V_o = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$ Expressed as a sensitivity (voltage per unit force): $$S_V = \frac{V_o}{F} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$ **Engineering Insight:** Sensitivity scales **linearly with thickness $t$** and **inversely with electrode area $A$**. This is why thin, large-area crystals are used for low-pressure (high-sensitivity) measurements. ### 3.2 Derivation II — Voltage Sensitivity in Terms of the g-Coefficient **Statement:** Show that the voltage sensitivity of a piezoelectric crystal is directly equal to its voltage coefficient $g_{33}$. By definition, the voltage coefficient is: $$g_{33} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon^T} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ From Derivation I, the voltage sensitivity is: $$S_V = \frac{V_o}{F} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$ But the applied stress is: $$T = \frac{F}{A}$$ Substituting $F = T \cdot A$ into the sensitivity: $$S_V = \frac{d_{33} \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \cdot \frac{t}{A} \cdot A \cdot \frac{1}{A}$$ Wait — let us reorganize cleanly. The electric field developed is $E = V_o / t$, and the stress is $T = F/A$. The voltage coefficient is, by definition: $$g_{33} = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{V_o/t}{F/A} = \frac{V_o \cdot A}{t \cdot F} = \frac{S_V \cdot A}{t}$$ But $S_V = V_o / F$, and for a unit area $A = 1$ m² and unit thickness $t = 1$ m, the geometric factors reduce to: $$g_{33} = \left. \frac{V_o}{F} \right|_{A=1, t=1} = S_V$$ **Conclusion:** The voltage coefficient $g_{33}$ (V·m/N) and the voltage sensitivity $S_V$ (V/N) are dimensionally and numerically related by the crystal's geometric aspect ratio. **For comparison of materials independent of geometry, $g_{33}$ is the intrinsic figure of merit.** ### 3.3 Derivation III — Frequency Response and the High-Pass Cutoff **Statement:** Show that a piezoelectric transducer behaves as a high-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_L = 1 / (2\pi R_L C_p)$. **Equivalent Circuit Model:** The transducer is modelled as a charge source $Q$ in parallel with its own capacitance $C_p$, with the load resistance $R_L$ connected across the output terminals.
