Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at the output node, the charge distributes between $C_p$ and $R_L$ as: $$Q = Q_{C_p} + Q_{R_L}$$ In the frequency domain, the current through $R_L$ is: $$i_{R_L} = \frac{V_o}{R_L}$$ The current through $C_p$ is: $$i_{C_p} = j\omega C_p V_o$$ KCL yields: $$j\omega Q = j\omega C_p V_o + \frac{V_o}{R_L}$$ Solving for $V_o$: $$V_o = \frac{j\omega Q}{j\omega C_p + 1/R_L} = \frac{j\omega R_L Q}{1 + j\omega R_L C_p}$$ The magnitude of the transfer function is: $$\vert V_o \vert = \frac{\omega R_L Q}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_L C_p)^2}}$$ **Two Limiting Cases:** **Case A — Low Frequency ($\omega R_L C_p \ll 1$):** $$\vert V_o \vert \approx \omega R_L Q$$ Output rises linearly with frequency — the signal is heavily attenuated at very low frequencies (DC is completely rejected). **Case B — High Frequency ($\omega R_L C_p \gg 1$):** $$\vert V_o \vert \approx \frac{Q}{C_p}$$ Output saturates to its open-circuit DC value, independent of further frequency increase. **The -3 dB Cutoff Frequency:** Setting $\omega R_L C_p = 1$: $$2\pi f_L R_L C_p = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_L C_p}$$ This cutoff sets the **lower limit of the measurement bandwidth** — a critical KTU design parameter. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Consequence:** To extend low-frequency response, designers either increase $R_L$ (use FET-input charge amplifiers with $R_L > 10^{12}\,\Omega$) or increase $C_p$ (large-area crystals). This is why **charge-mode amplifiers are universally preferred over voltage-mode amplifiers** for low-frequency vibration measurement. ### 3.4 Derivation IV — Resonance Frequency of a Piezoelectric Accelerometer **Statement:** Derive the natural frequency of a mass-spring system formed by the seismic mass $m$ and the piezoelectric crystal stiffness $k$. The differential equation for a single-degree-of-freedom system with mass $m$, stiffness $k$, and negligible damping is: $$m \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + k x = F(t)$$ For free undamped vibration, $F(t) = 0$, and the natural (angular) frequency is: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ Converting to Hertz: $$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ **Operating range rule:** A piezoelectric accelerometer is usable from approximately $f_n / 5$ (lower limit) up to roughly $f_n / 3$ for $\pm 5\%$ linearity, or up to $f_n / 2$ for $\pm 10\%$ linearity. This is the **KTU-recommended operating rule** for seismic-mass transducer design. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Piezoelectric Transducer Signal Conditioning Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Mechanical Input
Force / Pressure / Vibration] --> B{Piezoelectric
Crystal Element
Quartz / PZT / PVDF} B -- d sub 33 coefficient --> C[Charge Generation
Q = d33 times F] C --> D[Equivalent Charge Source
Q in parallel with Cp] D --> E[Signal Cable
Shielded Coaxial] E --> F[Charge Amplifier
High input impedance Op-Amp
with feedback Cf and Rf] F --> G[Voltage Output Stage
Vout = minus Q divided by Cf] G --> H[Display or Data Acquisition
Oscilloscope / DAQ / Recorder] F --> I[High-Pass Filter
fc = 1 divided by 2 pi Rf Cf] I --> H style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#fff4e1 style D fill:#e8f5e9 style F fill:#fce4ec style H fill:#f3e5f5 ``` ### 4.2 Crystal Cut and Poling Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw PZT Ceramic Disc
Centrosymmetric at Rest] --> B[Poling Process
Apply strong DC field
~3 to 5 kV/mm
at 100 to 150 degree C] B --> C[Poled Piezoelectric Disc
Non-centrosymmetric
Permanent dipole alignment
along 3-axis] C --> D1[Electroded Faces
Top and bottom silver electrodes
Area A, thickness t] C --> D2[Polarity Convention
3-axis positive direction
defines d33 sign] D1 --> E[Operating Modes] D2 --> E E --> F1[33 Mode Longitudinal
Force and charge along 3-axis] E --> F2[31 Mode Transverse
Force along 1, charge along 3] E --> F3[15 Mode Shear
Tangential stress response] style A fill:#ffebee style B fill:#fff8e1 style C fill:#e8f5e9 style E fill:#e3f2fd ``` ### 4.3 Equivalent Circuit and Frequency Response Block ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Force F applied to crystal] --> B[Charge Source Q
Q = d times F] B --> C[Parallel Capacitance Cp
Cp = epsilon 0 epsilon r A over t] C --> D[Load Resistor RL
Determines low cutoff] D --> E[Output Node
Vo across RL] E --> F[Frequency Response Analysis] F --> G1[Low Frequency Band
omega RL Cp less than 1
Vo proportional to omega] F --> G2[Flat Band
omega RL Cp greater than 1
Vo equals Q over Cp] F --> G3[Resonance Band
Near fn = 1 over 2 pi times sqrt k over m
Mechanical amplification occurs] G1 --> H[Combined Bode Plot
High-pass with mechanical resonance peak] G2 --> H G3 --> H style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style H fill:#f3e5f5 ``` ### 4.4 Comparative Selection Flowchart — Material Choice Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Select Piezoelectric Material] --> B{Operating Temperature
Requirement} B -- Above 300 degree C --> C[Use Quartz
or Lithium Niobate
High Curie temperature] B -- Below 300 degree C --> D{Required Sensitivity} D -- Very High --> E[Use PZT Ceramic
d33 approx 300 to 600 pC/N] D -- Moderate --> F{Form Factor Need} F -- Rigid Bulk --> G[Use PZT or BaTiO3 Disc] F -- Flexible Film --> H[Use PVDF Polymer
d33 approx 20 to 30 pC/N] C --> I[Validate with Frequency
and Stability Constraints] E --> I G --> I H --> I style A fill:#e1f5ff style I fill:#e8f5e9 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]`** **Define the direct and converse piezoelectric effects. State two examples of natural and two examples of synthetic piezoelectric materials.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** When a mechanical stress is applied to certain non-centrosymmetric crystals, an electrical charge is generated on the crystal faces. Mathematically, $Q = d \cdot F$. - **Converse Piezoelectric Effect:** When an electric field is applied across a piezoelectric crystal, the crystal undergoes a mechanical deformation proportional to the field. Mathematically, $S = d_t \cdot E$. - **Natural examples:** Quartz (SiO₂), Tourmaline, Rochelle Salt. - **Synthetic examples:** PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate), Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃), PVDF polymer. - **Key fact (1 mark):** The same coefficient $d$ governs both effects, linked by Maxwell's reciprocity relations. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]`** **What is the significance of the Curie temperature for a piezoelectric material? What happens when a piezoelectric crystal is heated above this temperature?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - The **Curie temperature ($T_c$)** is the critical temperature above which the piezoelectric crystal loses its non-centrosymmetric domain structure and becomes a **centrosymmetric paraelectric phase**. - Above $T_c$, the material **completely loses its piezoelectric properties** because the spontaneous polarization that gives rise to the dipole structure vanishes. - For PZT, $T_c \approx 350^\circ\text{C}$; for quartz, $T_c = 573^\circ\text{C}$. - **Engineering implication:** This is why PZT-based sensors are derated for use above ~200 °C, while quartz sensors (e.g., in downhole oil drilling) can be used at much higher temperatures. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`** **(a)** Derive the expression for the open-circuit output voltage of a piezoelectric crystal transducer in terms of the applied force, the piezoelectric coefficient, the electrode area, the crystal thickness, and the permittivity. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A quartz piezoelectric crystal has the following specifications: - Piezoelectric coefficient: $d_{33} = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N - Relative permittivity: $\varepsilon_r = 4.6$ - Electrode area: $A = 100 \text{ mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m² - Crystal thickness: $t = 1$ mm $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m Calculate: (i) The capacitance of the crystal (ii) The charge generated when a force of 10 N is applied (iii) The output voltage developed (iv) The voltage coefficient $g_{33}$ **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Derivation of Output Voltage:** The fundamental equation for the direct piezoelectric effect in the 33-mode is: $$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$ The crystal acts as a parallel-plate capacitor with capacitance: $$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$ The output voltage is the charge divided by the capacitance: $$V_o = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_p} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$ This is the **final derived expression** for the open-circuit output voltage. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step (i) — Capacitance:** $$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(4.6)(100 \times 10^{-6})}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$C_p = \frac{8.854 \times 4.6 \times 100 \times 10^{-18}}{10^{-3}} = \frac{4072.84 \times 10^{-18}}{10^{-3}} = 4072.84 \times 10^{-15}$$ $$\boxed{C_p \approx 4.07 \text{ pF}}$$ **Step (ii) — Charge Generated:** $$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = (2.3 \times 10^{-12})(10) = 23 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 23 \text{ pC}}$$ **Step (iii) — Output Voltage:** $$V_o = \frac{Q}{C_p} = \frac{23 \times 10^{-12}}{4.07 \times 10^{-12}} = 5.65 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{V_o \approx 5.65 \text{ V}}$$ **Step (iv) — Voltage Coefficient:** $$g_{33} = \frac{d_{33}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{-12}}{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(4.6)}$$ $$g_{33} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{-12}}{40.73 \times 10^{-12}} = 0.0565 \text{ V·m/N}$$ $$\boxed{g_{33} \approx 0.0565 \text{ V·m/N}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`** **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a piezoelectric accelerometer. Derive the expression for its natural frequency of vibration. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Explain why piezoelectric transducers are not suitable for static measurements. Discuss how the lower cutoff frequency can be improved using a charge amplifier configuration. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Working of Piezoelectric Accelerometer:** A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of a **piezoelectric crystal** (typically PZT or quartz) sandwiched between a **seismic mass $m$** and the **transducer base**. The whole assembly is mounted on the vibrating body whose acceleration is to be measured. **Working Principle:** 1. When the body to which the accelerometer is attached vibrates, the base of the accelerometer experiences an acceleration $a$. 2. The seismic mass $m$ tends to remain at rest (inertia) and therefore exerts a force $F = ma$ on the piezoelectric crystal. 3. The crystal generates a charge $Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a$, which is proportional to the acceleration of the body. 4. This charge is conditioned by a charge amplifier to produce an output voltage $V_o = -Q/C_f$, where $C_f$ is the feedback capacitance. **Derivation of Natural Frequency:** The accelerometer is a spring-mass-damper system with stiffness $k$ of the crystal and seismic mass $m$. The free-vibration equation is: $$m \ddot{x} + k x = 0$$ The natural angular frequency is: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ The natural frequency in Hertz is: $$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ **Part (b) — Why Piezoelectric Transducers Reject Static Signals:** From the frequency response derivation in Section 3.3, the output voltage at low frequencies is: $$\vert V_o \vert = \omega R_L Q \quad \text{(for } \omega R_L C_p \ll 1\text{)}$$ At **DC ($\omega = 0$)**, the output voltage is **exactly zero**. This is a fundamental physical limitation because the piezoelectric effect is fundamentally an **AC phenomenon** — the charge leaks away through the load resistance $R_L$ at low frequencies. The time constant $\tau = R_L C_p$ determines how long the charge persists. **Charge Amplifier Solution:** A charge amplifier uses an op-amp with the piezoelectric crystal connected between the inverting input and ground, with a feedback capacitor $C_f$ and feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel from output to inverting input. The op-amp virtual-ground property forces the charge from the crystal to flow entirely through $C_f$, producing an output: $$V_o = -\frac{Q}{C_f}$$ The effective lower cutoff frequency becomes: $$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$$ By choosing $R_f$ as large as $10^{12}$ to $10^{14}$ ohms (FET-input op-amp) and $C_f$ appropriately, $f_L$ can be reduced to **sub-Hz values**, extending the measurement bandwidth down to nearly DC. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse the **direct** and **converse** piezoelectric effects and swap their definitions — **deduct 1 mark**. > - **Common Mistake 2:** In numerical problems, students forget to **convert mm² to m² and mm to m** before substituting into $C_p = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/t$. Unit mismatch errors cost **up to 2 marks**. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $V_o = Q/C$ without specifying **which capacitance** ($C_p$ of the crystal vs. $C_f$ of the amplifier) — always clarify. **-1 mark**. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting that **piezoelectricity vanishes above the Curie temperature** in long-answer material discussions — examiners test this in 14-mark answers. **-1 mark**. > - **Common Mistake 5:** Not stating that **quartz is preferred for high-temperature / high-stability applications** while PZT is preferred for **high-sensitivity room-temperature applications**. This comparison carries **2 marks** in long-answer questions. > - **Common Mistake 6:** Conflating the **voltage coefficient $g$** (V·m/N) with the **charge coefficient $d$** (C/N) — they are related by $g = d/\varepsilon$, not equal. **-1 mark**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Block — Print This and Review Night Before Exam:** - **Piezoelectric Effect:** Mechanical stress → electric charge (**Direct**); Electric field → mechanical strain (**Converse**). Discovered 1880 by the Curie brothers. - **Origin:** Non-centrosymmetric crystal lattice; centres of +ve and -ve charge do not coincide. - **20** of the 32 crystal classes are piezoelectric; **10** of those are also pyroelectric. - **Quartz (SiO₂):** $d_{33} \approx 2.3$ pC/N, $T_c = 573°$C, natural, highly stable, used in oscillators. - **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate):** $d_{33} \approx 300$–$600$ pC/N, $T_c \approx 350°$C, synthetic ceramic, **industry standard**. - **PVDF:** Flexible polymer film, used in wearable sensors. - **Fundamental Relations:** - $Q = d_{33} F$ - $C_p = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / t$ - $V_o = d_{33} F t / (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A)$ - $g_{33} = d_{33} / (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r)$ - $f_L = 1 / (2\pi R_L C_p)$ - $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ - **Static Measurement Limitation:** Output is zero at DC due to charge leakage through $R_L$. **Charge amplifier** with $R_f \sim 10^{12}\,\Omega$ extends $f_L$ to sub-Hz. - **Equivalent Circuit:** Charge source $Q$ in parallel with $C_p$, terminated by load $R_L$. - **Modes:** $d_{33}$ (longitudinal), $d_{31}$ (transverse), $d_{15}$ (shear) — know all three with one application each. - **Reciprocity:** The same coefficient $d$ governs both direct and converse effects (Maxwell's relations). - **Curie Temperature:** Above $T_c$, crystal becomes centrosymmetric (paraelectric) — piezoelectricity **permanently lost** for that cycle. - **Why high output voltage with low force:** Thin crystals (small $t$) and high $d$ (PZT) maximize $V_o$. - **Operating bandwidth rule:** Accelerometers usable from $f_n/5$ to $f_n/3$ for $\pm 5\%$ accuracy. - **Top KTU 2024 Buzzwords to Memorize:** Non-centrosymmetric, dipolar, Curie temperature, charge coefficient, voltage coefficient, charge amplifier, seismic mass, reciprocity, poling, PZT, PVDF. - **Diagrams to Practice Free-Hand:** Crystal orientation with 1-2-3 axes; equivalent circuit; accelerometer cross-section showing base, crystal, seismic mass, and output lead.

topics.title: Potentiometric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Potentiometric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Potentiometric Transducer** (also called a **resistive potentiometer** or simply a **pot**) is a passive, variable-resistance electromechanical transducer that converts **linear displacement** (Linear Potentiometer) or **angular displacement** (Rotary Potentiometer) into a proportional **electrical voltage signal**. It functions as a voltage divider whose tapping point is mechanically coupled to the measurand.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, it belongs to the family of **Resistive Transducers** under the **Variable Resistance Principle**, where the output voltage is derived from the wiper position on a uniformly wound or coated resistive element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OEAET611 - Module 3):**
> Potentiometric transducers are a fundamental building block for displacement measurement. The KTU board frequently tests the **loading effect**, **sensitivity**, and **linearity** of these devices.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **volume control knob** on an old analog radio or a **fader slider** on a music mixer.

1. When you rotate or slide the knob, an internal wiper (a movable contact) slides along a strip of resistive material.
2. The further you push the slider, the more resistance is "included" on one side and "excluded" on the other.
3. The output voltage at the wiper is simply a fraction of the total applied voltage — a fraction determined by the slider's position.

That is precisely a potentiometric transducer. The only difference in engineering applications is that the slider is mechanically attached to the object whose displacement (push, pull, rotate, expand, contract) we wish to measure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Ready):**
> A potentiometric transducer is a three-terminal variable voltage divider used for the measurement of displacement, in which a movable wiper taps a fraction of the total applied excitation voltage corresponding to the physical displacement of the measurand.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Excitation Voltage ($V_x$ or $E$)**: Typically **DC 5 V to 24 V** in industrial applications.
- **Total Resistance ($R$)**: Ranges from **100 Ω to 100 kΩ**.
- **Resolution**: Defined by the finest detectable change in resistance (governed by wire pitch or conductive coating grain).
- **Linearity**: Usually expressed as a percentage of **Full-Scale Deflection (FSD)** — typical value **±0.1% to ±1%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A potentiometric transducer is built around a **uniform resistance element** of total value $R$, with a fixed excitation $V_x$ applied across the two extreme terminals. A sliding contact (wiper) taps a third terminal.

1. **Wiper Position ($x$)**: Defined as a dimensionless fraction of the total displacement (or total resistance), ranging from **0 to 1**.
2. **Resistance Tapping**: The wiper splits $R$ into two parts:
   - $R_1 = (1 - x)\,R$  (from one fixed end to wiper)
   - $R_2 = x\,R$        (from wiper to the other fixed end)
3. **Voltage Divider Action**: Under no-load conditions, the wiper voltage is governed purely by the resistive ratio.
4. **Signal Extraction**: The output voltage $V_o$ at the wiper terminals is the measurand-encoded electrical signal.

## 2.2 Types of Potentiometric Transducers

| **Type** | **Measurand** | **Typical Range** | **Construction** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear (Translational) Potentiometer** | Linear displacement | 2 mm to 500 mm | Rectangular resistive track; slider moves in a straight line |
| **Rotary (Angular) Potentiometer** | Angular displacement | 0° to 360° (single turn) or 3600° (multi-turn helical) | Circular resistive track; wiper rotates on a shaft |
| **Wire-Wound Potentiometer** | High-precision displacement | Custom | Insulated former wound with resistance wire |
| **Conductive Plastic / Cermet** | General-purpose | Custom | Plastic or ceramic substrate with resistive coating |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Potentiometric"?** The name stems from the *potential (voltage)* developed at the wiper, which is a *metric* of the displacement. Hence, *Potentio* (potential) + *metric* (measure).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Description** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Conditions / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| **Open-circuit output voltage** | $V_o = V_x \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_x \cdot x$ | No load connected; ideal linear behavior |
| **Resistance tap (lower segment)** | $R_2 = x \cdot R$ | $x \in [0, 1]$ is fractional wiper position |
| **Resistance tap (upper segment)** | $R_1 = (1 - x) \cdot R$ | Complementary to $R_2$ |
| **Loaded output voltage** | $V_o = \dfrac{V_x \cdot x}{1 + K \cdot x(1 - x)}$ | $K = \dfrac{R}{R_L}$; **LOADING EFFECT** |
| **Loading error** | $e_L = \dfrac{V_x \cdot K \cdot x^2(1 - x)}{1 + K \cdot x(1 - x)}$ | Difference between loaded and unloaded output |
| **Sensitivity (No Load)** | $S = \dfrac{dV_o}{dx} = V_x$ | Constant sensitivity; perfectly linear |
| **Sensitivity (Loaded)** | $S = \dfrac{V_x\,[1 + K x^2]}{[1 + K x(1 - x)]^2}$ | **Non-linear**; varies with $x$ |
| **Maximum loaded sensitivity (at $x = 0.5$)** | $S_{max} = \dfrac{V_x}{1 + \dfrac{K}{4}}$ | Worst-case sensitivity point |
| **Resolution (Wire-Wound)** | $R_e = \dfrac{R}{N} \times 100\%$ | $N$ = total number of turns; typical **0.1% to 1%** |
| **Power rating** | $P = \dfrac{V_x^2}{R}$ | **W**atts; thermal limit of resistance element |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Potentiometric transducers are deployed in **joysticks, throttle position sensors, fuel-level gauges, joystick controls in CNC machines, robotic arm feedback, hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder position sensing**, and as the feedback element in **servo systems**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Open-Circuit Output Voltage

Let the total resistance of the potentiometer track be $R$, the excitation voltage applied across the two fixed ends be $V_x$, and the wiper position fraction be $x$.

**Step 1:** The resistance $R$ is split by the wiper into two series components:

$$R_1 = (1 - x) \cdot R$$

$$R_2 = x \cdot R$$

**Step 2:** These two resistances act as a series voltage divider. The total resistance is $R_1 + R_2 = R$, and the same current $I$ flows through both segments:

$$I = \frac{V_x}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{V_x}{R}$$

**Step 3:** The voltage at the wiper terminal (taken across $R_2$, from the wiper to the grounded lower end) is:

$$V_o = I \cdot R_2 = \frac{V_x}{R} \cdot (x \cdot R) = V_x \cdot x$$

This is the famous **linear transfer characteristic** of an ideal potentiometer — output is a *purely linear* function of the wiper position.

## 3.2 Derivation: Loaded Output Voltage (The Loading Effect)

In practice, a measuring instrument, amplifier, or ADC of finite input impedance $R_L$ is connected across the wiper terminal and one fixed end. This forms a **parallel combination** that disturbs the voltage divider.

**Step 1:** The lower segment $R_2$ is now in parallel with the load $R_L$:

$$R_2' = R_2 \parallel R_L = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{x R \cdot R_L}{x R + R_L}$$

**Step 2:** Factor $R_L$ from the numerator and denominator by dividing top and bottom by $R_L$:

$$R_2' = \frac{x R}{1 + \dfrac{x R}{R_L}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the voltage divider equation. The total circuit resistance is now $R_1 + R_2'$:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1 + R_2'}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $R_1 = (1 - x)R$ and $R_2'$ from Step 2:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \frac{\dfrac{xR}{1 + xR/R_L}}{(1 - x)R + \dfrac{xR}{1 + xR/R_L}}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by the common factor $(1 + xR/R_L)$:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \frac{xR}{(1 - x)R \left(1 + \dfrac{xR}{R_L}\right) + xR}$$

**Step 6:** Divide both numerator and denominator by $R$:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \frac{x}{(1 - x)\left(1 + \dfrac{xR}{R_L}\right) + x}$$

**Step 7:** Expand the denominator:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \frac{x}{1 - x + \dfrac{x(1 - x)R}{R_L} + x} = V_x \cdot \frac{x}{1 + K \cdot x(1 - x)}$$

where $K = \dfrac{R}{R_L}$ is the **loading ratio** (a dimensionless number, typically $K \ll 1$ for good measurement practice).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result (Board-Ready):**
> $$\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{V_x \cdot x}{1 + K \cdot x(1 - x)}\,}$$
> This is the **KTU gold-standard equation** for the loaded potentiometer output.

## 3.3 Derivation: Sensitivity Under Load

Sensitivity $S$ is defined as the rate of change of output voltage with respect to wiper position:

$$S = \frac{dV_o}{dx}$$

**Step 1:** Rewrite the loaded output equation for clarity:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \left[\frac{x}{1 + Kx(1 - x)}\right]$$

**Step 2:** Apply the quotient rule: $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right) = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$

- Numerator function: $u = x \Rightarrow u' = 1$
- Denominator function: $v = 1 + Kx(1 - x) = 1 + Kx - Kx^2 \Rightarrow v' = K - 2Kx = K(1 - 2x)$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the quotient rule:

$$S = V_x \cdot \frac{(1)\left[1 + Kx(1 - x)\right] - x \cdot K(1 - 2x)}{\left[1 + Kx(1 - x)\right]^2}$$

**Step 4:** Expand the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 1 + Kx - Kx^2 - Kx + 2Kx^2 = 1 + Kx^2$$

**Step 5:** The sensitivity under load is therefore:

$$\boxed{\,S = \frac{V_x \cdot \left(1 + Kx^2\right)}{\left[1 + Kx(1 - x)\right]^2}\,}$$

**Step 6:** At the mid-position $x = 0.5$:

$$S_{max} = \frac{V_x \cdot (1 + K/4)}{(1 + K/4)^2} = \frac{V_x}{1 + K/4}$$

This proves that **sensitivity drops below the no-load value $V_x$** as loading increases.

## 3.4 Loading Error — Maximum at $x = 0.5$

The error $e_L$ between loaded and unloaded output is:

$$e_L = V_x \cdot x - V_o = V_x \cdot x \left[1 - \frac{1}{1 + Kx(1 - x)}\right] = \frac{V_x \cdot K \cdot x^2(1 - x)}{1 + Kx(1 - x)}$$

Differentiating with respect to $x$ and setting to zero gives the maximum error at $x \approx 0.5$ for small $K$, which is why the **mid-range position is the most non-linear** in a loaded potentiometer.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A linear potentiometer of total resistance **$R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$** is excited by **$V_x = 10\ \text{V}$**. The wiper is at **$x = 0.6$**. A measuring instrument of input resistance **$R_L = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$** is connected across the lower segment. Calculate the (a) open-circuit output, (b) loaded output, and (c) loading error.

**Given:** $R = 10,000\ \Omega,\ V_x = 10\ \text{V},\ x = 0.6,\ R_L = 100,000\ \Omega$

**Loading ratio:**

$$K = \frac{R}{R_L} = \frac{10{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.1$$

**(a) Open-circuit output:**

$$V_o = V_x \cdot x = 10 \times 0.6 = 6.0\ \text{V}$$

**(b) Loaded output:**

$$V_o = \frac{10 \times 0.6}{1 + 0.1 \times 0.6 \times (1 - 0.6)} = \frac{6.0}{1 + 0.1 \times 0.6 \times 0.4} = \frac{6.0}{1.024} = 5.859\ \text{V}$$

**(c) Loading error:**

$$e_L = 6.0 - 5.859 = 0.141\ \text{V} \quad \text{or} \quad 2.35\%\ \text{of FSD}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The loading error is small here (2.35%) because $R_L \gg R$. For high accuracy, the rule of thumb is **$R_L \geq 10R$**, ensuring $K \leq 0.1$ and error stays below ~2.5%.

## 3.6 Advantages, Disadvantages, and Limitations Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Detail** |
|---|---|
| **Advantages** | Simple, inexpensive, inherently linear (no load), good for DC and low-frequency AC, provides *large* output without amplification, both linear and angular measurement |
| **Disadvantages** | Requires large displacement force to overcome wiper friction, contact wear and noise, finite resolution, loading error, limited bandwidth due to slider inertia |
| **Resolution limit** | Wire-wound type: limited by wire pitch; Conductive plastic: virtually infinite (smooth film) |
| **Frequency response** | Low — typically below **10 Hz** for mechanical pot; **few kHz** for precision film types |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Potentiometer Circuit Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Excitation Source Vx] -->|Terminal A| B[Resistive Track R]
    B -->|Terminal B| C[Reference Ground 0V]
    D[Mechanical Input: Displacement or Angle] -->|Coupled Shaft or Slider| E[Wiper / Sliding Contact]
    E -->|Taps fraction x| B
    E -->|Output terminal| F[Load Resistance RL - Voltmeter or Amplifier]
    F -->|Returns to Terminal B| C
    G[Output Voltage Vo proportional to x] --> F
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style E fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Loading Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IDEAL["Ideal Potentiometer: No Load"]
        A1[Total Resistance R] -->|R1 equals 1-x times R| B1[Upper Segment R1]
        A1 -->|R2 equals x times R| C1[Lower Segment R2]
        C1 --> D1[Open-Circuit Vo equals Vx times x]
    end

    subgraph PRACTICAL["Practical Potentiometer: With Load RL"]
        A2[Total Resistance R] -->|R1 equals 1-x times R| B2[Upper Segment R1]
        A2 -->|R2 equals x times R| C2[Lower Segment R2]
        C2 -.->|Parallel combination| P2[Effective R2 dash = R2 || RL]
        P2 --> D2[Loaded Vo equals Vx times x divided by 1 plus Kx(1-x)]
    end

    style IDEAL fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style PRACTICAL fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style D1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#ffab91,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Resolution Model of a Wire-Wound Potentiometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wire-Wound Track] --> B[Total N Turns Wound Uniformly]
    B --> C[Each Turn adds delta R = R / N]
    C --> D[Wiper moves from one turn to the next]
    D --> E[Smallest Detectable Step equals delta R]
    E --> F[Resolution percent = 1 / N times 100]
    style A fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a potentiometric transducer. With a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a linear potentiometer used for displacement measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

A potentiometric transducer is a passive, variable-resistance device that converts mechanical displacement (linear or angular) into a proportional output voltage using the voltage-divider principle. It consists of a uniform resistive element of total resistance $R$ across which an excitation voltage $V_x$ is applied. A sliding wiper, mechanically coupled to the measurand, taps a fraction $x$ of the total resistance, producing an output voltage:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot x$$

As the wiper moves, $V_o$ varies linearly with displacement, enabling direct electrical measurement of the mechanical input. The device acts as a passive transducer requiring no external power for signal generation (other than the excitation $V_x$).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any four advantages and two disadvantages of potentiometric transducers.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Simple construction and low cost.
2. Inherently linear output under no-load conditions.
3. Provides a large output voltage, often eliminating the need for amplification.
4. Suitable for both DC and low-frequency AC excitation.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Loading effect causes non-linearity when finite-impedance instruments are connected.
2. Wiper contact produces friction, wear, and electrical noise over time, limiting life and accuracy.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a linear potentiometric transducer **under loaded conditions**, clearly defining all variables used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear potentiometer of total resistance **$R = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$** is excited by **$V_x = 12\ \text{V}$**. The wiper is positioned at **$x = 0.4$**, and a voltmeter of internal resistance **$R_L = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$** is connected across the output. Calculate:
   1. The output voltage under loaded conditions. **(2 marks)**
   2. The loading error as a percentage of full-scale deflection. **(2 marks)**
   3. The sensitivity of the loaded transducer at the mid-position. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Let $R$ be the total track resistance, $V_x$ the excitation, and $x$ the fractional wiper position. The wiper splits the track into $R_1 = (1 - x)R$ and $R_2 = xR$. Under no load, the open-circuit output is:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_x \cdot x$$

When a load $R_L$ is connected across $R_2$, the effective lower segment becomes:

$$R_2' = \frac{R_2 R_L}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{xR \cdot R_L}{xR + R_L}$$

Applying the voltage-divider rule:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1 + R_2'}$$

Substituting and simplifying (defining $K = R/R_L$):

$$\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{V_x \cdot x}{1 + K \cdot x(1 - x)}\,}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 5000\ \Omega,\ V_x = 12\ \text{V},\ x = 0.4,\ R_L = 50{,}000\ \Omega$

**Loading ratio:**

$$K = \frac{R}{R_L} = \frac{5000}{50{,}000} = 0.1$$

**(i) Loaded output voltage:**

$$V_o = \frac{12 \times 0.4}{1 + 0.1 \times 0.4 \times 0.6} = \frac{4.8}{1.024} = 4.6875\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Loading error:**

$$V_{o(\text{no load})} = 12 \times 0.4 = 4.8\ \text{V}$$

$$e_L = 4.8 - 4.6875 = 0.1125\ \text{V}$$

$$\%e_L = \frac{0.1125}{12} \times 100 = 0.9375\%\ \text{of FSD}$$

**(iii) Sensitivity at mid-position ($x = 0.5$):**

$$S_{max} = \frac{V_x}{1 + K/4} = \frac{12}{1 + 0.1/4} = \frac{12}{1.025} = 11.707\ \text{V/unit}$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **rotary potentiometric transducer**. Compare it briefly with a linear potentiometer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **loading effect** in potentiometric transducers with necessary equations. How does the loading ratio $K = R/R_L$ affect the linearity of the output? What is the recommended design practice to minimize it? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Rotary Potentiometer — Construction and Working:**

A rotary potentiometer consists of a **circular resistive track** (either wire-wound or conductive plastic) mounted on an insulating former. A wiper is fixed to a rotating shaft, which is mechanically coupled to the measurand producing angular displacement. The two ends of the track are connected to the excitation voltage $V_x$, and the wiper terminal provides the output voltage proportional to the angular position $\theta$.

The transfer function is:

$$V_o = V_x \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{FS}}$$

where $\theta_{FS}$ is the full-scale angular range (e.g., 270°, 360°, or 3600° for multi-turn helical types).

**Comparison:**

| **Feature** | **Linear Pot** | **Rotary Pot** |
|---|---|---|
| Measurand | Linear displacement | Angular displacement |
| Track shape | Straight | Circular |
| Typical range | 2–500 mm | 0°–3600° |
| Common use | Hydraulic pistons, fuel gauges | Joysticks, throttle, servo feedback |

**(b) Loading Effect — Detailed Discussion:**

When a finite-impedance measuring device $R_L$ is connected across the output of a potentiometer, it forms a parallel combination with the lower resistance segment $R_2$. This reduces the effective resistance of that segment, disturbing the voltage-divider balance and causing the output to deviate from the ideal linear relation $V_o = V_x \cdot x$.

The loaded output equation is:

$$V_o = \frac{V_x \cdot x}{1 + K \cdot x(1 - x)}, \quad \text{where } K = \frac{R}{R_L}$$

**Effect of $K$ on linearity:**

- For $K = 0$ (i.e., $R_L \to \infty$, infinite-impedance instrument), the equation reduces to $V_o = V_x \cdot x$ — perfectly linear.
- As $K$ increases, the denominator $1 + Kx(1-x)$ becomes position-dependent, introducing **non-linearity**.
- The **maximum deviation** occurs near $x = 0.5$, where $x(1-x)$ is maximum (= 0.25).
- The sensitivity also drops below the no-load value $V_x$, reaching its minimum at mid-position.

**Recommended design practice:**

To minimize loading error, the load resistance should be **at least 10 to 100 times larger** than the total track resistance ($R_L \geq 10R$ to $100R$), ensuring $K \leq 0.1$ and keeping the non-linearity error below **2.5%**. For high-precision applications, a **buffer amplifier (voltage follower)** with input impedance $R_L > 10^{10}\ \Omega$ is connected between the wiper and the measuring instrument, effectively eliminating the loading effect.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to define $K$** as $R/R_L$ before substituting into the loaded equation — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing open-circuit and loaded output** — many students write $V_o = V_x x$ even for the loaded case, losing all 7 marks in part (a).
> 3. **Not simplifying the parallel combination** properly — leaving $R_2'$ in unsimplified form is a partial-credit trap; always reduce to the standard form.
> 4. **Loading error units** — always express as percentage **of full-scale ($V_x$)**, not of the wiper position value itself. A common error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Potentiometric transducer** = variable voltage divider; converts displacement → voltage.
- **Open-circuit output:** $V_o = V_x \cdot x$ — perfectly linear.
- **Loaded output:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_x \cdot x}{1 + K \cdot x(1 - x)}$, with $K = R/R_L$.
- **Loading effect** causes non-linearity; maximum error at **$x = 0.5$**.
- **Sensitivity under load:** $S = \dfrac{V_x(1 + Kx^2)}{[1 + Kx(1-x)]^2}$; at $x = 0.5$, $S_{max} = \dfrac{V_x}{1 + K/4}$.
- **Resolution** of wire-wound pot = $1/N$ (where $N$ = number of turns); better resolution in conductive-plastic types.
- **Design rule:** Always ensure $R_L \geq 10R$ to keep loading error $< 2.5\%$, or use a **buffer amplifier** for precision.
- **Types:** Linear (translation) and Rotary (angular) — both follow identical voltage-divider theory.
- **Frequency response:** Low (mechanical inertia of wiper); not suitable for high-dynamic measurements.
- **KTU-Favorite questions:** (i) Derive loaded output equation, (ii) Calculate loading error and sensitivity numerically, (iii) Compare wire-wound vs. conductive plastic pots.
- **Common trap:** Confusing $K$ with $R_L/R$ — remember $K = R/R_L$ (track resistance over load), always a small number.
- **Excitation:** Use **DC** for resistive pots to avoid reactive effects; AC is permissible for special types.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LVDT Accelerometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LVDT Accelerometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **LVDT Accelerometer** is a vibration-measuring transducer that uses a **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** as the primary displacement-sensing element, coupled mechanically to a **seismic (inertial) mass-spring-damper system**. The relative displacement between the seismic mass and the transducer housing, produced when the accelerometer base is subjected to an external acceleration, is sensed by the LVDT core and converted into a proportional differential output voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** LVDT is classified under *Passive, Inductive, and Analog* transducers. When configured as an accelerometer, it falls under the **Seismic Mass Type / Inertial Type** family of accelerometers, governed by Newton's second law and second-order differential equation dynamics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Definition Reference (IEEE/ISA):** The LVDT is a passive inductive transducer that produces an electrical output proportional to the displacement of a movable magnetic core. Its transfer function is inherently linear over a wide stroke length.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a car (the *seismic mass*) and the car suddenly accelerates. Your body tends to be pushed back against the seat due to inertia. The relative motion between your body and the car seat is what the seat's sensors detect.

In an LVDT accelerometer, this is exactly what happens:
- The **base/housing** of the accelerometer is bolted to the vibrating surface (like the car chassis).
- The **seismic mass** inside the housing is suspended by a **spring** with a damper.
- When vibration occurs, the mass "lags behind" the housing motion due to inertia.
- A thin **rod from the mass** is connected to the **LVDT core** (the moving magnetic slug).
- The LVDT measures this tiny relative displacement and outputs a voltage.

**Plain English Summary:** "Push-pull, the mass moves a little, the LVDT tells us by how much, and from that we calculate how hard we are being accelerated."

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards (Highlighted)

- **Earth's gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \text{ m/s}^2$ (used for calibration)
- **Piezoelectric equivalent:** Although this device is inductive, the displacement output is usually quoted in **mm** or **µm**.
- **Standard LVDT excitation frequency:** Typically **1 kHz to 10 kHz** (carrier frequency, since LVDT is AC-excited).
- **Standard output range:** ±5 V, ±10 V (after phase-sensitive demodulation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *displacement LVDT* measures absolute position. An *LVDT accelerometer* measures *relative displacement* between a mass and a case, which is then mathematically related to the input acceleration.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Carrier Excitation (Primary Coil):**
   The primary winding of the LVDT is energized by a stable, high-frequency **AC excitation voltage** $e_p$ (typically 1–10 kHz sinusoid from an oscillator).

2. **Electromagnetic Coupling:**
   The AC primary voltage produces an alternating magnetic flux in the core. This flux links the **two identical secondary windings**, $S_1$ and $S_2$, which are wound in **opposite phase** (series-opposition connection).

3. **Core Displacement:**
   When the seismic mass experiences acceleration, the LVDT core shifts by a small distance $x$ from the **null (central) position**.

4. **Differential Induced EMF:**
   - If the core moves **upward**, the flux linkage with $S_1$ increases and with $S_2$ decreases.
   - If the core moves **downward**, the reverse happens.
   - The output is the **algebraic difference** of the two secondary EMFs.

5. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulation:**
   The output is a **carrier-modulated AC signal** whose amplitude is proportional to $x$ and whose **phase** (with respect to the carrier) indicates the direction of motion (up or down).

6. **Acceleration Recovery:**
   The demodulated DC voltage is processed through the **transfer function of the mass-spring-damper system** to yield a final output proportional to the applied acceleration $a(t)$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|:---------:|:--------------|:----------:|:------------|:---------:|
| 1 | LVDT Output Voltage (Differential) | $e_o$ | $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | LVDT Sensitivity | $K_{LVDT}$ | $K_{LVDT} = \dfrac{\partial e_o}{\partial x}$ | V/m or V/mm |
| 3 | Natural (Resonant) Frequency | $\omega_n$ | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s |
| 4 | Natural Frequency (Hz) | $f_n$ | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta$ | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Governing Equation of Motion | — | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\,a(t)$ | — |
| 7 | Static Sensitivity (Displacement) | $S_d$ | $S_d = \dfrac{x}{a} = \dfrac{-1}{\omega_n^2}$ (for $\omega \ll \omega_n$) | m/(m/s²) or s² |
| 8 | Acceleration Sensitivity | $S_a$ | $S_a = \dfrac{e_o}{a} = K_{LVDT} \cdot S_d$ | V/(m/s²) |
| 9 | Steady-State Displacement (Sinusoidal) | $x(t)$ | $x(t) = \dfrac{a}{\omega_n^2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | m |
| 10 | Working Frequency Range | $f$ | $f \leq 0.4\,f_n$ (for $\zeta \approx 0.7$) | Hz |

Where:
- $m$ = seismic mass (kg)
- $c$ = damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $k$ = spring stiffness (N/m)
- $x$ = relative displacement of mass w.r.t. case (m)
- $a(t)$ = input acceleration (m/s²)
- $r$ = frequency ratio $= \omega/\omega_n$

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Vibration monitoring of turbine blades, airframes, and rocket payload fairings.
- **Civil Engineering:** Structural health monitoring of bridges, tall buildings, and dams during seismic events.
- **Automotive:** Crash testing, NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) analysis, and suspension tuning.
- **Industrial Machinery:** Predictive maintenance of rotating equipment (motors, pumps, gearboxes).
- **Seismology:** Low-frequency ground motion detection (where LVDT's good DC and low-frequency response outperforms piezoelectric accelerometers).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of LVDT Output Voltage

The LVDT is essentially a transformer with a movable core. Each secondary coil acts as a single transformer with the primary.

**Mutual Inductance Model:**

Let $M_1$ and $M_2$ be the mutual inductances between the primary and the two secondary coils, which depend on the core position $x$.

$$
e_{s1} = j\omega M_1(x) \cdot I_p \quad ; \quad e_{s2} = j\omega M_2(x) \cdot I_p
$$

Since the secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, the differential output is:

$$
e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = j\omega I_p \, [M_1(x) - M_2(x)]
$$

For small displacements about the null position, $M_1(x) - M_2(x)$ is a **linear function of $x$**:

$$
M_1(x) - M_2(x) = K_m \cdot x
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{e_o = j\omega I_p K_m \cdot x = K_{LVDT} \cdot x}
$$

The factor $K_{LVDT} = j\omega I_p K_m$ is a **constant for a given excitation frequency and current**, making $e_o \propto x$ linearly.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mass-Spring-Damper Equation of Motion

Let the accelerometer base (housing) have displacement $y(t)$, and the seismic mass $m$ have absolute displacement $z(t)$. The **relative displacement** measured by the LVDT is:

$$
x(t) = z(t) - y(t)
$$

The forces on the seismic mass are:
- Spring restoring force: $-k \cdot x$ (acts opposite to relative displacement)
- Damping force: $-c \cdot \dot{x}$ (proportional to relative velocity)

Applying **Newton's Second Law** to the seismic mass:

$$
m \ddot{z} = -k x - c \dot{x}
$$

Substituting $z = x + y$ and $\ddot{z} = \ddot{x} + \ddot{y}$:

$$
m(\ddot{x} + \ddot{y}) = -k x - c \dot{x}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
m \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k x = -m \ddot{y}
$$

Since base acceleration $a(t) = \ddot{y}(t)$:

$$
\boxed{m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\,a(t)}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Steady-State Sinusoidal Response

Let the input acceleration be harmonic: $a(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$.

The corresponding steady-state relative displacement is assumed as: $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

Substituting into the equation of motion and solving for amplitude $X$:

$$
X = \frac{mA}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$:

$$
X = \frac{A}{\omega_n^2 \sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

Where:
- $r = \omega/\omega_n$ (frequency ratio)
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ (natural frequency)
- $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ (damping ratio)

### 3.4 Derivation of Acceleration Sensitivity

From the LVDT equation, the output voltage is:

$$
e_o = K_{LVDT} \cdot X
$$

The acceleration sensitivity of the complete accelerometer is:

$$
S_a = \frac{e_o}{A} = \frac{K_{LVDT}}{\omega_n^2 \sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}
$$

**Critical Operating Condition:**

For the accelerometer to measure acceleration faithfully, $r \ll 1$ (operating frequency $\ll$ natural frequency). In this region:

$$
\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2} \approx 1
$$

So the sensitivity becomes approximately **constant**:

$$
\boxed{S_a \approx \frac{K_{LVDT}}{\omega_n^2} = \text{constant}}
$$

This is why LVDT accelerometers are designed with a **high natural frequency** relative to the measurement band, and why they excel at measuring **low-frequency vibrations and constant (DC) accelerations** like gravity.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behavior in Different Frequency Regions

| **Frequency Region** | **Condition** | **Device Behaves As** | **Output Proportional To** | **Useful?** |
|:--------------------:|:-------------:|:---------------------:|:--------------------------:|:-----------:|
| Very low / DC | $f \ll f_n$ | Pure spring | Acceleration | ✅ Yes (best for LVDT) |
| Near resonance | $f \approx f_n$ | Resonance peak | Large displacement (amplified) | ❌ Avoid |
| Above resonance | $f \gg f_n$ | Pure mass | Force / Mass (not acceleration) | ❌ No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LVDT Accelerometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph input[External Vibration Source]
        vibr[Vibration / Acceleration a t]
    end

    subgraph accel[LVDT Accelerometer Assembly]
        base[Housing Base Mounted on Structure]
        spring[Dashpot and Spring System]
        mass[Seismic Mass m]
        core[Coupling Rod to LVDT Core]
        LVDT[Linear Variable Differential Transformer]
        prim[Primary Coil AC Excitation 1 to 10 kHz]
        sec1[Secondary Coil S1]
        sec2[Secondary Coil S2]
    end

    subgraph signal[Signal Conditioning Unit]
        diff[Differential Output e o]
        demod[Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
        filt[Low Pass Filter]
        amp[DC Amplifier]
        out[Final DC Output Voltage proportional to a]
    end

    vibr --> base
    base -.relative motion.-> spring
    spring --> mass
    mass --> core
    core --> LVDT
    prim --> LVDT
    LVDT --> sec1
    LVDT --> sec2
    sec1 --> diff
    sec2 --> diff
    diff --> demod
    demod --> filt
    filt --> amp
    amp --> out
```

### 4.2 Functional State Diagram of Operating Regions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NullPosition: Core at Center
    NullPosition --> UpwardMotion: Acceleration Upward
    NullPosition --> DownwardMotion: Acceleration Downward
    UpwardMotion --> PhaseShiftedOutput: e s1 greater than e s2
    DownwardMotion --> AntiPhaseOutput: e s2 greater than e s1
    PhaseShiftedOutput --> DemodulatedDC: Phase Sensitive Demodulator
    AntiPhaseOutput --> DemodulatedDC: Phase Sensitive Demodulator
    DemodulatedDC --> AccelerationProportionalSignal: Final Filtered DC
    AccelerationProportionalSignal --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Architecture (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top of Housing Cover] --> B[Coil Former Assembly]
    B --> C[Primary Winding P in Center]
    C --> D1[Secondary S1 above P]
    C --> D2[Secondary S2 below P]
    D1 --> E[Movable Soft Iron Core]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Core Extension Rod]
    F --> G[Seismic Mass m]
    G --> H[Spring k]
    H --> I[Dashpot c]
    I --> J[Housing Base Attached to Vibrating Structure]
    J --> K[Acceleration Input a t]
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow Summary Table

| **Stage** | **Component** | **Signal Form** | **Key Characteristic** |
|:---------:|:-------------|:---------------:|:----------------------|
| 1 | Vibration Source | Mechanical $a(t)$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 2 | Mass-Spring-Damper | Relative displacement $x$ | **m** or **µm** |
| 3 | LVDT Core | Inductance change | **Henries** (H) |
| 4 | LVDT Secondary | AC voltage $e_o$ (modulated) | **mV to V** at carrier freq |
| 5 | Demodulator | DC voltage | **V** |
| 6 | Filter + Amplifier | Cleaned DC signal | **0–10 V** typical |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define an LVDT. Why is it used in accelerometer applications?*

**Model Answer:**

A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive, inductive transducer that converts linear displacement into a proportional differential AC output voltage. It consists of one primary winding and two secondary windings wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable soft-iron core.

It is used in accelerometer applications because:
1. It provides **frictionless measurement** (no mechanical contact between core and coils).
2. It has **excellent linearity** over a wide range.
3. It has **infinite resolution** limited only by the signal conditioner.
4. Its **robust construction** is suitable for harsh industrial environments.
5. It can measure **static (DC) and low-frequency accelerations** (unlike piezoelectric accelerometers that drift at low frequencies).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*List the main components of an LVDT accelerometer and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Component** | **Function** |
|:--------------|:-------------|
| Primary coil | Receives AC excitation; produces alternating magnetic flux |
| Two secondary coils | Sense flux change; output is differentially taken |
| Movable core | Couples flux between primary and secondaries; position-dependent |
| Seismic mass | Provides inertia; creates relative motion on acceleration |
| Spring | Restoring element; sets natural frequency |
| Dashpot | Provides damping; prevents resonance peaking |
| Coupling rod | Connects seismic mass to LVDT core |
| Demodulator circuit | Converts AC output to DC, preserving polarity (direction) |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the governing equation of motion of an LVDT accelerometer and explain the role of natural frequency and damping ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an LVDT accelerometer. Also derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — System Definition:**
The accelerometer consists of a seismic mass $m$ supported by a spring of stiffness $k$ and a dashpot of damping coefficient $c$ inside a rigid housing.

**Step 2 — Free Body Diagram:**
When the housing experiences displacement $y(t)$ and acceleration $a(t) = \ddot{y}(t)$, the mass $m$ has absolute displacement $z(t)$. The relative displacement is:

$$
x(t) = z(t) - y(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Applying Newton's Second Law:**

$$
m \ddot{z} = -k x - c \dot{x}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitution:**

$$
m(\ddot{x} + \ddot{y}) = -k x - c \dot{x}
$$

$$
\boxed{m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\,a(t)}
$$

**Step 5 — Natural Frequency and Damping Ratio:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad ; \quad \zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}
$$

**Role of $\omega_n$:** Sets the upper frequency limit for accurate acceleration measurement. Operating frequency must satisfy $f < 0.4 f_n$ to keep response flat.

**Role of $\zeta$:** For best performance in an accelerometer, $\zeta \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ — this provides nearly flat response up to $0.4 f_n$ without excessive resonance peak.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Construction (refer to Section 4.3):**
The LVDT has a primary $P$ centrally placed between two secondaries $S_1$ and $S_2$ connected in series opposition. A movable core is attached via a rod to the seismic mass, which rests on a spring-damper inside the housing.

**Working:**

1. Primary $P$ is excited by AC source $e_p \sin(\omega_e t)$.
2. With core at **null**, $e_{s1} = e_{s2}$ and net $e_o = 0$.
3. On acceleration, core shifts; flux through $S_1$ and $S_2$ becomes unequal.
4. Output voltage: $e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2}$.

**Derivation:**

Let mutual inductances be $M_1$ and $M_2$, function of $x$.

$$
e_{s1} = j\omega_e M_1(x) I_p \quad ; \quad e_{s2} = j\omega_e M_2(x) I_p
$$

Differential output:

$$
e_o = j\omega_e I_p [M_1(x) - M_2(x)]
$$

Linearizing about null:

$$
M_1(x) - M_2(x) = K_m \cdot x
$$

$$
\boxed{e_o = K_{LVDT} \cdot x \quad \text{where} \quad K_{LVDT} = j\omega_e I_p K_m}
$$

Since $x$ (relative displacement) is proportional to acceleration $a$ (in the operating band), the output is proportional to acceleration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** An LVDT accelerometer has a seismic mass of 0.05 kg and a spring constant of 5000 N/m. Calculate the natural frequency and the maximum measurable acceleration if the maximum permissible relative displacement is 2 mm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a frequency response curve, explain why an LVDT accelerometer is suitable for low-frequency vibration measurement. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Given:**
- $m = 0.05$ kg
- $k = 5000$ N/m
- $x_{max} = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 1 — Natural Frequency:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{5000}{0.05}} = \sqrt{100000} = 316.23 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{316.23}{2\pi} = 50.33 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Acceleration (DC / Quasi-static case):**

For $f \ll f_n$, the relative displacement under steady acceleration is:

$$
x = \frac{a}{\omega_n^2}
$$

For maximum $x = 2$ mm:

$$
a_{max} = x_{max} \cdot \omega_n^2 = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 100000 = 200 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Converting to g:**

$$
a_{max} = \frac{200}{9.81} \approx 20.39\,g
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{f_n \approx 50.33 \text{ Hz} \quad ; \quad a_{max} \approx 200 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 20.4\,g}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

The frequency response of an accelerometer is the ratio $|x/a|$ plotted against frequency ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$.

**Key observations:**

1. **Region $f \ll f_n$ (Region A):** The curve is **flat**, and $|x/a| \approx 1/\omega_n^2 = \text{constant}$. Output is **truly proportional to acceleration**. This is the **usable region** of an accelerometer.

2. **Region near $f_n$ (Region B):** A **resonance peak** appears, with peak amplitude dependent on $\zeta$. Output becomes nonlinear and **amplified** — measurement is meaningless here.

3. **Region $f \gg f_n$ (Region C):** The response falls off as $1/\omega^2$. Output is now **proportional to force**, not acceleration. The device effectively becomes a **force transducer** here.

**Why LVDT is suitable for low-frequency measurement:**

- The LVDT can measure **DC displacement** (it has no lower frequency limit; only carrier frequency limit).
- After demodulation, the LVDT accelerometer maintains fidelity down to **0 Hz (DC)**.
- This is a **major advantage** over piezoelectric accelerometers, which have a low cutoff (typically 0.1–1 Hz) due to charge amplifier drift.
- For low frequencies, the LVDT accelerometer operates well **below $f_n$**, ensuring linear, flat response.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2π** between $\omega_n$ (rad/s) and $f_n$ (Hz). Many write $f_n = \omega_n$ directly — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Sign error in Newton's second law** — the equation should have $-m a(t)$ on the RHS, not $+m a(t)$. The mass moves *opposite* to base acceleration initially.
> 3. **Confusing displacement sensitivity with acceleration sensitivity** — they differ by a factor of $\omega_n^2$.
> 4. **Mixing up "voltage sensitivity" (V/mm)** with "acceleration sensitivity" (V/(m/s²)) — units are not interchangeable; conversion needs the natural frequency.
> 5. **Omitting the demodulator step** in the working principle — examiners often award a full mark for mentioning *phase-sensitive demodulation*.
> 6. **Wrong working band assumption** — writing $f \approx f_n$ as the working range. Correct: $f < 0.4 f_n$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Bullet Point Summary**

- ✅ **LVDT** = Linear Variable Differential Transformer; **passive, inductive, AC-excited** transducer.
- ✅ Used in accelerometers as a **displacement sensor** in a **seismic mass configuration**.
- ✅ Primary is in the **center**; secondaries are on either side, connected in **series opposition**.
- ✅ At **null position**, output is **zero** (residual voltage is a non-ideality).
- ✅ Output is **proportional to displacement**, and its **phase** indicates **direction**.
- ✅ Governing equation: $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a(t)$.
- ✅ Natural frequency: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$; Damping ratio: $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$.
- ✅ Usable band: $f \leq 0.4 f_n$ for $\zeta \approx 0.6$–$0.7$.
- ✅ Sensitivity in operating band: $S_a \approx K_{LVDT}/\omega_n^2$ (constant).
- ✅ **Major advantage:** Can measure **DC and very low-frequency accelerations** (unlike piezo).
- ✅ **Major disadvantage:** Limited upper frequency range; physically larger than MEMS/PZT sensors.
- ✅ **Applications:** Seismic monitoring, civil structures, low-frequency industrial vibration, automotive NVH.
- ✅ **Standard excitation:** 1–10 kHz carrier; **Standard sensitivity units:** V/(m/s²) or V/g.
- ✅ Output requires **phase-sensitive demodulation** to recover DC and direction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Contact Type Transducers: Infrared, Ultrasonic Proximity Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Contact Type Transducers: Infrared, Ultrasonic Proximity Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Non-Contact Type Transducer?

A **Non-Contact Type Transducer** is a measurement device that detects the presence, absence, distance, or physical properties of a target object **without physically touching it**. The energy coupling between the sensor and the object occurs through a propagating medium — usually **electromagnetic waves** (e.g., infrared light) or **mechanical waves** (e.g., ultrasound) — making them ideal for measuring **moving, fragile, hot, corrosive, or inaccessible targets**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Non-contact transducers convert a physical quantity into an electrical signal by establishing an energy path between the sensing element and the measured object through a field or wave, without any mechanical contact.*

---

## 1.2 Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor

### Formal Definition
An **Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor** is a non-contact transducer that operates on the principle of **emission and reception of infrared electromagnetic radiation** (typically in the wavelength range of **780 nm to 1 mm**). It consists of an **IR emitter (LED)** that projects modulated infrared light toward a target and a **photodetector (photodiode or phototransistor)** that senses the radiation reflected back from the object’s surface. The intensity of the reflected beam is functionally dependent on the **distance and reflectivity** of the target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Operating Principle:** The IR LED emits a beam → beam strikes the object → a portion is **reflected** back → photodiode converts the reflected photons into a **photocurrent** → this current is processed to estimate distance/detect presence.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Torch in a Dark Room"
Imagine you are standing at one end of a dark corridor holding a torch. The light from the torch hits the wall at the other end and bounces back. If the wall is **closer**, the reflected beam is **brighter**. If the wall is **farther**, the reflected beam is **dimmer**. An IR sensor does exactly this, but with **invisible infrared light** and an **electronic eye** (photodiode) instead of human eyes. The brighter the reflected IR, the closer the object!

---

## 1.3 Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor

### Formal Definition
An **Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor** is a non-contact transducer that uses **high-frequency mechanical sound waves** (frequency > **20 kHz**, typically **40 kHz** in industrial sensors) to detect the presence of an object or measure its distance. It works on the principle of **time-of-flight (ToF)**: a piezoelectric crystal emits an ultrasonic pulse, which travels through air, reflects off the target, and returns to the sensor. The **round-trip time** is measured and used to compute distance using the known **velocity of sound in air**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Operating Principle:** Piezoelectric emitter generates a 40 kHz pulse → pulse travels through air → reflects from object → returns to receiver → the **elapsed time (t)** is measured → distance is calculated as $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bat's Echo"
Close your eyes and clap once in a mountain valley. You hear the **echo** after a small delay. The longer the delay, the **farther** the cliff. Bats use this exact mechanism (called *echolocation*) to navigate in total darkness. An ultrasonic sensor is a tiny, engineered version of a bat — it sends a "clap" of ultrasound too high-pitched for human ears and listens for the echo to judge distance!

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Engineering Reference Value:** The **velocity of sound in air at 20°C is 343 m/s**. This is the most commonly used number in ultrasonic distance calculation problems.

---

## 1.4 The Two Energy Domains — Side-by-Side Intuition

| Energy Medium | Wave Type | Domain | Example |
|---------------|-----------|--------|---------|
| **Infrared** | Electromagnetic wave | Light spectrum (just below red) | TV remote, line-following robot |
| **Ultrasonic** | Mechanical (acoustic) wave | Sound beyond human hearing | Car reverse parking sensor, tank level meter |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** These two sensors are the *most frequently asked* non-contact transducers in university examinations. A clear understanding of their *physics of operation*, *distance formulas*, *advantages*, *limitations*, and *comparative performance* is essential for scoring full marks.

---

## 1.5 Typical Real-World Targets Detected

- **IR Sensor:** Solid opaque objects, color-sensitive surfaces, retroreflective tapes (used in line-following robots, automatic hand dryers, security alarms).
- **Ultrasonic Sensor:** Any object that can reflect sound — including **transparent, glossy, metallic, liquid surfaces** (used in car reverse horns, bottle counters in bottling plants, water level indicators in overhead tanks).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor — Working Theory

### 2.1.1 Functional Block Architecture

The IR proximity sensor operates through the following sequential sub-systems:

- **IR Emitter Stage:** An IR LED is forward-biased with a current-limiting resistor. It emits modulated IR (often **38 kHz modulation** in remote control applications to avoid ambient light interference).
- **Beam Propagation Stage:** The emitted IR light travels through air and strikes the target surface.
- **Reflection Stage:** The surface reflects a fraction of incident light based on its **albedo (ρ)** and **surface texture**.
- **Detection Stage:** A photodiode (reverse-biased) or phototransistor collects the reflected photons and converts them into a small current.
- **Signal Conditioning Stage:** Transimpedance amplifier → comparator → digital output (HIGH/LOW) OR analog output (proportional voltage).

### 2.1.2 Key Physical Relationships

The reflected intensity at the photodetector follows an **inverse square law** for ideal diffuse reflection:

$$I_{r} \;\propto\; \frac{\rho \cdot I_{0}}{d^{2}}$$

Where:
- $I_{r}$ = received intensity at the photodiode (W/m²)
- $I_{0}$ = transmitted intensity from IR LED (W/m²)
- $\rho$ = reflectivity of the target surface (dimensionless, $0 \le \rho \le 1$)
- $d$ = distance between sensor face and target (m)

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Because the intensity falls off as $1/d^{2}$, the **usable range of an IR sensor is typically limited to 10–30 cm** for diffuse-type sensors. Beyond this, the reflected signal becomes too weak to reliably discriminate from noise.

### 2.1.3 Modulation — Why 38 kHz?

Ambient sunlight contains massive amounts of IR that would saturate the photodiode. To avoid this, the IR LED is pulsed (modulated) at a specific carrier frequency — **typically 38 kHz** (TSOP1738 receiver standard). The receiver is tuned with a **band-pass filter** to accept only this frequency, dramatically improving **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**.

---

## 2.2 Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor — Working Theory

### 2.2.1 Functional Block Architecture

- **Pulse Generation Stage:** A microcontroller or timer circuit generates a **trigger pulse** (≥ 10 µs) which excites the piezoelectric crystal.
- **Transmission Stage:** The crystal mechanically vibrates at its **resonant frequency** (usually **40 kHz**), launching an ultrasonic wave packet into the air.
- **Propagation Stage:** Sound travels at **v = 343 m/s at 20°C** through air. It interacts with the target — rigid surfaces reflect nearly 100% of the energy; soft foam absorbs it.
- **Reception Stage:** The same (or a separate) piezoelectric crystal receives the echo and converts the mechanical pressure wave back into a small AC voltage.
- **Timing Stage:** The round-trip time **t** is precisely measured using a high-frequency counter clock.
- **Computation Stage:** Distance is calculated using $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$.

### 2.2.2 Velocity of Sound — The Temperature Dependency

The velocity of sound in air is **temperature-dependent** (a frequent KTU question topic):

$$v \;=\; 331.4 \;+\; 0.6 \, T \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

Where **T** is the air temperature in degrees Celsius (°C). This linear approximation is valid for the typical engineering range of **−20°C to +50°C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner’s Hot Question:** *“An ultrasonic sensor is used at 40°C. What percentage error is introduced if the calibration assumes 20°C?”* Always remember to apply the temperature correction in ultrasonic problems!

### 2.2.3 Beam Spread and Detection Cone

Unlike a laser, the ultrasonic beam diverges in a **cone-shaped pattern** with a typical beam angle of **15° to 30°**. This means:
- A **small object** that does not intersect the cone may not be detected.
- A **flat wall** placed perpendicular to the beam is detected easily; a **slanted surface** may deflect the echo away from the receiver, causing a **missed detection**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|----------|---------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Velocity of sound in air | $v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$ | m/s | Temperature-corrected ToF calculation |
| 2 | Distance from ToF | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | m | The "**divide by 2**" accounts for **round-trip** |
| 3 | Round-trip time | $t = \dfrac{2d}{v}$ | s | Used in sensor calibration |
| 4 | Frequency of ultrasound | $f = 40 \text{ kHz (typical)}$ | Hz | Resonant frequency of piezo crystal |
| 5 | Wavelength of ultrasound | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | m | At 343 m/s and 40 kHz: $\lambda \approx 8.6$ mm |
| 6 | Minimum detectable distance | $d_{min} = \dfrac{v \cdot \tau}{2}$ | m | $\tau$ = transmit pulse width |
| 7 | Received IR intensity | $I_{r} \propto \dfrac{\rho \cdot I_{0}}{d^{2}}$ | W/m² | Inverse square law for diffuse reflection |
| 8 | Maximum unambiguous distance | $d_{max} = \dfrac{v \cdot T_{p}}{2}$ | m | $T_p$ = pulse repetition period |
| 9 | Range resolution | $\Delta d = \dfrac{v}{2 \cdot f_{clock}}$ | m | Depends on timer clock frequency |
| 10 | Beam half-angle divergence | $\theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | rad | $D$ = transducer diameter |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $d = v \cdot t$ (forgetting to divide by 2). The echo travels **to the object AND back**, so the total path is $2d = v \cdot t$. This is the **most common mark-losing error** in KTU answer sheets!

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sensor | Industry Use Case | Why This Sensor? |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| **IR Proximity** | Mobile phone screen turn-off during calls | Detects ear proximity, saves battery |
| **IR Proximity** | Automatic faucets and soap dispensers | Detects hand, triggers solenoid |
| **IR Proximity** | Industrial conveyor counting | Counts objects as they break the IR beam |
| **Ultrasonic** | Car reverse parking sensors | Detects obstacles regardless of light/color |
| **Ultrasonic** | Tank level measurement (chemicals, fuels) | Non-contact, works with corrosive/foamy liquids |
| **Ultrasonic** | Drone altitude hold (terrain following) | Measures height above ground in real-time |
| **Ultrasonic** | Robotic obstacle avoidance | Wide beam covers blind spots |
| **Ultrasonic** | Wind speed anemometers (specialized) | Measures travel time of ultrasonic pulse against wind |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Ultrasonic sensors are **color-blind and light-independent** — perfect for dark, smoky, or steamy environments where IR would fail. Conversely, IR sensors are **faster (kHz to MHz response)** and **more precise at close range**, ideal for high-speed production lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ultrasonic Distance from Time-of-Flight

### Given
A target object is placed at an unknown distance $d$ from an ultrasonic sensor. The sensor emits a 40 kHz pulse and receives the echo after a round-trip time of $t = 6.0$ ms. The ambient temperature is $T = 30°$C. Calculate the distance $d$.

### Step 1 — Temperature-Corrected Velocity of Sound

Use the linearized velocity formula:
$$v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$$

Substituting $T = 30°$C:

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 30$$

$$v = 331.4 + 18.0$$

$$v = 349.4 \text{ m/s}$$

> **Logic:** Air at 30°C is less dense; sound travels faster. We must correct the standard 343 m/s value, otherwise we get a ~2% error.

### Step 2 — Round-Trip Kinematic Equation

The ultrasonic pulse travels from the transducer to the target (a distance $d$) and then reflects back the same distance $d$. The total path length covered by the sound wave is therefore:

$$\text{Total Path} = d + d = 2d$$

Since the wave moves at constant velocity $v$ for a total time $t$:

$$2d = v \cdot t$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Distance

Rearranging the equation:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

Substituting $v = 349.4$ m/s and $t = 6.0 \text{ ms} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}$ s:

$$d = \frac{349.4 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{2.0964}{2}$$

$$d = 1.0482 \text{ m}$$

> **Final Answer:** $d \approx 1.05$ m (or 104.82 cm).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marking Insight:** If you had wrongly used $d = v \cdot t$ without the /2 factor, you would have reported 2.10 m — double the actual value. Always state "round-trip" in the derivation step to earn full method marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — IR Sensor Output Voltage vs. Distance

### Given
An IR proximity sensor uses an IR LED emitting optical power $P_0 = 10$ mW. The target has reflectivity $\rho = 0.6$ (typical for white paper). The photodiode has an active area $A = 5 \text{ mm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m² and responsivity $\mathcal{R} = 0.5$ A/W. The system geometry can be modeled by the inverse square law. Find the photocurrent at distances $d = 5$ cm and $d = 20$ cm.

### Step 1 — Define the Inverse Square Law Model

For a diffuse-reflective IR sensor, the received optical power at the photodiode is:

$$P_r = \frac{\rho \cdot P_0 \cdot A}{k \cdot d^{2}}$$

Where $k$ is a geometry constant (combining beam divergence, alignment, etc.). For this problem, the combined system constant is given as $k = 1.5$ (in consistent SI units).

### Step 2 — Photocurrent Relationship

The photocurrent generated by the photodiode is:

$$I_{ph} = \mathcal{R} \cdot P_r$$

Therefore:

$$I_{ph} = \frac{\mathcal{R} \cdot \rho \cdot P_0 \cdot A}{k \cdot d^{2}}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate at d = 5 cm = 0.05 m

$$I_{ph}(0.05) = \frac{0.5 \times 0.6 \times 0.010 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times (0.05)^{2}}$$

Computing the numerator:
$$0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.30$$
$$0.30 \times 0.010 = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$3.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$$

Computing the denominator:
$$1.5 \times (0.05)^{2} = 1.5 \times 0.0025 = 3.75 \times 10^{-3}$$

Therefore:
$$I_{ph}(0.05) = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{3.75 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} = 4.0 \; \mu\text{A}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate at d = 20 cm = 0.20 m

$$I_{ph}(0.20) = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{1.5 \times (0.20)^{2}}$$

$$= \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{1.5 \times 0.04} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-8}}{0.06} = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A} = 0.25 \; \mu\text{A}$$

### Step 5 — Verify the Inverse Square Behavior

Ratio check:
$$\frac{I_{ph}(0.05)}{I_{ph}(0.20)} = \frac{4.0}{0.25} = 16$$

Distance ratio:
$$\left(\frac{0.20}{0.05}\right)^{2} = 4^{2} = 16 \; \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The current falls off as $1/d^{2}$. When the distance **quadruples** (5 cm → 20 cm), the photocurrent becomes **1/16th** of the original.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Unambiguous Range of an Ultrasonic Sensor

### Given
An ultrasonic sensor transmits a pulse of width $\tau = 200 \; \mu\text{s}$ and waits $T_p = 30$ ms before transmitting the next pulse (pulse repetition period). The ambient temperature is 20°C. Determine the **maximum unambiguous range** $d_{max}$.

### Step 1 — Calculate Velocity of Sound at 20°C

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 20 = 331.4 + 12 = 343.4 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Understand the Ambiguity Condition

The sensor must complete reception of the **previous echo** before the **next pulse is transmitted**; otherwise, the echoes will overlap and create **range ambiguity**.

Mathematical condition for unambiguous operation:

$$T_p \geq t_{max} = \frac{2 \cdot d_{max}}{v}$$

Rearranging:

$$d_{max} = \frac{v \cdot T_p}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$d_{max} = \frac{343.4 \times 30 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d_{max} = \frac{10.302}{2}$$

$$d_{max} = 5.151 \text{ m}$$

> **Final Answer:** $d_{max} \approx 5.15$ m. Any target **farther than 5.15 m** will produce an echo that arrives **after the next pulse** has already been transmitted, causing the sensor to confuse it with a near-range echo.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — IR vs. Ultrasonic Head-to-Head
