| Parameter | Infrared (IR) Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Operating Medium** | Electromagnetic (IR photons) | Mechanical (acoustic) |
| **Typical Frequency** | ~38 kHz modulation (or DC) | 40 kHz (industrial standard) |
| **Typical Range** | 1 – 30 cm (diffuse) | 2 cm – 10 m |
| **Speed of Propagation** | $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (light) | 343 m/s (sound at 20°C) |
| **Response Time** | Very fast ($\mu$s range) | Slower (ms range, due to ToF) |
| **Effect of Color** | Strongly affected (black absorbs) | Negligible |
| **Effect of Transparency** | Transparent objects are missed | Detects glass/liquid surfaces well |
| **Effect of Light** | Affected by ambient IR/sunlight | Completely unaffected |
| **Effect of Temperature** | Negligible | Velocity changes ~0.6 m/s per °C |
| **Effect of Humidity** | Negligible | Slight reduction in velocity |
| **Effect of Soft/Porous Surface** | Works if surface reflects | Foam/fabric absorbs sound |
| **Effect of Dust/Fog** | Negligible | Significant attenuation |
| **Beam Pattern** | Narrow, focused cone | Wide diverging cone (15–30°) |
| **Output Type** | Analog (proportional) or Digital | Time-based (ToF) → distance |
| **Cost** | Very low (₹30–₹100) | Moderate (₹150–₹600) |
| **Best Use Case** | Short range, color contrast, fast counting | Long range, dark/dusty environment, transparent targets |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exam:** *"IR is **I**nstantaneous and **I**nexpensive; Ultrasonic is **U**biquitous and **U**niversal."*

---

## 3.5 Comparative Error Analysis — When IR Beats Ultrasonic (and Vice Versa)

**Scenario 1: Detecting a transparent glass bottle on a conveyor**
- **IR Sensor:** ❌ Fails — light passes through the glass.
- **Ultrasonic Sensor:** ✅ Succeeds — sound reflects off the glass surface.

**Scenario 2: Counting shiny metal components at high speed**
- **IR Sensor:** ✅ Excels — fast response, mirror-like reflection is detected.
- **Ultrasonic Sensor:** ⚠️ Marginal — slanted metal may deflect the echo away.

**Scenario 3: Measuring water level in a fuel tank with foam**
- **IR Sensor:** ❌ Fails — IR scatters in foam.
- **Ultrasonic Sensor:** ⚠️ Compromised — foam absorbs sound. Better choice would be a **radar** sensor.

**Scenario 4: Outdoor security perimeter in bright sunlight**
- **IR Sensor:** ❌ Saturates — ambient sunlight contains IR.
- **Ultrasonic Sensor:** ✅ Unaffected by light.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Design Rule:** Never use a single sensor technology for critical applications. Industrial systems **always combine** IR and ultrasonic to cover each other’s blind spots.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IR Proximity Sensor — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Power Supply 5V] --> B[Oscillator 38 kHz]
    B --> C[Driver Amplifier]
    C --> D[IR LED Emitter]
    D -- IR Beam --> E[Target Object]
    E -- Reflected Beam --> F[Photodiode Detector]
    F --> G[Band-Pass Filter 38 kHz]
    G --> H[Amplifier Stage]
    H --> I[Comparator]
    I --> J[Output: HIGH or LOW]
    K[Microcontroller] --> B
    J --> K
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- The **Oscillator** generates a 38 kHz carrier (modulation).
- The **Driver Amplifier** boosts this signal to drive the **IR LED**.
- The **IR LED** emits a modulated infrared beam toward the target.
- The **Target Object** reflects a portion of the beam.
- The **Photodiode** receives the reflected photons and converts them into current.
- The **Band-Pass Filter** rejects ambient light and passes only the 38 kHz modulated signal.
- The **Amplifier** and **Comparator** convert the tiny analog signal into a clean digital output.

---

## 4.2 Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Microcontroller Trigger Pulse 10 us] --> B[Pulse Generator Circuit]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Transmitter 40 kHz]
    C -- Ultrasonic Pulse --> D[Air Medium]
    D -- Echo --> E[Piezoelectric Receiver]
    E --> F[Low Noise Amplifier]
    F --> G[Band-Pass Filter 40 kHz]
    G --> H[Envelope Detector]
    H --> I[Time to Voltage Converter]
    I --> J[Analog Output OR Digital Display]
    B --> K[Timer Counter START]
    E --> K[TIMER STOP on echo]
    K --> J
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- The **Microcontroller** sends a **trigger pulse** of at least 10 µs to initiate measurement.
- The **Pulse Generator** excites the **Piezoelectric Transmitter** at 40 kHz.
- The **Air Medium** carries the pulse to the target and back.
- The **Piezoelectric Receiver** picks up the returning echo.
- The **Time-to-Voltage Converter** translates the round-trip time into a proportional DC voltage.
- The **Timer Counter** (started on transmission, stopped on echo reception) directly provides a digital time measurement for computing distance.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree — Which Sensor to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need Non-Contact Sensor] --> B{Target Range}
    B -- Less than 30 cm --> C{Ambient Light Conditions}
    B -- More than 30 cm --> D[Choose ULTRASONIC]
    C -- Bright or Sunlit --> E[Choose IR with 38 kHz Modulation]
    C -- Dark Indoor --> F{Target Material}
    F -- Transparent or Liquid --> G[Choose ULTRASONIC]
    F -- Solid Opaque --> H[Choose IR Proximity]
    H --> I[Output: Low Cost and Fast Response]
    G --> J[Output: Reliable at Long Range]
    E --> K[Output: Ambient Light Immune]
    D --> L[Output: Works in Smoke or Dust]
```

**Engineering Decision Logic:**
- **Range < 30 cm + Opaque target** → IR is the best, fastest, cheapest choice.
- **Range > 30 cm OR transparent/liquid target** → Ultrasonic is mandatory.
- **Bright sunlight environment** → Modulated IR (38 kHz) avoids saturation.
- **Smoky, dusty, or foggy environment** → Ultrasonic is unaffected by particulates.

---

## 4.4 Signal Waveform Timeline — Ultrasonic ToF

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as Microcontroller
    participant T as Transmitter
    participant A as Air Path
    participant R as Receiver
    participant C as Timer Counter
    M->>T: Trigger Pulse (10 us)
    activate T
    T->>A: 40 kHz Burst (8 cycles)
    deactivate T
    Note over A: Sound travels at 343 m/s
    A->>R: Echo Returns
    activate R
    R->>C: STOP pulse to Timer
    deactivate R
    M->>C: Read Timer Count
    C-->>M: Time t in microseconds
    M->>M: Calculate d = v*t/2
```

**Timeline Events:**
1. **Microcontroller** sends a 10 µs trigger pulse to the transmitter.
2. **Transmitter** emits a short burst of 8 cycles at 40 kHz.
3. **Sound** propagates through air to the target.
4. **Echo** returns and is detected by the receiver.
5. **Timer** stops counting the moment the echo is detected.
6. **Microcontroller** reads the elapsed time and computes distance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Explain the working principle of an ultrasonic proximity sensor with a neat block diagram. Mention any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An ultrasonic proximity sensor operates on the principle of **time-of-flight (ToF)** of high-frequency sound waves (typically **40 kHz**).

**Working:**
A **piezoelectric crystal** is excited by an electrical pulse, causing it to vibrate and emit an ultrasonic wave packet. This wave travels through air, strikes the target object, and **reflects back** as an echo. The same (or a second) piezoelectric crystal receives the echo. The round-trip time **t** is precisely measured using a high-frequency counter. The distance is then calculated using:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where $v = 343$ m/s is the velocity of sound in air at 20°C.

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Car reverse parking assistance** — detects obstacles behind the vehicle.
2. **Liquid level measurement in tanks** — measures the height of chemical/fuel in storage tanks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note:** Award 1 mark for the principle, 1 mark for the formula with explanation, and 1 mark for applications. Always state the **temperature-corrected velocity**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Compare infrared and ultrasonic proximity sensors based on operating principle, range, and effect of target color.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | IR Proximity Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Operating Principle** | Emission and detection of reflected **IR light** (electromagnetic wave) | Emission and detection of reflected **ultrasound** (mechanical wave) |
| **Typical Range** | 1 – 30 cm (short range) | 2 cm – 10 m (long range) |
| **Effect of Target Color** | **Strongly affected** — black/dark objects absorb IR and may be missed | **Negligibly affected** — color does not change acoustic reflection |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Tabulated answers with bold keywords score higher because they are easy for examiners to tick off against the marking scheme.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an **infrared (IR) proximity sensor**. Discuss how modulation at 38 kHz helps in improving the signal-to-noise ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **ultrasonic sensor** is used to measure the level of water in a tank. The ambient temperature is 35°C. The sensor is mounted at the top of the tank. The round-trip time measured for the ultrasonic pulse is **4.5 ms**. Calculate the depth of water in the tank. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
IR LED Emitter → Target Object → Photodiode Detector
   ↑                                  ↓
Oscillator (38 kHz)              Band-Pass Filter
   ↑                                  ↓
Driver Amplifier                 Comparator → Digital Output
```

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The IR proximity sensor consists of an **IR LED** (emitter) and a **photodiode** (detector) placed side by side. The IR LED emits a beam of infrared light which strikes the target object. A portion of this light is **reflected** back and falls on the photodiode. The photodiode generates a **photocurrent** proportional to the intensity of reflected light. As distance increases, the reflected intensity falls off as $1/d^2$, producing a smaller current. A **comparator** converts this analog signal into a clean digital HIGH/LOW output indicating presence/absence of an object.

**Role of 38 kHz Modulation (2 marks):**
Ambient sunlight contains a large amount of IR radiation, which would otherwise saturate the photodiode. To avoid this:
- The IR LED is **modulated** (pulsed ON/OFF) at a carrier frequency of **38 kHz**.
- The receiver photodiode is followed by a **band-pass filter tuned to 38 kHz**.
- The filter **rejects all ambient IR noise** (which is essentially DC) and passes only the modulated signal.
- This dramatically improves the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** and enables reliable operation even in bright sunlight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Verification Mark:** Award 1 extra mark if the student correctly mentions the **TSOP1738** standard IR receiver IC used in TV remotes.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Calculate the velocity of sound at 35°C (2 marks):**

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times T$$

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 35$$

$$v = 331.4 + 21.0$$

$$v = 352.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the time-of-flight formula (3 marks):**

The ultrasonic pulse travels from the sensor (mounted at the top) to the water surface and back. The total path length is $2d$ where $d$ is the depth of water.

$$2d = v \cdot t$$

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values and compute (2 marks):**

$$d = \frac{352.4 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{1.5858}{2}$$

$$d = 0.7929 \text{ m}$$

> **Final Answer:** The depth of water in the tank is approximately **0.793 m** or **79.3 cm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Full Marks Trick:** Always carry forward units throughout the calculation. The $10^{-3}$ factor (converting ms to s) is where most students drop marks. Write "4.5 ms = 4.5 × 10⁻³ s" explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the working of an **ultrasonic proximity sensor** with a block diagram. Derive the expression for distance measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **IR proximity sensor** is installed on a conveyor to detect black and white objects. Discuss the **challenges** faced and explain how **modulated IR** and **threshold adjustment** overcome these challenges. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Working (3 marks):**
An ultrasonic proximity sensor uses a **piezoelectric transducer** that vibrates at its **resonant frequency** (typically 40 kHz) when excited by an electrical pulse. The transducer emits a short burst of ultrasonic waves into the air. When these waves strike a target object, they reflect back as an echo. The same transducer (or a second one) receives the echo and converts it back into an electrical signal. A high-speed **timer** measures the round-trip time between emission and reception.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let:
- $d$ = distance between sensor and target (m)
- $v$ = velocity of sound in air (m/s)
- $t$ = round-trip time (s)

The ultrasonic wave travels from the transducer to the target (distance $d$) and then reflects back to the transducer (another distance $d$).

Total path length = $d + d = 2d$

Applying the fundamental kinematic relation (distance = velocity × time):

$$2d = v \cdot t$$

Solving for $d$:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

Since the velocity of sound in air is temperature-dependent:

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \, T \quad \text{(m/s, with T in °C)}$$

Substituting:

$$d = \frac{(331.4 + 0.6\,T) \cdot t}{2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme Alignment:** Award 2 marks for the working block diagram explanation, 1 mark for correctly identifying the round-trip logic, and 1 mark for the final formula with the temperature correction.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Challenges in Detecting Black vs. White Objects (3 marks):**
- IR sensors rely on **reflected light intensity** for detection.
- A **white object** has high reflectivity ($\rho \approx 0.8$) and reflects most of the incident IR back to the photodiode.
- A **black object** has low reflectivity ($\rho \approx 0.05$) and absorbs most of the incident IR, producing a very weak echo.
- If the threshold is fixed at a single value, the sensor may:
  - **Miss the black object** (signal below threshold) → false negative.
  - **Detect the white object as closer than it is** (signal too strong) → range error.
- **Ambient light interference** (sunlight, fluorescent lamps) adds noise to the photodiode signal, making threshold discrimination unreliable.

**How Modulation and Threshold Adjustment Help (4 marks):**

**1. Modulation at 38 kHz (2 marks):**
- The IR LED is pulsed ON/OFF at a carrier frequency of 38 kHz.
- A **band-pass filter** at the receiver passes only the 38 kHz component.
- Ambient IR (which is essentially DC or broadband) is **rejected** by the filter.
- This dramatically improves the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**, allowing even weak reflections from black objects to be detected reliably.
- The **modulation depth** (ratio of pulsed signal to ambient noise) can be 1000:1 or higher, providing excellent discrimination.

**2. Threshold Adjustment (2 marks):**
- The comparator threshold in the receiver circuit is **calibrated to a specific distance**.
- For conveyor applications, the sensor is mounted at a fixed height, and the threshold is tuned to detect objects passing beneath it.
- A **hysteresis comparator** is used to prevent false triggering at the boundary.
- For color-sensitive applications, the threshold can be **adaptively adjusted** using a microcontroller that samples multiple readings and sets the threshold based on the average ambient level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Industry Note:** Advanced IR proximity sensors (e.g., Vishay TCRT5000, Sharp GP2Y0A21) use **automatic gain control (AGC)** to dynamically adjust the receiver sensitivity, eliminating the need for manual threshold adjustment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks Every Semester:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the divide-by-2 in ToF formula:** Students write $d = v \cdot t$ instead of $d = (v \cdot t)/2$. This **doubles** the answer and is the **#1 mark-losing error** in ultrasonic problems.
>
> 2. **Using 343 m/s at all temperatures:** The velocity of sound **changes with temperature** (0.6 m/s per °C). At 40°C, it is 355.4 m/s, not 343 m/s. Always check if the question specifies a temperature.
>
> 3. **Confusing IR and ultrasonic principles:** IR uses **light** (electromagnetic wave), ultrasonic uses **sound** (mechanical wave). Writing "ultrasonic sensor uses infrared light" is a fundamental conceptual error.
>
> 4. **No block diagram:** KTU 2024 scheme awards **at least 2 marks** for a well-labeled block diagram in any sensor question. Skipping the diagram is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
>
> 5. **Missing units in numerical answers:** Always write the final answer with correct units (m, cm, mm, m/s). An answer "d = 0.793" without "m" loses a mark.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning modulation:** For IR sensor questions, failing to mention 38 kHz modulation in the answer shows incomplete understanding. Examiners explicitly look for this keyword.
>
> 7. **Confusing "piezoelectric" with "piezo-resistive":** Ultrasonic sensors use **piezoelectric crystals** (generate voltage when mechanically stressed). Do not confuse with piezo-resistive strain gauges (resistance changes with stress).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

### Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor
- ⭐ Operates on **reflected IR light** — uses an **IR LED** and **photodiode**.
- ⭐ Reflected intensity follows **inverse square law**: $I_r \propto 1/d^2$.
- ⭐ **38 kHz modulation** is the industry standard to reject ambient light noise.
- ⭐ Typical range: **1 – 30 cm** (diffuse), up to meters (through-beam).
- ⭐ **Affected by target color** — black objects absorb IR; white objects reflect strongly.
- ⭐ **Cannot detect transparent objects** (glass, clear plastic).
- ⭐ Response time: **microseconds** (very fast).
- ⭐ Key applications: line-following robots, mobile proximity sensors, object counters.

### Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor
- ⭐ Operates on **time-of-flight of sound** — uses a **piezoelectric transducer**.
- ⭐ Standard frequency: **40 kHz** (above human hearing).
- ⭐ **Velocity of sound in air at 20°C = 343 m/s**; formula: $v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$.
- ⭐ **Distance formula:** $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ — **always divide by 2** for round-trip.
- ⭐ Typical range: **2 cm to 10 m**.
- ⭐ **Color-independent** and **light-independent**.
- ⭐ **Can detect transparent objects** (glass, liquids) and works in dark/smoky environments.
- ⭐ **Affected by temperature** (velocity changes), **soft/foam surfaces** (absorb sound), and **beam angle** (15–30° cone).
- ⭐ Response time: **milliseconds** (slower than IR).
- ⭐ Key applications: car reverse parking, tank level measurement, drone altitude hold, robotic obstacle avoidance.

### Comparison Mnemonics
- ⭐ **IR = "Indoor-Range"** (short, indoor, intense).
- ⭐ **Ultrasonic = "Universal"** (long range, any color, any light).

### Universal Numerical Checklist
- ⭐ Always apply **temperature correction** to $v$ in ultrasonic problems.
- ⭐ Always state **assumed temperature** if not given (default 20°C).
- ⭐ Always include a **block diagram** in descriptive answers.
- ⭐ Always quote the **formula** before plugging in numbers.
- ⭐ Always carry **units** through the calculation.

### Sample Formula Quick-Reference Card
| Use this formula | When to use it |
|------------------|----------------|
| $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Ultrasonic distance from round-trip time |
| $v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$ | Velocity of sound at temperature $T$°C |
| $I_r \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}$ | IR intensity falloff with distance |
| $d_{max} = \dfrac{v \cdot T_p}{2}$ | Maximum unambiguous ultrasonic range |
| $d_{min} = \dfrac{v \cdot \tau}{2}$ | Minimum detectable ultrasonic distance |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Level Sensor - Ultrasonic Level Sensor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Level Sensor - Ultrasonic Level Sensor

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Optical Level Sensor

An **Optical Level Sensor** is a non-contact / quasi-contact electro-optical transducer that detects the presence, absence, or position of a liquid level by exploiting the difference in the **refractive index** between the liquid medium and the gaseous medium (usually air) above it. The fundamental operating principle is **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** of light at a glass–medium interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> An optical level sensor is a device that uses a light source (LED or laser diode) and a photodetector (photodiode/phototransistor) housed in a transparent polymer or glass tip. When the tip is immersed in liquid, the refractive index change causes the light to refract out of the tip instead of reflecting back to the detector — switching its output state.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Torch in the Swimming Pool"**
Imagine you shine a torch at a steep angle into a swimming pool from inside the water. When there is **no water** (only air at the tip), the light beam is internally reflected back to your eyes like a mirror — you see a bright spot. The moment you dip the torch into **water**, the light escapes out of the water surface into the air and you can no longer see the bright reflection. An optical level sensor does exactly this — it "sees" the bright reflection in air and "loses" it in liquid (or vice-versa, depending on design).

**Physical Constants & Standards Used:**

- Refractive index of air, $n_{air} \approx 1.0003$
- Refractive index of water, $n_{water} \approx 1.33$
- Refractive index of glass (sensor tip), $n_{glass} \approx 1.50$
- Refractive index of common oils, $n_{oil} \approx 1.46$
- Standard LED emission wavelength used: $\lambda = 660\ \text{nm}$ (visible red) or $\lambda = 940\ \text{nm}$ (near-infrared)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **critical angle** $\theta_c$ is the cornerstone parameter. For Total Internal Reflection to occur at the glass–air interface, the angle of incidence inside the glass must be **greater than** $\theta_c$. This is what makes the device fundamentally a *binary* (digital) level detector.

---

### 1.2 Ultrasonic Level Sensor

An **Ultrasonic Level Sensor** is a non-contact acoustic transducer that determines the distance from the sensor face to the surface of a liquid (or solid bulk material) by measuring the **Time of Flight (ToF)** of a high-frequency acoustic pulse — typically in the range **20 kHz to 200 kHz** — emitted by a piezoelectric crystal and reflected back from the target surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> An ultrasonic level sensor operates on the echo-ranging principle. A piezoelectric transducer emits an ultrasonic burst, which travels through the air/gas column above the liquid, reflects off the liquid surface, and returns to the same (or a second) transducer. The round-trip time $t$ is measured electronically, and using the known speed of sound $v$ in the medium, the distance (and hence the level) is calculated.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Bat in the Well"**
Picture yourself standing at the top of an empty well and shouting *"Hello!"* into it. The deeper the well, the longer it takes to hear the echo come back. A bat uses exactly this same trick — it screams ultrasonic chirps and listens for the echo to find insects in pitch darkness. An ultrasonic level sensor is essentially a "blind bat" pointed at a tank: it yells an ultrasonic pulse, waits for the echo from the liquid surface, measures the round-trip time, and converts that into the distance to the surface.

**Physical Constants & Standards Used:**

- Speed of sound in dry air at **20 °C**: $v = 343\ \text{m/s}$
- Speed of sound in dry air at **0 °C**: $v = 331.4\ \text{m/s}$
- Typical operating frequency: $f = 40\ \text{kHz}$ to $200\ \text{kHz}$
- Wavelength at 40 kHz: $\lambda \approx 8.6\ \text{mm}$
- Wavelength at 200 kHz: $\lambda \approx 1.7\ \text{mm}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Time of Flight (ToF)** principle combined with the **known speed of sound** in the propagation medium is the foundational physics of every ultrasonic level sensor. The factor of **2** in the distance formula (round trip) is the single most forgotten constant in KTU exam scripts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Optical Level Sensor — Working Principles

#### 2.1.1 The Physics of Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

The optical level sensor is built on **Snell's Law of Refraction**, which governs how a light ray bends when crossing a boundary between two media of different refractive indices.

**Snell's Law:**

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

where:
- $n_1$ = refractive index of the medium containing the incident ray (sensor glass tip)
- $n_2$ = refractive index of the medium containing the refracted ray (air or liquid)
- $\theta_1$ = angle of incidence (measured from the normal)
- $\theta_2$ = angle of refraction (measured from the normal)

**The Critical Angle $\theta_c$:**

The critical angle is the specific angle of incidence at which the refracted ray travels exactly along the interface, i.e., $\theta_2 = 90°$. Substituting into Snell's Law:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2$$

$$\boxed{\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}} \quad \text{— valid only when } n_1 > n_2$$

**Why is this important?**
- For $n_1 > n_2$ (light going from a denser to a rarer medium), if $\theta_1 < \theta_c$, the light refracts and partially escapes.
- For $n_1 > n_2$, if $\theta_1 > \theta_c$, the light undergoes **Total Internal Reflection** — 100% of the light is reflected back into the denser medium.
- This abrupt change in behavior (from "light escapes" to "light is reflected") is what produces the digital ON/OFF switching action of the sensor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The optical level sensor always operates with the **internal angle of incidence greater than the critical angle** for the glass–air interface but **less than the critical angle** for the glass–liquid interface. This guarantees an unambiguous switching state.

#### 2.1.2 Two Operational States of an Optical Level Sensor

**State 1 — Sensor in Air (No Liquid Touching the Tip):**
- The light from the LED strikes the inner surface of the dome/prism tip at an angle > $\theta_{c,air}$.
- Since $n_{glass} > n_{air}$, the condition for TIR is satisfied.
- Light is reflected back internally onto the photodetector.
- Photodetector output = **HIGH** (saturated / conducting).

**State 2 — Sensor Immersed in Liquid:**
- The liquid (with $n_{liquid} \approx 1.33$) replaces air at the boundary.
- The critical angle for the glass–liquid interface is much **larger** than for the glass–air interface.
- The fixed internal incidence angle is now **less than** $\theta_{c,liquid}$.
- Light refracts out of the tip into the liquid (and is absorbed/scattered by it).
- Very little light reaches the photodetector.
- Photodetector output = **LOW** (dark / non-conducting).

#### 2.1.3 Construction Variants

| Variant | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Dome / Conical Tip (TIR type)** | Most common. Uses a transparent polymer dome. | Point-level detection in tanks, sumps. |
| **Prism Tip** | Uses a triangular prism (often PMMA or glass). | High-temperature, high-pressure service. |
| **Fork / Through-Beam** | Separate emitter and detector on either side of a gap. | Detects liquid passing through a windowed section. |
| **Fiber-Optic Probe** | Light carried to/from a remote tip via optical fibers. | Hazardous / explosive / high-voltage environments. |
| **Refractometric Probe** | Two prisms at different angles for multi-liquid detection. | Distinguishing oil from water interfaces. |

#### 2.1.4 Beer–Lambert Law (for Optical Absorption Type Sensors)

Some optical level sensors operate on **light absorption** rather than TIR. For these, the transmitted intensity through the medium follows the Beer–Lambert Law:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha L}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = intensity of incident light
- $I$ = intensity of transmitted light
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient of the medium (in $\text{m}^{-1}$)
- $L$ = path length through the medium (in $\text{m}$)

This principle is used in optical level switches that need to distinguish between clear and opaque/turbid liquids.

---

### 2.2 Ultrasonic Level Sensor — Working Principles

#### 2.2.1 The Time of Flight (ToF) Principle

The sensor emits a short burst of ultrasound from a piezoelectric diaphragm. This pulse travels through the air column above the liquid, strikes the liquid surface, and a portion of its energy is reflected back (specular reflection) to the transducer. The same transducer (in monostatic design) or a second one (in bistatic design) receives the echo.

**Round-Trip Time Equation:**

The total distance traveled by the pulse is $2d$, where $d$ is the one-way distance from the sensor face to the liquid surface.

$$\text{Distance}, \quad d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

where:
- $v$ = speed of sound in the propagation medium (air), in $\text{m/s}$
- $t$ = measured round-trip echo time, in $\text{s}$
- $d$ = one-way distance from sensor to surface, in $\text{m}$

**Level Calculation:**

If the sensor is mounted at the **top** of a tank of total height $H$, then the actual liquid level (depth filled) is:

$$\text{Level}, \quad L = H - d = H - \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

If the sensor is mounted at the **bottom** (looking upward), then the level (depth empty from the top) is:

$$L = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

#### 2.2.2 Speed of Sound in Air — Temperature Dependence

The speed of sound in an ideal gas is given by:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}$$

For dry air, with $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$, and $M = 0.02897\ \text{kg/mol}$:

$$v_{air} \approx 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T_C \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

where $T_C$ is the air temperature in **°C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For every **1 °C** change in air temperature, the speed of sound changes by approximately **0.6 m/s**. A 10 °C error in temperature compensation causes roughly a **1.7 %** distance error. This is the single largest source of measurement error in ultrasonic level sensing.

#### 2.2.3 Piezoelectric Transducer Operation

The heart of the ultrasonic sensor is a piezoelectric crystal (typically **Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT**). It converts:

- **Electrical energy → Mechanical (acoustic) energy** (during transmission, as a transmitter)
- **Mechanical (acoustic) energy → Electrical energy** (during reception, as a receiver)

The resonant frequency $f_0$ of the disc is determined by its thickness $t$ and the speed of sound $c$ in the piezoelectric material:

$$f_0 = \frac{c}{2 \cdot t}$$

Most commercial sensors operate at **40 kHz** (long range, low resolution) or **200 kHz** (short range, high resolution).

#### 2.2.4 Beam Angle, Dead Zone & Attenuation

**Beam Angle (Divergence):**
The acoustic beam spreads in a cone. The half-angle of divergence is:

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{D} \approx 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

where $D$ is the diameter of the transducer aperture and $\lambda$ is the acoustic wavelength. A 40 kHz transducer (large $\lambda$) has a wide beam (~10°–15°), while a 200 kHz transducer (small $\lambda$) has a narrow beam (~3°–5°).

**Dead Zone (Minimum Sensing Distance):**
The transducer cannot receive while it is still ringing from the transmit pulse. The dead zone is typically:

$$d_{min} = \frac{v \cdot t_{ring}}{2}$$

where $t_{ring}$ is the ring-down time of the transducer (typically 1–3 ms). For 40 kHz sensors, $d_{min} \approx 15\ \text{–}\ 30\ \text{cm}$.

**Attenuation:**
Acoustic waves in air are attenuated by:

$$\alpha_{dB/m} = k_1 \cdot f + k_2 \cdot f^2$$

where $f$ is the frequency in kHz, and the coefficients $k_1, k_2$ account for classical and molecular absorption respectively. Higher frequencies attenuate **faster**, which is why high-frequency (high-resolution) sensors are limited to short ranges.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | Snell's Law of Refraction | Dimensionless; valid for any two media |
| 2 | $\sin \theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | Critical angle (TIR condition) | Valid only when $n_1 > n_2$ |
| 3 | $\theta_1 > \theta_c \Rightarrow \text{TIR}$ | Condition for Total Internal Reflection | $\theta_1$ measured from normal |
| 4 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha L}$ | Beer–Lambert Law (absorption sensors) | $\alpha$ in $\text{m}^{-1}$, $L$ in $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | One-way distance from ToF | $v$ in $\text{m/s}$, $t$ in $\text{s}$, $d$ in $\text{m}$ |
| 6 | $L = H - d = H - \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Liquid level (top-mounted sensor) | $H$ = tank height, $L$ = filled depth |
| 7 | $L = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | Liquid level (bottom-mounted sensor) | $L$ = empty height from top |
| 8 | $v_{air} = 331.4 + 0.6 \, T_C$ | Speed of sound in dry air (linear approx.) | $T_C$ in °C, $v$ in m/s |
| 9 | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma R T}{M}}$ | Speed of sound in ideal gas (exact) | $T$ in K, $M$ in kg/mol |
| 10 | $f_0 = \dfrac{c}{2 t}$ | Resonant frequency of piezo disc | $c$ = sound speed in PZT, $t$ = thickness |
| 11 | $\alpha_{dB/m} = k_1 f + k_2 f^2$ | Acoustic attenuation in air | $f$ in kHz |
| 12 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Acoustic wavelength | $v$ in m/s, $f$ in Hz, $\lambda$ in m |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical Level Sensors:** Used in pharmaceutical bioreactors, food and beverage filling lines, leak detection in double-walled tanks, and aseptic liquid handling where metallic sensors would corrode or contaminate. Their immunity to EMI and intrinsic safety (when fiber-optic) make them ideal for chemical plants and oil refineries.
- **Ultrasonic Level Sensors:** Used in wastewater treatment plants, bulk solid silos (grain, cement, plastic pellets), chemical storage tanks, and open-channel flow measurement. Their non-contact nature makes them suitable for corrosive, dirty, or foamy liquids where contact sensors would foul.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Angle for a Glass–Air Interface

**Problem Statement:** A dome-shaped optical level sensor is made of polycarbonate (refractive index $n_1 = 1.586$). The tip is exposed first to air ($n_2 = 1.0003$) and then to a detergent solution ($n_2 = 1.41$). The internal angle of incidence of the LED beam on the inner dome surface is $55°$. Determine in which medium the sensor will undergo Total Internal Reflection.

**Step 1 — State Snell's Law.**

Snell's Law relates the angles and refractive indices across an interface:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

**Step 2 — Define the Critical Angle.**

The critical angle $\theta_c$ is defined as the angle of incidence for which the refracted ray grazes the interface, i.e., $\theta_2 = 90°$. Substituting:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2 \cdot 1$$

$$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Critical Angle for the Glass–Air Interface.**

Insert $n_1 = 1.586$ (polycarbonate) and $n_2 = 1.0003$ (air):

$$\sin \theta_{c,\,air} = \frac{1.0003}{1.586} = 0.6308$$

$$\theta_{c,\,air} = \sin^{-1}(0.6308) = 39.12°$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Critical Angle for the Glass–Detergent Interface.**

Insert $n_1 = 1.586$ and $n_2 = 1.41$ (detergent solution):

$$\sin \theta_{c,\,liq} = \frac{1.41}{1.586} = 0.8891$$

$$\theta_{c,\,liq} = \sin^{-1}(0.8891) = 62.69°$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the Internal Incidence Angle.**

The LED beam inside the dome hits the inner surface at $\theta_1 = 55°$.

- For air: $\theta_1 = 55°$ vs. $\theta_{c,air} = 39.12°$. Since $55° > 39.12°$, we have $\theta_1 > \theta_{c,air}$, so **TIR occurs** in air.
- For detergent: $\theta_1 = 55°$ vs. $\theta_{c,liq} = 62.69°$. Since $55° < 62.69°$, we have $\theta_1 < \theta_{c,liq}$, so **TIR does NOT occur** in the detergent solution.

**Step 6 — Conclusion.**

The sensor undergoes TIR in air and refracts out into the detergent. The detector sees a HIGH signal in air and a LOW signal in detergent — exactly the desired binary switching behaviour.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Distance and Level using Time of Flight

**Problem Statement:** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted at the top of a vertical cylindrical tank of total height $H = 4.5\ \text{m}$. The air temperature inside the tank is uniform at $T = 32\ ^\circ\text{C}$. The round-trip echo time recorded by the sensor is $t = 18.7\ \text{ms}$. Determine:
(a) the speed of sound in the air column,
(b) the one-way distance $d$ from the sensor to the liquid surface,
(c) the actual liquid level (depth of liquid) inside the tank.

**Step 1 — Compute the Speed of Sound in Air.**

Using the linear approximation valid for dry air near standard conditions:

$$v_{air} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T_C$$

Substitute $T_C = 32\ ^\circ\text{C}$:

$$v_{air} = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 32$$

$$v_{air} = 331.4 + 19.2$$

$$v_{air} = 350.6\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert Echo Time to Seconds.**

$$t = 18.7\ \text{ms} = 18.7 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 0.0187\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the One-Way Distance.**

The pulse travels the distance from sensor to surface and back, so the total path length is $v \cdot t$. The one-way distance is half of this:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

Substitute values:

$$d = \frac{350.6 \times 0.0187}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{6.55622}{2}$$

$$d = 3.278\ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Liquid Level (Depth of Liquid).**

For a top-mounted sensor, the empty space above the liquid is $d$. The filled depth $L$ is:

$$L = H - d$$

$$L = 4.5 - 3.278$$

$$L = 1.222\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of Units and Reasonableness.**

- Distance: $3.278\ \text{m}$ is less than tank height $4.5\ \text{m}$ ✓ (valid, not below tank bottom)
- Level: $1.222\ \text{m}$ is positive ✓
- Fractional filling: $1.222 / 4.5 \approx 27.2\%$ ✓ (reasonable for typical process tanks)

**Final Answer:** $v = 350.6\ \text{m/s}$, $d = 3.278\ \text{m}$, $L = 1.222\ \text{m}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Temperature Compensation in an Ultrasonic Sensor

**Problem Statement:** A level sensor is calibrated at $T = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and gives a distance reading of $d_0 = 2.000\ \text{m}$ for an actual target at that distance. If the air temperature later rises to $T = 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and the measurement is made without temperature compensation, calculate the percentage error in the displayed distance.

**Step 1 — Speed of Sound at Calibration Temperature.**

$$v_0 = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 20 = 331.4 + 12.0 = 343.4\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Speed of Sound at Elevated Temperature.**

$$v_1 = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 40 = 331.4 + 24.0 = 355.4\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Round-Trip Time (Geometry Does Not Change).**

The true distance is $d_0 = 2.000\ \text{m}$, so the actual round-trip time at the new temperature is:

$$t_{actual} = \frac{2 d_0}{v_1} = \frac{2 \times 2.000}{355.4} = \frac{4.000}{355.4} = 0.011255\ \text{s} = 11.255\ \text{ms}$$

**Step 4 — Displayed Distance (Using Calibration Speed).**

The sensor uses the calibration value $v_0 = 343.4\ \text{m/s}$ to compute the distance:

$$d_{displayed} = \frac{v_0 \cdot t_{actual}}{2} = \frac{343.4 \times 0.011255}{2} = \frac{3.8668}{2} = 1.9334\ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Percentage Error.**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{d_{displayed} - d_{actual}}{d_{actual}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{1.9334 - 2.000}{2.000} \times 100 = \frac{-0.0666}{2.000} \times 100 = -3.33\%$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

A 20 °C rise in temperature causes the uncompensated ultrasonic sensor to **under-read** the distance by 3.33%. For a 10 m tank, this corresponds to a 33 cm error — a major process upset. This is why every commercial ultrasonic level sensor includes a built-in temperature sensor and applies the equation $v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$ in real time.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Optical vs. Ultrasonic Level Sensor

| Parameter | Optical Level Sensor | Ultrasonic Level Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Total Internal Reflection (TIR) | Time of Flight (ToF) of acoustic pulse |
| **Output Type** | Digital (ON/OFF) — point level | Analog (continuous) — level profile |
| **Contact vs. Non-Contact** | Quasi-contact (tip wets) | Truly non-contact (air gap) |
| **Measurement Range** | Point only (single switching point) | Wide: 0.1 m to 15 m typical |
| **Accuracy** | Binary (binary resolution) | ±0.25 % to ±1 % of full scale |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | Low (refractive index changes are minor) | High (~0.18 % per °C uncompensated) |
| **Effect of Foam / Vapour** | Negligible (mechanical light path) | Significant (false echoes, absorption) |
| **Effect of Turbidity** | Reduces reliability in absorption-mode sensors | Negligible (acoustic reflection) |
| **Effect of Tank Pressure** | Minimal (light not affected) | Minor (affects air density → speed of sound) |
| **Response Time** | Instantaneous (< 1 ms) | Slower (50 ms to 2 s) |
| **Hazardous Area Use** | Excellent (intrinsically safe with fiber optics) | Acceptable (with Ex-rated housing) |
| **Cost** | Low to moderate | Moderate to high |
| **Typical Application** | Leak detection, overfill protection, point switching | Tank level monitoring, inventory management |
| **Dead Zone** | None (point device) | 0.15 m to 0.5 m (sensor-dependent) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Optical Level Sensor (TIR Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LED Driver Circuit] --> B[Light Emitting Diode LED]
    B --> C[Transparent Dome Tip PMMA or Glass]
    C --> D{Medium at Tip Air or Liquid}
    D -- Air --> E[Total Internal Reflection TIR]
    D -- Liquid --> F[Refraction Out of Tip]
    E --> G[Photodiode or Phototransistor]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    H --> I[Comparator with Hysteresis]
    I --> J[Transistor Switch Output ON or OFF]
    J --> K[Load Lamp or PLC or Relay]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **LED Driver** supplies a constant current to the **LED** to produce a stable light beam.
- The light enters the **transparent dome tip** at a fixed internal angle of incidence.
- At the **decision node** (the medium at the tip), two physical outcomes are possible based on refractive index.
- In air, **Total Internal Reflection** directs the light back to the **photodetector**, producing a high signal.
- In liquid, **refraction** lets the light escape, producing a low signal at the detector.
- The **amplifier and comparator** clean up the signal and provide a clean digital **transistor switch** output.

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: Ultrasonic Level Sensor (Time-of-Flight)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Microcontroller or Timing Circuit] --> B[Pulse Generator 40 kHz Burst]
    B --> C[Piezoelectric Transducer PZT]
    C --> D[Acoustic Pulse into Air Column]
    D --> E[Liquid or Solid Surface]
    E -- Echo --> D
    D --> F[Received Echo Signal at PZT]
    F --> G[Bandpass Filter and Amplifier]
    G --> H[Envelope Detector]
    H --> I[Time of Flight Measurement t]
    J[Temperature Sensor PT100 or Built-in NTC] --> K[Temperature Compensation v equals 331.4 plus 0.6 T]
    I --> L[Distance Calculation d equals v times t over 2]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Tank Level Calculation L equals H minus d]
    M --> N[Display or 4-20 mA Output or HART]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **microcontroller** orchestrates a transmit–receive–compute cycle.
- The **pulse generator** drives the **piezoelectric transducer** with a short burst (typically 5–20 cycles at 40 kHz).
- The acoustic wave travels through the air column, **reflects off the liquid surface**, and returns as an echo.
- The **bandpass filter** rejects electrical and mechanical noise outside the operating band.
- The **envelope detector** extracts the envelope of the received RF signal for clean edge detection.
- The **temperature sensor** is critical: its reading is used to update the speed of sound in real time.
- The **level output** is then computed and converted to a 4–20 mA / HART / Modbus signal for the plant's DCS or PLC.

---

### 4.3 Beam Propagation and Dead Zone Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ultrasonic Transducer Mounted on Tank Top] --> B[Near Field Fresnel Region d less than D squared over 4 lambda]
    A --> C[Far Field Fraunhofer Region]
    C --> D[Beam Divergence Angle alpha = 0.61 lambda divided by D]
    D --> E[Liquid Surface Target]
    E -- Echo Path --> A
    A --> F[Dead Zone d sub min: Transducer Ringing]
    F --> G[Objects in this region are UNDETECTABLE]
    A --> H[Measurement Zone: d sub min to d sub max]
    H --> I[Reliable Level Measurement]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **near field** (Fresnel region) is a complex interference zone right in front of the transducer.
- The **far field** (Fraunhofer region) is where the beam shape is well-behaved and the angle of divergence is predictable.
- The **dead zone** is a critical specification: any target within this distance cannot be detected because the transducer is still mechanically vibrating from the transmit pulse.
- The **measurement zone** is the useful operating range — typically from $d_{min}$ to $d_{max}$ (where SNR drops below 6 dB).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Explain the working principle of an optical level sensor based on Total Internal Reflection. State any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An optical level sensor works on the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** of light at the boundary between a transparent sensor tip and the surrounding medium.

The sensor consists of an LED (light source) and a photodiode or phototransistor (light detector) housed inside a transparent polymer or glass dome-shaped tip. The internal angle of incidence of the LED beam on the inner surface of the dome is designed to be **greater than the critical angle** for the glass–air interface but **less than the critical angle** for the glass–liquid interface.

- When the tip is in **air**, the condition for TIR is satisfied. Light is reflected back internally to the photodetector, which produces a **HIGH** output signal.
- When the tip is immersed in **liquid**, the refractive index at the boundary changes, the TIR condition fails, the light refracts out into the liquid, and the photodetector receives very little light, producing a **LOW** output signal.

This abrupt change in detector output is interpreted as the level-switching point.

**Two advantages (any two, ½ mark each):**
1. No moving parts — high reliability and long life.
2. Intrinsically safe when constructed with fiber-optic probes — suitable for explosive/hazardous environments.
3. Immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI).
4. Suitable for very small, point-level detection in confined spaces.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**State and explain the Time-of-Flight (ToF) principle used in ultrasonic level measurement. Why is the factor 2 included in the distance formula?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle states that the distance to a target can be determined by measuring the time taken by a wave (in this case, an ultrasonic pulse) to travel from the transmitter to the target and back to the receiver.

An ultrasonic level sensor mounted at the top of a tank emits a short burst of high-frequency sound (typically 20–200 kHz) using a piezoelectric transducer. This pulse travels through the air column, strikes the liquid surface, and is reflected back as an echo. The transducer receives the echo, and the electronic circuit measures the round-trip time $t$.

The distance is calculated using:

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

**Why the factor 2?**
The factor 2 is included because the measured time $t$ is the **round-trip** time — the pulse travels from the sensor to the surface AND back. The total path length is therefore $v \cdot t$, and the one-way distance (sensor to surface) is half of this.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Optical Level Sensor (KTU Pattern: 2 sub-parts × 7 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an optical level sensor using the principle of Total Internal Reflection. Derive the expression for the critical angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An optical level sensor uses a glass tip of refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$. The sensor is used to detect the level of a chemical with refractive index $n_2 = 1.40$ and is also exposed to air of refractive index $n_2' = 1.00$. The LED beam inside the glass strikes the inner surface at an angle of incidence of $42°$. Determine:
(i) the critical angle for the glass–air interface,
(ii) the critical angle for the glass–chemical interface,
(iii) whether TIR occurs in air, in chemical, or in both. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (4 marks) + Critical Angle Derivation (3 marks)**

**Construction:**
- A transparent dome or prism made of glass or PMMA (refractive index ~1.5) forms the tip of the sensor.
- An **LED** (light source) and a **photodiode/phototransistor** (light detector) are placed side-by-side inside the housing, with their optical axes directed toward the inner curved surface of the dome at a fixed angle.
- A signal conditioning circuit (amplifier + Schmitt trigger) and a switching transistor are housed in the body of the sensor.
- Electrical cable (or fiber-optic cable) connects the sensor to the controller/PLC.

**Working:**
1. The LED emits light into the dome.
2. The light strikes the inner surface of the dome at an angle $\theta_1$ greater than $\theta_{c,air}$.
3. **In air:** TIR occurs. Light is reflected back to the photodetector → detector output is HIGH → switching transistor is ON (or OFF, depending on logic).
4. **In liquid:** TIR fails. Light refracts out into the liquid → very little light reaches detector → detector output is LOW → switching transistor changes state.
5. The Schmitt trigger provides **hysteresis** to prevent chattering near the level boundary.

**Derivation of Critical Angle (3 marks):**

Apply Snell's Law at the interface:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$

The critical angle $\theta_c$ corresponds to $\theta_2 = 90°$ (grazing refraction along the interface):

$$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2$$

$$\boxed{\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}}$$

This formula is valid only when $n_1 > n_2$ (light goes from optically denser to rarer medium). If $n_1 < n_2$, no total internal reflection can occur and there is no critical angle.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Critical angle for glass–air interface:**

$$\sin \theta_{c,air} = \frac{n_2'}{n_1} = \frac{1.00}{1.50} = 0.6667$$

$$\theta_{c,air} = \sin^{-1}(0.6667) = 41.81°$$

**(ii) Critical angle for glass–chemical interface:**

$$\sin \theta_{c,chem} = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.40}{1.50} = 0.9333$$

$$\theta_{c,chem} = \sin^{-1}(0.9333) = 69.07°$$

**(iii) Comparison with $\theta_1 = 42°$:**

- For air: $\theta_1 = 42°$ vs. $\theta_{c,air} = 41.81°$. Since $42° > 41.81°$, we have $\theta_1 > \theta_{c,air}$ → **TIR occurs in air**.
- For chemical: $\theta_1 = 42°$ vs. $\theta_{c,chem} = 69.07°$. Since $42° < 69.07°$, we have $\theta_1 < \theta_{c,chem}$ → **TIR does NOT occur in chemical**; light refracts out.

**Conclusion:** The sensor undergoes TIR in air and loses its TIR in the chemical. It will function correctly as a level sensor for this chemical. However, note that the margin in air is very small ($0.19°$) — a slight variation in the LED's internal angle or in the dome's geometry could make the sensor unreliable. A redesign (smaller $\theta_1$ or higher-$n$ tip material) is recommended.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Ultrasonic Level Sensor (KTU Pattern: 2 sub-parts × 7 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an ultrasonic level sensor based on the Time-of-Flight principle. Discuss the role of temperature compensation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic level sensor is mounted at the top of a tank of height $H = 6\ \text{m}$. The air temperature in the tank is $T = 45\ ^\circ\text{C}$. The measured round-trip echo time is $t = 27.5\ \text{ms}$. Calculate:
(i) the speed of sound in air,
(ii) the distance from the sensor to the liquid surface,
(iii) the actual liquid level in the tank.
If the temperature compensation circuit fails and the sensor continues to use the calibration value at $T = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$, find the new displayed liquid level. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Working (4 marks) + Temperature Compensation (3 marks):**

*Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.*

**Working:**
1. The microcontroller triggers the pulse generator to produce a short burst (5–20 cycles) of an electrical signal at the resonant frequency of the PZT (typically 40 kHz).
2. The **piezoelectric transducer** converts this electrical burst into an acoustic pulse, which propagates down the air column.
3. The acoustic pulse strikes the liquid surface and is reflected (specularly) back to the same transducer.
4. The received echo is converted back into an electrical signal by the PZT.
5. The signal passes through a **bandpass filter and amplifier** to improve SNR.
6. An **envelope detector** extracts the envelope, and a **time-interval measurement circuit** (or ADC) determines the round-trip time $t$.
7. The microcontroller uses the **current temperature** to update the speed of sound $v$ in real time and computes $d = v \cdot t / 2$.
8. The level $L = H - d$ is then transmitted as a 4–20 mA, HART, or Modbus signal.

**Role of Temperature Compensation:**
The speed of sound in air is a strong function of temperature ($v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$). Without compensation, a 1 °C change in air temperature produces roughly a 0.18 % error in distance. The temperature sensor (often a built-in NTC thermistor or an external Pt100) provides the air temperature to the microcontroller, which then computes the correct value of $v$ and applies it in the distance formula. This is the most important correction in any ultrasonic level measurement system.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Speed of sound at $T = 45\ ^\circ\text{C}$:**

$$v = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 45 = 331.4 + 27.0 = 358.4\ \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) One-way distance from sensor to liquid surface:**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{358.4 \times 0.0275}{2} = \frac{9.856}{2} = 4.928\ \text{m}$$

**(iii) Actual liquid level (depth of liquid):**

$$L_{actual} = H - d = 6.0 - 4.928 = 1.072\ \text{m}$$

**With failed temperature compensation (using $T = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$):**

$$v_{cal} = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331.4 + 15.0 = 346.4\ \text{m/s}$$

$$d_{displayed} = \frac{v_{cal} \cdot t}{2} = \frac{346.4 \times 0.0275}{2} = \frac{9.526}{2} = 4.763\ \text{m}$$

$$L_{displayed} = H - d_{displayed} = 6.0 - 4.763 = 1.237\ \text{m}$$

**Error in level reading:**

$$\Delta L = L_{displayed} - L_{actual} = 1.237 - 1.072 = +0.165\ \text{m} = +16.5\ \text{cm}$$

**Conclusion:** The temperature compensation failure causes the sensor to **over-read** the level by 16.5 cm, which is a significant error for a 6 m tank (~2.75 % of full scale).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Optical & Ultrasonic Level Sensor Questions (KTU Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the ToF equation.** Students frequently write $d = v \cdot t$ instead of $d = v \cdot t / 2$. This single error can cost **2–3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Mixing up the conditions for TIR.** TIR occurs when $\theta_1 > \theta_c$ AND $n_1 > n_2$. Students often forget the second condition and apply the formula even when light is going from a rarer to a denser medium — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong refractive index in the numerator.** The critical angle formula is $\sin \theta_c = n_2 / n_1$ where $n_1$ is the medium containing the **incident** ray, not the medium containing the refracted ray. Inverting these will produce a meaningless angle greater than 90°.
> 4. **Not converting units consistently.** $t$ in ms, $v$ in m/s, and $d$ in m must be consistent. A common mistake is to leave $t$ in ms without converting to seconds — this causes a 1000× error in the distance.
> 5. **Failing to state Snell's Law before deriving the critical angle.** KTU examiners specifically award marks for stating the governing law before the derivation begins. A direct jump to the critical angle formula without showing Snell's Law is penalized.
> 6. **Ignoring temperature compensation in ultrasonic problems.** Any numerical problem with an ultrasonic sensor that includes a non-standard temperature (not 20 °C) is implicitly testing whether the student knows the formula $v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$. Skipping this step costs a full 2-mark sub-part.
> 7. **Confusing top-mounted vs. bottom-mounted sensor sign convention.** For a top-mounted sensor, $L = H - d$; for a bottom-mounted sensor, $L = d$. Examiners frequently set problems that test this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Optical & Ultrasonic Level Sensors**

- [ ] **Optical level sensors operate on Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at a glass/polymer–medium interface.
- [ ] The critical angle is given by $\sin \theta_c = n_2 / n_1$, valid only when $n_1 > n_2$.
- [ ] TIR occurs when the angle of incidence **inside the denser medium** exceeds $\theta_c$.
- [ ] In **air**: TIR is satisfied → photodetector receives light → output HIGH.
- [ ] In **liquid**: TIR fails (light refracts out) → photodetector receives little light → output LOW.
- [ ] Optical sensors are **point-level (binary) devices**, not continuous level devices.
- [ ] **Ultrasonic sensors operate on Time of Flight (ToF)** of acoustic pulses.
- [ ] The one-way distance formula is $d = v \cdot t / 2$ — the **factor of 2** is for the round trip.
- [ ] For a top-mounted sensor, the liquid level is $L = H - d$; for a bottom-mounted sensor, $L = d$.
- [ ] The speed of sound in dry air is $v = 331.4 + 0.6\,T$ (m/s), with $T$ in °C.
- [ ] **Temperature compensation** is mandatory for accurate ultrasonic measurement.
- [ ] A 10 °C error in temperature causes approximately a 1.7 % error in measured distance.
- [ ] The **piezoelectric transducer** (typically PZT) acts as both transmitter and receiver.
- [ ] Typical operating frequencies are **40 kHz** (long range) and **200 kHz** (short range, high resolution).
- [ ] The **dead zone** of an ultrasonic sensor is the minimum detectable distance (typically 15–50 cm) due to transducer ring-down.
- [ ] The **beam angle** depends on wavelength and transducer diameter: $\sin \alpha \approx 1.22\,\lambda / D$.
- [ ] Ultrasonic sensors are **non-contact**; optical sensors are **quasi-contact** (the dome tip touches the medium).
- [ ] Foam, vapours, and turbulence can cause **false echoes** in ultrasonic systems.
- [ ] High turbidity can affect **absorption-mode** optical sensors (Beer–Lambert: $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha L}$).
- [ ] Optical level sensors are intrinsically safe (when fiber-optic) — ideal for **hazardous/explosive** environments.
- [ ] Ultrasonic sensors are ideal for **wastewater, bulk solids, and corrosive chemicals** due to non-contact operation.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Optical Level Sensor & Ultrasonic Level Sensor**
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Pyrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Pyrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Radiation Pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measurement transducer that determines the temperature of a remote, moving, or inaccessible hot body by measuring the **thermal radiation** (electromagnetic energy) emitted by the body, without requiring physical contact with the source. It operates on the fundamental principle of **Blackbody Radiation** and quantifies emitted energy using the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** of radiation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "A radiation pyrometer is a device used to measure the temperature of a body by measuring the radiation emitted by it, based on the principle that the intensity of radiation emitted by a body is a function of its temperature."

Mathematically, the emitted radiant energy per unit area per unit time is expressed as:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

Where:
- $E$ = Emitted radiant flux per unit area (W/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity of the body (0 ≤ $\varepsilon$ ≤ 1)
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature of the body (in **Kelvin**)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Campfire Sensation"**

Imagine you are standing near a campfire on a cold winter night. Without touching the flames, you can **feel** the heat on your face. Your skin is acting like a **radiation detector**! The hotter the fire, the more intense the heat you feel — even from a distance. 

Similarly, a radiation pyrometer is like a very **scientific, calibrated "heat-sensing eye"** that:
- Does **not touch** the hot object (like your skin doesn't touch the flame).
- Captures invisible infrared and visible light radiation from the object.
- Converts this energy into a temperature reading.

**Another Analogy — The "Red-Hot Iron Test":**
When a blacksmith heats iron in a forge, the iron first glows **dull red** (around 500°C), then **cherry red** (around 800°C), then **orange**, and finally **white-hot** (around 1300°C+). The color of the glow tells the experienced blacksmith the temperature. A radiation pyrometer does the same thing — but with **scientific precision** using optics and electronics.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Radiation Pyrometers (KTU Module 3 Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme classifies Radiation Pyrometers into THREE main types:**

| # | Type | Energy Measured | Key Dependency |
|---|------|-----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Total Radiation Pyrometer** | All wavelengths emitted | $\lambda = 0 \to \infty$ |
| 2 | **Optical Pyrometer** | Monochromatic (single wavelength) | Single $\lambda$ comparison |
| 3 | **Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer** | Two discrete wavelengths | Ratio of intensities at $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Wien's displacement constant:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physical Principles

### 2.1.1 Blackbody Radiation Concept

A **Blackbody** is an idealized physical object that absorbs **all** incident electromagnetic radiation, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence. Since it absorbs perfectly, it also emits the maximum possible thermal radiation at any given temperature (by **Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Realization:** Most practical objects are **not** perfect blackbodies. They are called **gray bodies**, with emissivity $\varepsilon < 1$. The radiation pyrometer must account for this.

### 2.1.2 The Three Pillars of Radiation Theory

1. **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** — Total emitted energy ∝ T⁴
2. **Planck's Radiation Law** — Spectral distribution of emitted energy
3. **Wien's Displacement Law** — Peak wavelength inversely proportional to T

---

## 2.2 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (The Foundation)

The **total** energy radiated per unit surface area of a blackbody per unit time is proportional to the **fourth power** of its absolute temperature.

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

For a real (gray) body:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

### Why is this T⁴ and not linear?

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Reason:** Temperature represents average kinetic energy of particles. As T rises, not only do more particles vibrate, but they vibrate *much more energetically*. The 4th power relation arises from quantum electrodynamics and the integration of Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths.

### Boundary Conditions & Validity

- Valid for **all temperatures above 0 K**.
- More sensitive at **high temperatures** (small ΔT → large ΔE).
- At room temperature, T⁴ is small; pyrometers become impractical.
- At T > 600°C, pyrometers become the **preferred** measurement method.

---

## 2.3 Planck's Radiation Law (Spectral Energy Distribution)

Planck's law describes how the spectral emissive power of a blackbody varies with wavelength at a given temperature:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right)}$$

Where:
- $E_{b\lambda}$ = Spectral emissive power (W/m³)
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength (m)
- $h$ = Planck's constant
- $c$ = Speed of light
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Optical pyrometers use only a **narrow band** of wavelengths (monochromatic) to compare target brightness with an internal reference lamp — this is the **disappearing filament principle**.

---

## 2.4 Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which the spectral emissive power is **maximum** is inversely proportional to absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

**Example:** A body at 3000 K has peak emission at $\lambda_{max} = 9.66 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 966 \text{ nm}$ (near-infrared, just beyond visible red).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 90% of KTU numerical problems on this topic:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Total emitted radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann) | W/m² |
| 2 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | For a perfect blackbody ($\varepsilon = 1$) | W/m² |
| 3 | $E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5(e^{C_2/\lambda T}-1)}$ | Planck's spectral formula | W/m³ |
| 4 | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b$ | Wien's displacement law | m·K |
| 5 | $\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^4$ | Ratio of emissions between two blackbodies | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\frac{E_{\lambda T}}{E_{\lambda T_s}} = \frac{e^{C_2/\lambda T_s}-1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T}-1}$ | Monochromatic emissive power ratio (optical) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_2 = \frac{hc}{k} = 0.014388$ | Second radiation constant (m·K) | m·K |
| 8 | $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ | First radiation constant (W·m²) | W·m² |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Temperature Range |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| Steel Plants | Molten metal in furnaces | 1500–1800°C |
| Glass Manufacturing | Molten glass tanks | 1000–1600°C |
| Cement Kilns | Rotary kiln interior | 800–1450°C |
| Aerospace | Rocket exhaust plumes | 1000–3000°C |
| Power Plants | Boiler tubes & superheaters | 500–1200°C |
| Semiconductor | Silicon wafer processing | 600–1400°C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-contact?** At these temperatures, contact sensors would melt, contaminate, or simply fail. Radiation pyrometers allow safe, remote, and continuous monitoring.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Temperature is Proportional to the Fourth Root of Emitted Energy

### Given:
- A body emits total radiation $E$ per unit area, related to its absolute temperature $T$ via the Stefan-Boltzmann equation.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Write the basic Stefan-Boltzmann equation for a blackbody:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

**Step 2:** This equation arises from integrating Planck's spectral formula over **all** wavelengths from $\lambda = 0$ to $\lambda = \infty$:

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right)} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3:** Introduce a substitution to convert the integral into a standard form. Let $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k T}$, so $\lambda = \frac{hc}{xkT}$ and $d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^2 kT} dx$.

The integral becomes (after handling limits and sign):

$$E_b = \frac{2\pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx$$

**Step 4:** The integral $\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx$ is a known result from the Riemann zeta function:

$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$E_b = \frac{2\pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \left(\frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2}\right) T^4$$

**Step 6:** Define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:

$$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, the final compact form is:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

**Step 8:** Solving for T (the form used by radiation pyrometers):

$$T = \left(\frac{E_b}{\sigma}\right)^{1/4} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{E_b}{\sigma}}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example 1: Temperature from Total Radiation

**Problem:** A total radiation pyrometer measures a radiant flux of $E = 4.5 \times 10^4$ W/m² from a furnace opening. Assuming the furnace behaves as a blackbody, calculate its temperature.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Apply the Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$$E = \sigma T^4$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for T:

$$T^4 = \frac{E}{\sigma}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$T^4 = \frac{4.5 \times 10^4}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$T^4 = 7.937 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 4:** Take the fourth root:

$$T = (7.937 \times 10^{11})^{0.25}$$

$$T = 2984.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to Celsius for practical reading:

$$T = 2984.4 - 273.15 \approx 2711.3 \text{ °C}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Check:** Always express the final answer in Kelvin when using radiation laws, then convert to °C for real-world readability.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example 2: Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer Computation

**Problem:** A two-color pyrometer measures spectral intensities at $\lambda_1 = 0.6 \, \mu m$ and $\lambda_2 = 0.9 \, \mu m$. The ratio of emissive powers is $R = 1.85$. Using the simplified Wien's approximation (valid for $\lambda T \ll C_2$), calculate the temperature.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Wien's approximation to Planck's law (when $e^{C_2/\lambda T} \gg 1$):

$$E_{b\lambda} \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5} e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 2:** The ratio of emissive powers at two wavelengths is:

$$R = \frac{E_{b\lambda_1}}{E_{b\lambda_2}} = \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 e^{C_2/T \left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values:
- $\lambda_1 = 0.6 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\lambda_2 = 0.9 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $C_2 = 0.014388$ m·K
- $R = 1.85$

**Step 4:** Compute the wavelength ratio term:

$$\left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 = \left(\frac{0.9}{0.6}\right)^5 = (1.5)^5 = 7.5938$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the exponential term:

$$e^{C_2/T \left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)} = \frac{R}{7.5938} = \frac{1.85}{7.5938} = 0.2436$$

**Step 6:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\frac{C_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right) = \ln(0.2436) = -1.4123$$

**Step 7:** Compute the bracket term:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_2} - \frac{1}{\lambda_1} = \frac{1}{0.9 \times 10^{-6}} - \frac{1}{0.6 \times 10^{-6}} = (1.111 - 1.667) \times 10^{6} = -5.556 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute back:

$$\frac{0.014388}{T} \times (-5.556 \times 10^{5}) = -1.4123$$

$$\frac{-7993.5}{T} = -1.4123$$

$$T = \frac{7993.5}{1.4123} = 5660.3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 9:** Sanity check: this is very high, suggesting the assumption $\lambda T \ll C_2$ may be slightly violated. Use full Planck's law for higher accuracy.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example 3: Optical Pyrometer — Monochromatic Brightness

**Problem:** An optical pyrometer compares brightness of a target at $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu m$ with a calibrated tungsten filament. The reference filament appears to match the target when its current produces a temperature of $T_s = 1500$ K. The second radiation constant is $C_2 = 0.014388$ m·K. Find the target temperature.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** By Wien's law, the ratio of monochromatic emissive powers:

$$\frac{E_{\lambda T}}{E_{\lambda T_s}} = \frac{e^{C_2/\lambda T_s} - 1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1}$$

**Step 2:** When target and reference filament brightness match at the same wavelength, their emissive powers are equal. Therefore the ratio is **1**:

$$1 = \frac{e^{C_2/\lambda T_s} - 1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1}$$

**Step 3:** This implies $T = T_s$ — but in a more practical case, the **monochromatic emissivity** of the target differs from the filament. Let the emissivity ratio be $\varepsilon_\lambda / \varepsilon_{\lambda,s}$:

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_\lambda} = \frac{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T_s} - 1}$$

**Step 4:** For a tungsten filament with $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.5$ and the target behaving as a blackbody ($\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$):

$$\frac{1}{0.5} = 2 = \frac{e^{0.014388/(0.65 \times 10^{-6} \times T)} - 1}{e^{0.014388/(0.65 \times 10^{-6} \times 1500)} - 1}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the reference exponent:

$$\frac{0.014388}{0.65 \times 10^{-6} \times 1500} = \frac{0.014388}{9.75 \times 10^{-4}} = 14.758$$

**Step 6:** So the denominator is $e^{14.758} - 1 \approx 2.555 \times 10^6$.

**Step 7:** The numerator is $2 \times 2.555 \times 10^6 = 5.11 \times 10^6$, so:

$$e^{0.014388/(0.65 \times 10^{-6} \times T)} = 5.11 \times 10^6 + 1 \approx 5.11 \times 10^6$$

**Step 8:** Take the natural log:

$$\frac{0.014388}{0.65 \times 10^{-6} \times T} = \ln(5.11 \times 10^6) = 15.445$$

**Step 9:** Solve for T:

$$T = \frac{0.014388}{0.65 \times 10^{-6} \times 15.445} = \frac{0.014388}{1.004 \times 10^{-5}} = 1433 \text{ K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The target appears at a **lower** temperature than the filament because the filament is less efficient an emitter, so its perceived brightness requires a hotter actual temperature to match.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Radiation Pyrometer. Mention any two advantages of using it over contact-type thermometers.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Radiation Pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measurement device that determines the temperature of a body by measuring the intensity of thermal radiation emitted by the body. It is based on **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**, which states that the total radiant energy emitted by a body is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature ($E = \sigma T^4$).

**Two Advantages:**
1. **No physical contact required** — can measure temperatures of moving, rotating, or chemically aggressive objects without contamination or wear.
2. **High temperature capability** — can measure temperatures ranging from 500°C to 6000°C, well beyond the range of thermocouples and RTDs.
3. **Fast response time** — radiation is detected at the speed of light, enabling nearly instantaneous temperature reading.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State and explain Stefan-Boltzmann Law. What is the significance of the constant $\sigma$?**

**Model Answer:**

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law** states that the total radiant energy emitted per unit surface area per unit time by a blackbody is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

Mathematically:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Total emissive power (W/m²)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

**Significance of $\sigma$:**
The constant $\sigma$ represents the **total energy radiated per square meter per second by a blackbody at 1 K**. It encapsulates the contributions of Planck's constant ($h$), speed of light ($c$), and Boltzmann's constant ($k$):

$$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **lose 3–4 marks** on this topic by:
> 1. Confusing Stefan-Boltzmann Law (T⁴) with Wien's Law ($\lambda_{max}$ · T = b).
> 2. Forgetting to convert Celsius to Kelvin (always use K in radiation formulas).
> 3. Not mentioning the **emissivity correction** when the body is not a perfect blackbody.
> 4. Skipping the diagram — KTU deducts up to 2 marks for missing labeled diagrams.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Total Radiation Pyrometer** (Féry Type). Derive the relationship between the EMF generated and the temperature of the target.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A total radiation pyrometer is sighted on a furnace opening. The pyrometer reading stabilizes at 1850 °C. The emissivity of the furnace surface is 0.78. Calculate the **true temperature** of the furnace. Also, determine what the pyrometer would read if the same furnace had an emissivity of 0.95 (assume all other conditions unchanged).

---

### **Question A — Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a): Construction & Working of Total Radiation Pyrometer**

**Construction (Block Diagram):**

The Total Radiation Pyrometer consists of the following components:

1. **Convex Mirror (Objective Lens):** Collects radiation from the hot target and focuses it.
2. **Diaphragm with Adjustable Aperture:** Controls the amount of radiation entering.
3. **Thermopile (Series-connected Thermocouples):** The hot junctions receive the focused radiation; cold junctions are maintained at a reference temperature.
4. **Reference Temperature Bath:** Maintains cold junctions at known temperature.
5. **Galvanometer / Display:** Indicates the EMF generated.
6. **Eyepiece (in some designs):** For sighting the target.
