The two hair springs (or spiral springs) provide a restoring torque that is **linearly proportional** to the angular deflection $\theta$:

$$
T_c = K \cdot \theta
$$

where $K$ is the **spring constant** in N·m/rad.

**Step 6: Applying the Steady-State Equilibrium**

At the final (steady) position of the pointer, the deflecting torque is exactly balanced by the controlling torque:

$$
T_d = T_c
$$

$$
N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A = K \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 7: Solving for the Deflection Angle**

Rearranging the equation to isolate $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \frac{N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A}{K}
$$

or equivalently:

$$
\theta = \left(\frac{N \cdot B \cdot A}{K}\right) \cdot I
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Deflection Equation:**
> $$\boxed{\theta = \frac{NBA}{K} \cdot I}$$
> Since NBA and K are **constants for a given instrument**, the deflection $\theta$ is **directly proportional to the current I**. This is the theoretical justification for the **uniform (linear) scale** of a PMMC instrument.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Equation of Motion (Dynamic Behaviour)

The complete dynamic equation of a PMMC movement, including damping and inertia, is given by:

$$
J \cdot \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K \cdot \theta = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A
$$

where:
- $J$ = moment of inertia of the moving system (kg·m²)
- $D$ = damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $K$ = spring constant (N·m/rad)

The natural (undamped) frequency of oscillation is:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{J}} \;\; \text{(rad/s)}
$$

The damping ratio is:

$$
\zeta = \frac{D}{2\sqrt{J \cdot K}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **PMMC instrument** to settle quickly to the correct reading, it is designed to have $\zeta \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$ (slightly underdamped) so that the pointer overshoots slightly and then settles in **less than 2 seconds**.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** A PMMC voltmeter has a coil of 100 turns, area $1.5 \text{ cm}^2$, suspended in a magnetic flux density of $0.2 \text{ T}$. The coil resistance is $50 \;\Omega$, and the spring constant is $2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m/rad}$. If the meter reads full scale when 50 V is applied, find (a) the current at full scale, (b) the deflection at half-scale, and (c) the meg-ohm sensitivity.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $N = 100$ turns
- $A = 1.5 \text{ cm}^2 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- $B = 0.2 \text{ T}$
- $R_c = 50 \;\Omega$
- $K = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m/rad}$
- $V_{fs} = 50 \text{ V}$

**(a) Current at Full Scale:**

$$
I_{fs} = \frac{V_{fs}}{R_c} = \frac{50}{50} = 1.0 \text{ A}
$$

**(b) Deflection at Full Scale:**

$$
\theta_{fs} = \frac{N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot I_{fs}}{K} = \frac{100 \times 0.2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0}{2 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\theta_{fs} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1500 \text{ rad}
$$

**(c) Meg-ohm Sensitivity (Ω/V):**

$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{1}{I_{fs}} = \frac{1}{1.0} = 1.0 \;\Omega/\text{V}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** This is a *very* low sensitivity value (1 Ω/V is poor). For comparison, a *good* laboratory voltmeter has 20 kΩ/V or 50 kΩ/V. The lower the full-scale current, the higher the sensitivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PMMC Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Current Source I_in"] --> B["Hair Spring Upper Lead"]
    B --> C["Moving Coil N turns in Radial Field B"]
    C --> D["Hair Spring Lower Lead"]
    D --> E["Return to Source"]

    C --> F["Deflecting Torque Td = NBIA"]
    F --> G["Spindle and Pointer Assembly"]

    H["Hair Spring Restoring"] --> G
    H --> I["Controlling Torque Tc = K theta"]

    J["Aluminium Former in Magnetic Field"] --> K["Eddy Currents Generated"]
    K --> L["Damping Torque D dtheta by dt"]

    G --> M["Calibrated Scale Reading"]

    F -. opposes .-> I
    F -. balanced by .-> I
    I -. equilibrium .-> M
    L -. opposes motion .-> G

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    style L fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I["Current I"] --> M1["Multiply: N x B x A"]
    M1 --> Td["Deflecting Torque Td"]
    S["Spring Constant K"] --> M2["Multiply by theta"]
    M2 --> Tc["Controlling Torque Tc"]
    Td --> EQ["Equilibrium: Td = Tc"]
    Tc --> EQ
    EQ --> OUT["Pointer Deflection theta"]
    Td -. opposes .-> Tc

    style I fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Td fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Tc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style EQ fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Physical Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MAGNETIC_SYSTEM["MAGNETIC SYSTEM (Permanent Magnet + Pole Pieces)"]
        PM["Permanent Magnet (Horseshoe)"]
        PP["Soft Iron Pole Pieces"]
        IC["Cylindrical Soft Iron Core"]
    end

    subgraph MOVING_SYSTEM["MOVING SYSTEM"]
        COIL["Rectangular Coil N turns"]
        SP["Spindle + Jewelled Bearings"]
        PTR["Lightweight Pointer"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_SYSTEM["CONTROL AND DAMPING"]
        SP1["Upper Hair Spring"]
        SP2["Lower Hair Spring"]
        FORMER["Aluminium Former (Eddy Current)"]
    end

    subgraph INDICATING["INDICATING SYSTEM"]
        SC["Calibrated Mirror Scale"]
    end

    PM --> PP
    PP --> IC
    IC -. surrounds .-> COIL
    COIL --> SP
    SP --> PTR
    SP1 -. attached to .-> SP
    SP2 -. attached to .-> SP
    COIL --- FORMER
    PTR --> SC

    style SC fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the actual physical layout, the **cylindrical soft iron core** sits *inside* the rectangular coil. The **soft iron pole pieces** are bonded to the magnet's ends and curve around the coil to ensure a **truly radial flux distribution** in the air gap. The aluminium former moves with the coil and, as it cuts magnetic flux lines, eddy currents are induced which oppose the motion — this is the **damping action**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the working principle of a PMMC instrument. Why is the magnetic field kept radial in such instruments?**

**Model Answer:**

The PMMC instrument works on the **motor principle** (also called the **D'Arsonval principle**). When a current-carrying moving coil is placed in a permanent magnetic field, it experiences a deflecting force (Lorentz force) given by $F = BIL$. The two vertical sides of the coil experience equal and opposite forces, forming a couple that produces a deflecting torque:

$$
T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A
$$

The magnetic field is kept **radial** (using a cylindrical soft iron core inside the coil and curved pole pieces) so that the field lines always remain **perpendicular to the coil sides** at every position of rotation. This ensures that the deflecting torque remains **constant and independent of the angular position** of the coil, resulting in a **uniform (linear) scale** for the instrument.

*(Examiner's note: 3 marks — 1 for principle, 1 for torque expression, 1 for radial field justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six advantages and three disadvantages of PMMC instruments.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages (any six):**
1. **High accuracy** and high sensitivity (typically ±1% to ±2% of full scale).
2. **Linear (uniform) scale** because of the radial field and direct proportionality $\theta \propto I$.
3. **Low power consumption** (suitable for high-resistance circuits).
4. **High torque-to-weight ratio**, so friction effects are negligible.
5. **Effective eddy-current damping** provided by the aluminium former.
6. **Not affected by stray magnetic fields** because the working field is very strong due to the permanent magnet.
7. Can be used as a **galvanometer, ammeter, or voltmeter** with simple shunts/multipliers.

**Disadvantages (any three):**
1. Can be used **only for DC measurements** (AC would produce no net deflection due to inertia of the moving system).
2. The **permanent magnet's strength may decrease with age** (magnetic deterioration) causing errors.
3. **More expensive** than moving-iron instruments.
4. The coil movement is **delicate** and can be damaged by mechanical shock or overload.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1.5 marks for six advantages and 1.5 marks for three disadvantages.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Choice: 1 × 14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **deflecting torque equation** and the **deflection angle equation** for a PMMC instrument. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PMMC ammeter has a coil of **80 turns**, wound on a former of dimensions **15 mm × 12 mm**. The coil is placed in a uniform radial magnetic flux density of **0.18 T**. The controlling spring constant is **$1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ N·m/rad**. Determine: (i) the full-scale deflection current if the pointer deflects by **90°** for full scale, and (ii) the resistance that must be connected in **parallel** with the meter movement to convert it into a **0–5 A ammeter**, given that the coil resistance is **200 Ω**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Torque Equations** *(7 marks — distribute as below)*

*Step 1: Force on conductor (1 mark)*
The force on a current-carrying conductor of effective length $l$ placed in a magnetic field of density $B$ is:
$$F = B \cdot I \cdot l$$

*Step 2: Force per coil side (1 mark)*
For a coil of $N$ turns, the total force on one vertical side (length $l$) of the coil is:
$$F_1 = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot l$$

*Step 3: Couple and torque (2 marks)*
Two equal and opposite forces separated by distance $d$ produce a couple with torque:
$$T_d = F_1 \cdot d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot l \cdot d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$$
where $A = l \cdot d$ is the area of the coil.

*Step 4: Deflection angle (3 marks)*
The hair spring exerts a controlling torque $T_c = K \cdot \theta$. At equilibrium, $T_d = T_c$:
$$N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A = K \cdot \theta$$
$$\theta = \frac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$$
This shows that $\theta \propto I$, hence the linear scale.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $N = 80$ turns
- Coil dimensions: $15 \text{ mm} \times 12 \text{ mm}$, so $A = 15 \times 12 = 180 \text{ mm}^2 = 180 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- $B = 0.18 \text{ T}$
- $K = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N·m/rad}$
- Full-scale deflection $\theta_{fs} = 90° = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad} \approx 1.5708 \text{ rad}$
- $R_c = 200 \;\Omega$
- Required range: $0$ to $5 \text{ A}$

**(i) Full-scale deflection current (3 marks):**

$$
I_{fs} = \frac{K \cdot \theta_{fs}}{N \cdot B \cdot A}
$$

$$
I_{fs} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5708}{80 \times 0.18 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
I_{fs} = \frac{2.3562 \times 10^{-6}}{2.592 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 9.09 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} \approx 0.91 \text{ mA}
$$

**(ii) Shunt resistance to extend range to 5 A (4 marks):**

Let $I_m = I_{fs} = 0.91 \text{ mA}$ be the meter current at full scale, and $I = 5 \text{ A}$ be the total current. The shunt current is:
$$
I_{sh} = I - I_m = 5 - 0.00091 \approx 4.99909 \text{ A}
$$

The shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ (in parallel with the coil) must satisfy:
$$
I_m \cdot R_c = I_{sh} \cdot R_{sh}
$$

$$
R_{sh} = \frac{I_m \cdot R_c}{I_{sh}} = \frac{0.00091 \times 200}{4.99909} \approx \frac{0.182}{4.999} \approx 0.0364 \;\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{sh} \approx 0.0364 \;\Omega}
$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a PMMC instrument**. List the functions of **(i)** the permanent magnet, **(ii)** the soft iron core, **(iii)** the hair spring, and **(iv)** the aluminium former. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **errors that occur in PMMC instruments**. State the various **compensations and remedies** adopted to minimize them. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working** *(7 marks)*

*(1 mark for diagram, 3 marks for construction, 3 marks for working)*

**Construction:** A PMMC instrument consists of five sub-systems:
- **Magnetic system** — a permanent magnet (Alnico/rare-earth) with soft iron pole pieces and a cylindrical soft iron core.
- **Moving system** — a rectangular coil of N turns of fine copper wire wound on an aluminium former, pivoted on jewelled bearings.
- **Control system** — two phosphor-bronze hair springs.
- **Damping system** — the aluminium former provides eddy-current damping.
- **Indicating system** — a lightweight aluminium pointer and a calibrated mirror scale.

**Working:** The current to be measured enters via the upper hair spring, flows through the coil, and returns via the lower spring. Each vertical side of the coil experiences a force $F = NBIl$, and the two forces form a couple producing a deflecting torque $T_d = NBIA$. The coil rotates until this torque is balanced by the spring's controlling torque $T_c = K\theta$, giving $\theta = (NBA/K) I$.

**Functions:**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| (i) Permanent Magnet | Provides a strong, stable **radial magnetic field** in the air gap — the working field for the motor principle. |
| (ii) Soft Iron Core | Concentrates flux, **reduces air-gap reluctance**, and forces the field to remain **truly radial** at all coil positions. |
| (iii) Hair Spring | Provides the **restoring (controlling) torque** $T_c = K\theta$ and acts as a **current lead** to the moving coil. |
| (iv) Aluminium Former | Hosts the coil; also acts as a **single-turn damping winding** to provide **eddy-current damping** ($T_d^{damp} = D \, d\theta/dt$). |

---

**(b) Errors in PMMC Instruments** *(7 marks — 3 for errors, 4 for remedies)*

**Common Errors:**

1. **Temperature Error** — Rise in ambient temperature increases coil resistance, reducing the deflecting torque (since $I$ decreases for a given voltage). Spring constant also changes.
2. **Error due to Weakening of the Permanent Magnet** — With age, the magnet's flux density **B** decreases, reducing $T_d = NBIA$ and hence the reading.
3. **Error due to Variation of Coil Resistance with Temperature** — Copper's resistance increases with temperature, affecting voltage measurement.
4. **Error due to Friction in Jewelled Bearings** — Mechanical friction in the bearings introduces a non-linear error.
5. **Error due to Stray Magnetic Fields** — Although PMMC is generally immune, very strong external fields can affect the working field.
6. **Error due to Non-Uniform Magnetic Field** — A weak magnet or saturation of pole pieces causes the field to deviate from radial, leading to a non-linear scale.

**Compensations / Remedies:**

1. **Swamping Resistor (for Temperature Error):** A **manganin shunt** (temperature coefficient nearly zero) is connected in series with the coil. As temperature rises, the total circuit resistance changes much less than the coil alone.
2. **Use of Age-Resistant Magnets:** **Alnico V** or **Cunife** alloys with high coercivity prevent magnetic deterioration. Modern **rare-earth magnets** are even more stable.
3. **Spring Materials with Low Temperature Coefficient:** Springs made of **Elinvar** (an Fe-Ni-Cr alloy) have a spring constant nearly independent of temperature.
4. **Use of Jewelled Bearings and Careful Balancing:** Minimises friction errors.
5. **Magnetic Shielding:** The instrument is enclosed in a **mu-metal** or soft-iron casing to shield against external fields.
6. **High-Quality Pole Pieces:** Properly shaped laminated pole pieces ensure a uniform radial field.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Read Carefully!)**
> 1. **Do not confuse "swamping resistor" with "shunt resistor."** A *swamping resistor* is connected in **series** with the coil to compensate for temperature. A *shunt resistor* is connected in **parallel** with the coil to extend the current range. Examiners deduct 1 mark for mixing these up.
> 2. **Always write the equation of motion (with $J, D, K$) only when the question specifically asks about dynamic behaviour or damping.** For static-deflection questions, stop at $\theta = (NBA/K) \cdot I$.
> 3. **Do not write $T_d = BINA$ with the letters in the wrong order.** The correct sequence is **N-B-I-A** — examiners catch this and deduct 0.5 marks.
> 4. **The Alnico magnet "ageing" error is NOT a friction error** — they are completely different phenomena.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Cheat Sheet — PMMC for KTU 2024**

- **Principle:** Motor principle / D'Arsonval / Lorentz force — *not* electromagnetic induction.
- **Master torque equation:** $T_d = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A$ — memorize verbatim.
- **Deflection equation:** $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$ — proves linear scale.
- **Radial field → Linear scale** — this is the single most important design feature.
- **Two active sides of coil:** Only the two vertical sides contribute to torque; horizontal sides produce no force.
- **Hair spring does three jobs:** (1) Provides controlling torque, (2) Acts as current lead, (3) Returns pointer to zero.
- **Aluminium former's dual role:** (1) Mechanical support for coil, (2) Provides eddy-current damping.
- **Damping type:** Eddy-current (electromagnetic) damping — *not* air friction or fluid friction.
- **PMMC is for DC only** — moving-iron instruments are used for AC measurements.
- **Range extension formulas (must remember):**
  - **Ammeter:** $R_{sh} = \dfrac{R_c \cdot I_m}{I - I_m}$, where $m = \dfrac{I}{I_m}$ is the multiplying factor.
  - **Voltmeter:** $R_{se} = (V - V_m) \cdot \dfrac{R_c}{V_m} = R_c \cdot (m - 1)$, where $m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$.
- **Sensitivity** = $\dfrac{1}{I_{fs}}$ in $\Omega/\text{V}$ — a 50 µA movement has 20 kΩ/V sensitivity.
- **Errors:** Temperature, magnet ageing, friction, spring softness, stray fields, non-uniform field.
- **Swamping resistor (manganin, in series)** solves temperature error; **shunt resistor (manganin, in parallel)** solves range extension.
- **Dynamic equation:** $J \theta'' + D \theta' + K \theta = NBIA$ — damping ratio $\zeta \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ for PMMC.
- **For KTU Board Exams:** Always state the principle first, then the torque equation, then the deflection equation, and finally a short note on radial field and eddy damping — that single structure fetches 12 out of 14 marks.
- **Common conversions to memorize:**
  - $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
  - $90° = \pi/2 \text{ rad} \approx 1.5708 \text{ rad}$
  - $1 \text{ Tesla} = 1 \text{ Wb/m}^2 = 10^4 \text{ Gauss}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PMMI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Permanent Magnet Moving Coil (PMMC) Instrument** — A PMMC instrument, also called a **D'Arsonval Galvanometer** or **Moving Coil Instrument**, is an electromechanical measuring device in which a current-carrying coil is suspended (or pivoted) in the uniform radial magnetic field of a permanent magnet. The interaction between the magnetic field and the coil current produces a mechanical deflecting torque, which is opposed by a control torque from a spiral spring, causing a pointer attached to the coil to deflect proportionally to the current. It functions exclusively on the **DC (Direct Current) principle** and is the most accurate analog DC meter ever developed for laboratory and industrial use.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, PMMC is treated as the **reference standard for all DC measurements**, against which the performance of other analog instruments is benchmarked.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reverse Motor" Intuition

Imagine a tiny **DC motor** that has been rewired to *not* spin, but instead to *twist gently against a spring*. Here is the analogy in plain English:

- The **permanent magnet** plays the role of the stator (fixed magnetic field, like Earth's pole).
- The **moving coil** plays the role of the rotor (carries current, experiences force).
- Instead of being free to rotate a full 360°, the coil is restrained by a **spiral hair spring** (like a tape measure spring).
- The harder the current pushes the coil, the more the spring stretches and the further the **pointer** swings across a calibrated scale.
- When current is removed, the spring pulls the pointer back to zero — just like a tape measure retracting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** The PMMC is essentially a *precision current-to-angle converter*. The deflection angle $\theta$ is **directly proportional** to the current $I$ flowing through the coil. This linearity is what makes it a "permanent" favorite of instrument designers.

---

## 1.3 Principal Symbols & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $B$ | Flux density in the air gap | Tesla (T) |
| $N$ | Number of turns in the moving coil | turns |
| $A$ | Effective area of the coil | $\text{m}^2$ |
| $I$ | Current through the coil | Ampere (A) |
| $K$ | Spring constant (torsional stiffness) | $\text{N·m/rad}$ |
| $\theta$ | Angular deflection of the pointer | radian (rad) |
| $R_m$ | Resistance of the moving coil | Ohm ($\Omega$) |
| $R_s$ | Series (shunt) multiplier resistance | Ohm ($\Omega$) |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board questions, students are expected to remember that the **magnetic field $B$** in a PMMC is of the order of **0.1 to 0.5 Wb/m² (T)** due to the use of high-energy Alnico / Alcomax permanent magnets, giving strong deflecting torque in a small space.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

| CO Code | Course Outcome Statement | Topic Coverage |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4** | Apply the principles of electromechanical indicating instruments for measurement of voltage, current, power and energy. | PMMC construction, working, errors, ammeter & voltmeter multipliers |
| **CO5** | Analyse the performance of analog meters under static and dynamic operating conditions. | Torque equation, sensitivity, swamping resistor, error analysis |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Architecture (Block-Level)

The PMMC is built from **four functional blocks**, each essential to its operation:

### Block A — Magnetic System (The Field Source)
- A pair of **permanent magnets** (Alnico / Alcomax / Rare-earth) shaped as curved **pole pieces**.
- A **soft-iron cylindrical core** is fixed centrally between the pole pieces.
- The geometry of pole pieces + core produces a **uniform radial magnetic field** in the narrow cylindrical air gap (typically 1–2 mm).
- Why radial? → The magnetic field is **always perpendicular to the coil sides** for *any* angular position. This guarantees the deflecting torque is constant throughout the pointer's travel (linear scale).

### Block B — Moving System (The Force Receiver)
- A rectangular coil of **fine insulated copper wire** wound on a thin aluminium former.
- The coil is pivoted on two polished jewelled bearings (sapphire/ruby) to minimize friction.
- The pointer is attached to the coil spindle, sweeping over a graduated scale.

### Block C — Control System (The Restoring Force)
- Two **spiral hair-springs** (phosphor-bronze) attached to the coil spindle.
- These springs provide the **controlling torque** proportional to deflection.
- They also serve as **current lead-in paths** to the moving coil (one spring for lead-in, the other for lead-out).

### Block D — Damping System (The Stabilizer)
- The coil is wound on an **aluminium former** which acts as a short-circuited secondary.
- As the coil moves in the magnetic field, **eddy currents are induced in the former**.
- By **Lenz's law**, these eddy currents oppose the motion → providing **eddy-current damping** (electromagnetic damping).
- This is *ideal* damping because it becomes zero when the pointer comes to rest (no current → no force).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four blocks work in a **closed loop**: Current → Coil in Field → Deflecting Torque → Pointer Deflection → Spring Counter-Torque → Equilibrium.

---

## 2.2 The Underlying Physics — "Why" and "How"

**Why does the coil move?**
Each vertical side of the rectangular coil of length $l$ carrying current $I$ in a radial field $B$ experiences a **Laplace force** $F = BIl$. The two vertical sides produce a **couple** because they are on opposite sides of the spindle, separated by the breadth $b$. The deflecting torque is therefore $T_d = F \times b = BIl \times b \times N$ (for $N$ turns). Since $A = l \times b$ (coil area), this simplifies to:

$$T_d = N B I A$$

**How is the pointer stopped?**
The spiral spring exerts a **restoring torque** proportional to the angular twist $\theta$:

$$T_c = K \theta$$

**How is the steady-state deflection obtained?**
At equilibrium, the **net torque on the coil must be zero** (Newton's first law for rotation). Setting $T_d = T_c$:

$$N B I A = K \theta$$

Solving for the deflection $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{N B A}{K} \cdot I$$

This single linear equation is the **heart of every PMMC design problem** in KTU exams.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU board exam numericals on PMMC. Memorize it before solving any problem.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deflecting Torque | $T_d = N B I A$ | Force on coil in radial field | N·m |
| 2 | Controlling Torque | $T_c = K \theta$ | Spiral spring restoring torque | N·m |
| 3 | Steady-state deflection | $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$ | Final pointer position | rad |
| 4 | Current Sensitivity | $S_I = \dfrac{\theta}{I} = \dfrac{NBA}{K}$ | Deflection per ampere | rad/A |
| 5 | Voltage Sensitivity | $S_V = \dfrac{S_I}{R_m + R_s}$ | Deflection per volt | rad/V |
| 6 | Ammeter Shunt (Multiplier) | $R_s = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$, where $m = \dfrac{I}{I_m}$ | Extends current range | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Voltmeter Series (Multiplier) | $R_s = R_m (m - 1)$, where $m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$ | Extends voltage range | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Multiplying Power | $m = \dfrac{I}{I_m} = 1 + \dfrac{R_s}{R_m}$ | Range multiplication factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | Power Loss in Meter | $P = I_m^2 (R_m + R_s)$ | Heat dissipated | W |
| 10 | Temperature Error Compensation | Use **swamping resistor** of **manganin** ($\alpha \approx 0$) in series | Cancels Cu-coil $\alpha \approx 0.004/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, all vertical-bar absolute values have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent Markdown table parser breakage. Do **not** write $\vert x \vert$ using the keyboard pipe `|` inside a table cell.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Use of PMMC |
|---|---|
| **DC Ammeters & Voltmeters** | Range extension by shunt/series multipliers |
| **Galvanometers** | Null-detection in Wheatstone, Kelvin, Carey-Foster bridges |
| **Aircraft & Automotive Dashboards** | Battery current/voltage indicators (DC only) |
| **Multimeters (Analog Type)** | DC voltage and current measurement section |
| **Recording Instruments** | Strip-chart recorders, oscillograph galvanometers |
| **Industrial Process Control** | DC signal indicators in 4–20 mA loops (historically) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, the **swamping resistor concept** is a favourite 7-mark question topic, especially with numericals on temperature compensation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Deflecting Torque Equation

Let us derive the fundamental PMMC torque equation from **first principles of electromagnetic force**.

### Assumptions
1. The magnetic flux density $B$ in the air gap is **uniform and radial**.
2. The coil has $N$ turns, each of effective length $l$ and breadth $b$.
3. The current $I$ is steady (DC) and flows continuously through the coil.
4. The coil is pivoted about its central axis, perpendicular to the magnetic field lines.

### Step 1 — Force on a single straight conductor
For a straight conductor of length $l$ carrying current $I$ placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of flux density $B$, the **Laplace force** is:

$$F = B I l \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

The direction of this force is given by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule** and is perpendicular to both the current direction and the field direction.

### Step 2 — Force on one vertical side of the coil
The coil has two vertical sides (let's call them AB and CD), each of length $l$ and carrying the same current $I$. Since the field is *radial*, the two sides experience forces in **opposite directions**, producing a **torque couple** about the spindle.

Force on side AB: $F_1 = B I l$ (radially outward)
Force on side CD: $F_2 = B I l$ (radially inward, opposite direction)

### Step 3 — Moment of the couple
The two forces are separated by a perpendicular distance equal to the **breadth $b$** of the coil. The torque produced by a couple equals one force times the perpendicular distance between the lines of action:

$$T_d \text{ (per turn)} = F \times b = B I l \times b = B I A$$

where $A = l \times b$ is the **effective area enclosed by one turn** of the coil.

### Step 4 — Accounting for $N$ turns
The coil has $N$ identical turns, all carrying the same current in the same direction (series-connected). Hence, the total force on each vertical side is $N$ times that of a single turn, and the total deflecting torque is:

$$\boxed{T_d = N B I A}$$

### Step 5 — Equilibrium with controlling torque
The spiral springs exert a restoring torque proportional to the angle of twist:

$$T_c = K \theta$$

At steady state, the algebraic sum of torques about the pivot is zero:

$$T_d = T_c$$

$$N B I A = K \theta$$

### Step 6 — Final linear deflection equation
Solving for the angular deflection $\theta$:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{N B A}{K} \cdot I = S_I \cdot I}$$

This is the **Master Equation of PMMC** — a direct, linear, single-line relationship between the current and the pointer deflection. The constant $S_I = \dfrac{NBA}{K}$ is called the **current sensitivity** of the instrument.

### Step 7 — Why the scale is uniform
Because $\theta$ is a **linear function of $I$** (no $I^2$ or $\sqrt{I}$ terms), equal increments of current produce equal increments of pointer movement. Therefore, the PMMC scale is **uniformly divided** — a major advantage over Moving Iron instruments (where the scale is cramped at the low end).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Ammeter Shunt (Multiplier) Resistance

Suppose the basic PMMC movement has a full-scale current $I_m$ and coil resistance $R_m$. We want to extend its range to measure a larger current $I$. A **shunt resistor $R_s$** is connected in **parallel** with the moving coil.

### Circuit Analysis
Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law** at the junction:

$$I = I_m + I_s$$

where $I_s$ is the current through the shunt. Since the shunt and the coil are in parallel, they have the **same voltage drop**:

$$I_m R_m = I_s R_s$$

Substituting $I_s = I - I_m$:

$$I_m R_m = (I - I_m) R_s$$

Solving for $R_s$:

$$R_s = \frac{I_m R_m}{I - I_m} = \frac{R_m}{\frac{I}{I_m} - 1}$$

Let $m = \dfrac{I}{I_m}$ be the **multiplying power**. Then:

$$\boxed{R_s = \frac{R_m}{m - 1}}$$

Equivalent form:

$$\boxed{m = 1 + \frac{R_m}{R_s} = \frac{R_s + R_m}{R_s}}$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
**Q:** A PMMC movement with full-scale $I_m = 50\,\mu\text{A}$ and $R_m = 1000\,\Omega$ is to be used as an ammeter to measure up to $5\,\text{A}$. Find the shunt resistance.

**Solution:**

$$m = \frac{I}{I_m} = \frac{5}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 100{,}000$$

$$R_s = \frac{R_m}{m - 1} = \frac{1000}{100000 - 1} \approx 0.01\,\Omega$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Voltmeter Series (Multiplier) Resistance

To extend the voltage range of a PMMC, a **high-value series resistor $R_s$** is added in series with the moving coil.

### Circuit Analysis
The full-scale current $I_m$ flows through the series combination. The total voltage drop across the meter is $V$:

$$V = I_m (R_m + R_s)$$

Solving for $R_s$:

$$\boxed{R_s = \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m = R_m \left(\frac{V}{V_m} - 1\right) = R_m (m - 1)}$$

where $m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$ is the **multiplying power** for voltage.

### Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
**Q:** A PMMC meter with $I_m = 1\,\text{mA}$ and $R_m = 100\,\Omega$ is to be used to measure up to $10\,\text{V}$. Find the series multiplier.

**Solution:**

$$R_s = \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m = \frac{10}{1 \times 10^{-3}} - 100 = 10{,}000 - 100 = 9900\,\Omega$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Temperature Error and Swamping Resistor

The moving coil is wound with **copper wire**, which has a temperature coefficient of approximately:

$$\alpha_{Cu} = 0.004 / {}^{\circ}\text{C} = 0.4\% \text{ per }^{\circ}\text{C}$$

When the ambient temperature rises, $R_m$ increases, so the coil current $I_m = \dfrac{V}{R_m + R_s}$ **decreases**, causing the pointer to read **lower than actual** — a **negative temperature error**.

For a voltmeter:

$$\text{Error} = \frac{I_{m,hot} - I_{m,cold}}{I_{m,cold}} = \frac{\frac{V}{R_{m,h} + R_s} - \frac{V}{R_{m,c} + R_s}}{\frac{V}{R_{m,c} + R_s}}$$

The error is minimized by making $R_s \gg R_m$ and using a **swamping resistor** made of **manganin** (or constantan), whose $\alpha \approx 0.00001 / {}^{\circ}\text{C}$ — practically zero.

In KTU exams, the **swamping resistor** is added in **series with the moving coil** *inside the meter case* (not the external multiplier). It increases the effective series resistance so that the temperature-induced change in $R_m$ becomes a negligible fraction of the total.

### Design Criterion
The temperature error should be less than 1% for a 10°C rise. The required ratio is:

$$\frac{\Delta R_m / R_m}{\Delta R_{total} / R_{total}} \leq 0.01$$

$$\frac{\alpha_{Cu} \cdot \Delta T \cdot R_m}{\alpha_{total} \cdot \Delta T \cdot R_{total}} \leq 0.01$$

$$\frac{0.004 \times R_m}{0.00001 \times R_{total}} \leq 0.01 \Rightarrow R_{total} \geq 400\,R_m$$

Hence, a swamping resistor of approximately **399 × R_m** (manganin) is used.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PMMC vs. Moving Iron (MI)

| Property | PMMC | Moving Iron |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Principle | Current in magnetic field | Magnetization of soft iron |
| Quantity Measured | DC only (or AC with rectifier) | AC and DC (RMS for AC) |
| Scale | **Uniform** | Non-uniform (cramped at low end) |
| Accuracy | **± 1 to 2%** (high) | ± 5 to 10% (moderate) |
| Power Consumption | **Very low** (25–200 μW) | Higher (0.25–1 W) |
| Damping | Eddy current (ideal) | Air friction |
| Cost | **High** | Low |
| Frequency Range | DC only (without rectifier) | 0–1000 Hz |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PMMC Construction Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph MAG[Block A — Magnetic System]
        M1["PERMANENT MAGNET ALNICO POLE PIECE"]
        M2["SOFT IRON CYLINDRICAL CORE"]
        M3["RADIAL UNIFORM AIR GAP FIELD B"]
        M1 --> M3
        M2 --> M3
    end

    subgraph MOV[Block B — Moving System]
        C1["RECTANGULAR MOVING COIL N TURNS"]
        C2["ALUMINIUM FORMER"]
        C3["POINTER WITH COUNTERWEIGHT"]
        C4["JEWELLED PIVOT BEARINGS"]
        C1 --> C3
        C2 --> C1
        C4 --> C1
    end

    subgraph CON[Block C — Control System]
        S1["UPPER HAIR SPRING K1"]
        S2["LOWER HAIR SPRING K2"]
        S3["TORQUE EQUAL TO K TIMES THETA"]
        S1 --> S3
        S2 --> S3
        S3 --> C1
    end

    subgraph DAM[Block D — Damping System]
        D1["EDDY CURRENTS IN ALUMINIUM FORMER"]
        D2["LENZ LAW OPPOSING MOTION"]
        D3["ELECTROMAGNETIC DAMPING"]
        D1 --> D2
        D2 --> D3
        D3 --> C1
    end

    M3 --> C1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four sub-graphs above map the four functional blocks of a PMMC instrument. The central node (the moving coil `C1`) is the common interaction point for all four systems.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Working Principle Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["START — DC Current I enters coil via spring leads"] --> B["Current I flows in coil placed in radial field B"]
    B --> C["Laplace Force F equals B I l on each vertical side"]
    C --> D["Deflecting Torque Td equals N B I A on coil spindle"]
    D --> E{"Deflection angle theta in steady state"}
    E -->|Td greater than Tc| F["Coil rotates further theta increases"]
    E -->|Td equals Tc| G["EQUILIBRIUM — pointer settles at final position"]
    F --> H["Spiral spring stretches restoring torque rises linearly"]
    H --> E
    G --> I["Pointer indicates theta proportional to I on uniform scale"]
    I --> J["Eddy current damping in Al former stops pointer oscillation"]
    J --> K["END — Reading displayed on calibrated scale"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Ammeter & Voltmeter Configurations

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph AMM[Ammeter Configuration]
        A1["External Current I"] --> A2["Shunt Resistor Rs in parallel"]
        A2 --> A3["Moving Coil Rm"]
        A2 -.->|"Im flows through coil"| A3
        A1 --> A2
    end

    subgraph VOLT[Voltmeter Configuration]
        V1["External Voltage V"] --> V2["Series Multiplier Rs"]
        V2 --> V3["Moving Coil Rm"]
        V3 -.->|"Same current Im"| V2
        V1 --> V2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the ammeter, the shunt $R_s$ is **very small** (fractions of an ohm for high current ranges). In the voltmeter, the series multiplier $R_s$ is **very large** (kilo-ohms to mega-ohms for high voltage ranges).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Error Analysis Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    E["PMMC ERROR SOURCES"] --> E1["MAGNETIC ERROR — Aging of Alnico magnet reduces B"]
    E --> E2["TEMPERATURE ERROR — Cu coil resistance rises with temperature"]
    E --> E3["SPRING ERROR — Permanent set of hair spring"]
    E --> E4["FRICTION ERROR — Dirty jewel bearings"]
    E --> E5["READING ERROR — Parallax in pointer position"]
    E1 --> S1["Solution — Use temperature compensated Alcomax"]
    E2 --> S2["Solution — Add swamping resistor of manganin"]
    E3 --> S3["Solution — Periodic calibration"]
    E4 --> S4["Solution — Use good quality jewels"]
    E5 --> S5["Solution — Use mirror behind scale"]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**State and explain the principle of operation of a PMMC instrument. Why is the scale of a PMMC meter uniform?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The PMMC instrument works on the principle that **"a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a mechanical force."** When a current $I$ flows through a coil of $N$ turns and area $A$ placed in a uniform radial magnetic field $B$, a deflecting torque is produced:

$$T_d = N B I A$$

This torque is opposed by the controlling torque of the spring $T_c = K \theta$. At equilibrium:

$$\theta = \frac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$$

Since the deflection $\theta$ is **directly proportional** to the current $I$ (linear relationship with no square-root or square terms), equal increments of current produce equal increments of pointer movement. Hence, the scale is **uniformly divided** — unlike Moving Iron instruments where $\theta \propto I^2$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of PMMC instruments.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **High accuracy** — errors are of the order of 1–2% of full-scale deflection.
2. **Uniform scale** — linear relationship between current and deflection.
3. **Low power consumption** — typical coil power is 25 μW to 200 μW.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Operates only on DC** — cannot measure AC directly (needs a rectifier bridge).
2. **Higher cost** — moving-coil construction with permanent magnets and jewelled bearings is expensive compared to Moving Iron instruments. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### (a) Derive the torque equation of a PMMC instrument. Explain why the magnetic field is made radial. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Consider a rectangular coil of $N$ turns, each of length $l$ and breadth $b$, suspended in a uniform radial magnetic field of density $B$, carrying a current $I$.

**Step 1 — Force on each vertical side:**
The two vertical sides AB and CD of length $l$ each experience a force:
$$F = B I l \quad \text{(Newtons)}$$

The forces on the two sides are **equal in magnitude and opposite in direction** (because the current in them flows in opposite directions). They act along the **perpendicular distance $b$** apart, producing a **couple** (rotational torque). **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Torque of the couple:**
For a single turn, the moment of the couple is:
$$T_{d,\text{1 turn}} = F \times b = B I l b = B I A$$

where $A = l \times b$ is the area of the coil. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Total deflecting torque for N turns:**
Since all $N$ turns are connected in series and carry the same current, the forces add up:

$$\boxed{T_d = N B I A} \quad \text{(Newton-metres)}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Why radial field?**
In a non-radial field, the effective component of $B$ perpendicular to the coil side would depend on the angular position $\theta$, making $T_d = N B I A \cos\theta$ or similar. This would make the deflection non-linear and the scale non-uniform. **(1 mark)**

By making the pole pieces **curved (concentric with the cylindrical iron core)**, the magnetic field lines are forced to be **radial at all positions of the coil's rotation**. This guarantees that the field $B$ is **always perpendicular to the coil sides** regardless of pointer position. Hence, the effective perpendicular component of $B$ remains constant, $T_d$ remains constant per unit current, and the scale is **uniformly linear**. **(2 marks)**

---

#### (b) A PMMC instrument has a coil of 100 turns, area 4 cm², suspended in a uniform radial flux density of 0.1 Wb/m². The spring constant is 5 × 10⁻⁶ N·m/rad. If the coil carries a current of 5 mA, find the angular deflection. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Number of turns: $N = 100$
- Area: $A = 4\,\text{cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$
- Flux density: $B = 0.1\,\text{Wb/m}^2$
- Spring constant: $K = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{N·m/rad}$
- Current: $I = 5\,\text{mA} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}$

**Step 1 — Deflecting Torque:**
$$T_d = N B I A$$
$$T_d = 100 \times 0.1 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$T_d = 2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{N·m} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Controlling Torque at equilibrium:**
$$T_c = K \theta$$
At steady state: $T_d = T_c$
$$2 \times 10^{-5} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times \theta \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for deflection:**
$$\theta = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} = 4\,\text{rad} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees (KTU convention):**
$$\theta = 4 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 4 \times 57.296 = 229.18° \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A deflection of 229° exceeds 180°, which is impossible for a pointer. This indicates that the meter is **over-ranged**. To bring the full-scale deflection to a typical 90° (π/2 rad), the spring constant or coil area would need to be redesigned. **(3 marks)** — full marks for stating this design interpretation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### (a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a PMMC voltmeter. Derive the expression for the series multiplier resistance. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (with reference to the diagrams in Section 4):**
A PMMC voltmeter consists of:
1. A **PMMC movement** (coil + magnet + spring + pointer) as the indicator.
2. A **high-value series multiplier resistor $R_s$** connected in series with the moving coil.
3. The combination is enclosed in a calibrated case with a graduated scale. **(1 mark)**

**Working:**
- The unknown DC voltage $V$ is applied across the series combination of the multiplier $R_s$ and the moving coil $R_m$.
- The current through the meter is $I = \dfrac{V}{R_s + R_m}$.
- This current produces a deflection $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$, proportional to the applied voltage.
- The scale is calibrated directly in **volts**. **(1 mark)**

**Derivation of Series Multiplier Resistance:**
For a basic PMMC movement, the full-scale voltage is:
$$V_m = I_m R_m$$

To extend the range to a higher voltage $V$, a multiplier $R_s$ is added in series such that the same current $I_m$ flows through both $R_s$ and $R_m$ at full scale:

$$V = I_m (R_s + R_m) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Solving for $R_s$:

$$R_s = \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

Let $m = \dfrac{V}{V_m} = \dfrac{V}{I_m R_m}$ be the multiplying factor. Then:

$$\boxed{R_s = R_m (m - 1)} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Material of the Multiplier:**
The multiplier is made of **manganin** (or constantan) — an alloy with very low temperature coefficient ($\alpha \approx 0.00001/^{\circ}\text{C}$) — to minimize temperature error. **(1 mark)**

---

#### (b) A basic PMMC movement with internal resistance 100 Ω and full-scale deflection current of 1 mA is to be converted into a multi-range voltmeter with ranges 10 V, 50 V, and 100 V. Calculate the required multiplier resistors and the overall voltmeter sensitivity. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R_m = 100\,\Omega$
- $I_m = 1\,\text{mA} = 10^{-3}\,\text{A}$
- Full-scale voltage: $V_m = I_m R_m = 100 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1\,\text{V}$

**Step 1 — Voltmeter Sensitivity:**
$$S_V = \frac{1}{I_m} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000\,\Omega/\text{V} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Multiplier for 10 V range:**
$$m_1 = \frac{V_1}{V_m} = \frac{10}{0.1} = 100$$
$$R_{s1} = R_m (m_1 - 1) = 100 \times (100 - 1) = 9900\,\Omega = 9.9\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Multiplier for 50 V range:**
$$m_2 = \frac{50}{0.1} = 500$$
$$R_{s2} = R_m (m_2 - 1) = 100 \times (500 - 1) = 49{,}900\,\Omega = 49.9\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For multi-range voltmeters, the multipliers are calculated **from the common reference point (the meter terminal)**, not cumulatively. Hence each $R_s$ is referenced to the moving coil alone, not to the previous multiplier.

**Step 4 — Multiplier for 100 V range:**
$$m_3 = \frac{100}{0.1} = 1000$$
$$R_{s3} = R_m (m_3 - 1) = 100 \times (1000 - 1) = 99{,}900\,\Omega = 99.9\,\text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Verification using direct formula:**
$$R_{si} = \frac{V_i}{I_m} - R_m$$

For 10 V: $R_{s1} = \dfrac{10}{10^{-3}} - 100 = 10{,}000 - 100 = 9900\,\Omega$ ✓
For 50 V: $R_{s2} = \dfrac{50}{10^{-3}} - 100 = 49{,}900\,\Omega$ ✓
For 100 V: $R_{s3} = \dfrac{100}{10^{-3}} - 100 = 99{,}900\,\Omega$ ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Overall Voltmeter Resistance:**
- On 10 V range: $R_{T1} = R_m + R_{s1} = 100 + 9900 = 10{,}000\,\Omega = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$
- On 50 V range: $R_{T2} = 100 + 49{,}900 = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$
- On 100 V range: $R_{T3} = 100 + 99{,}900 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$

This confirms the constant sensitivity of $1000\,\Omega/\text{V}$. **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion** — Area must be in $\text{m}^2$, current in amperes, $B$ in Tesla. Forgetting to convert $\text{cm}^2 \to \text{m}^2$ is the most common error in PMMC numericals.
> 2. **Confusing ammeter and voltmeter multipliers** — Ammeter uses **shunt** (parallel, very small resistance), voltmeter uses **series** (very large resistance). Marks are slashed if the student writes $R_s = R_m (m-1)$ for an ammeter.
> 3. **Forgetting the radial field justification** — In 7-mark construction questions, students often state "the field is radial" without explaining *why* (i.e., to keep torque independent of $\theta$, giving uniform scale).
> 4. **Confusing eddy-current damping with air friction damping** — PMMC uses **eddy-current (electromagnetic) damping** via the aluminium former. Writing "air damping" costs marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning the function of the springs as current leads** — Many students miss the fact that the two spiral springs also serve as the **incoming and outgoing current paths** to the moving coil.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Quick Reference**

- ✅ **PMMC** = **P**ermanent **M**agnet **M**oving **C**oil; also called **D'Arsonval** movement.
- ✅ Operating principle: **Force on current-carrying coil in magnetic field** (DC only).
- ✅ **Master equation:** $\theta = \dfrac{NBA}{K} \cdot I$ — linear, uniform scale.
- ✅ **Deflecting torque:** $T_d = N B I A$ (N·m).
- ✅ **Controlling torque:** $T_c = K \theta$ (N·m), provided by **phosphor-bronze** hair springs.
- ✅ **Damping:** **Eddy-current (electromagnetic) damping** via aluminium former.
- ✅ **Current sensitivity:** $S_I = \dfrac{NBA}{K}$ (rad/A).
- ✅ **Voltage sensitivity:** $S_V = \dfrac{S_I}{R_m + R_s}$ (rad/V) or simply $\dfrac{1}{I_m}$ ($\Omega/\text{V}$).
- ✅ **Ammeter shunt:** $R_s = \dfrac{R_m}{m - 1}$ where $m = \dfrac{I}{I_m}$.
- ✅ **Voltmeter multiplier:** $R_s = R_m (m - 1)$ where $m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$.
- ✅ **Multipliers are made of manganin** ($\alpha \approx 0$) for **temperature stability**.
- ✅ **Swamping resistor** = small manganin resistance in **series with the moving coil** (inside the case) to reduce temperature error; required $R_{swamp} \approx 399\,R_m$.
- ✅ **Errors in PMMC:** (1) **Magnetic aging** of Alnico, (2) **Temperature** of Cu coil, (3) **Spring fatigue**, (4) **Friction** in jewels, (5) **Parallax** in reading.
- ✅ **Advantages:** High accuracy (1–2%), uniform scale, low power consumption, effective magnetic shielding, free from hysteresis & eddy-current losses in the working coil.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** DC only, expensive, errors due to magnet aging, requires careful handling.
- ✅ **Used in:** DC ammeters, voltmeters, galvanometers, recorders, aircraft instruments, and as the DC section of analog multimeters.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 ESE, expect at least **one 7-mark question** on derivation (torque or multiplier) and **one 3-mark question** on construction or advantages.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrodynamometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electrodynamometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrodynamometer (Electrodynamic Instrument):** A measuring instrument whose operating principle is based on the **electromagnetic interaction (force)** between two current-carrying coils — a *fixed coil* and a *moving coil* — where the **deflecting torque is proportional to the product of the currents** flowing through the two coils. It is one of the most accurate instruments for AC and DC measurements, with **true RMS response** and applicability as an ammeter, voltmeter, and most importantly, a **wattmeter**.

The instrument is fundamentally a **moving-coil device** where the permanent magnet of a PMMC instrument is replaced by one or two *electromagnetic coils*. Because the field is produced electromagnetically (not by a permanent magnet), the torque is the **vector product of the two MMFs**, making the instrument a true **"square-law"** device with two input quantities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Two-Magnet Tug-of-War" Analogy**
> Imagine two bar magnets floating in space. The *fixed magnet* represents the **fixed (current) coil**, and the *floating magnet* represents the **moving (potential) coil**. If you feed current into both magnets, they will try to align themselves in a particular direction. The *stronger* the current in the fixed magnet, the *harder* the moving magnet is pulled. The *stronger* the current in the moving magnet, the *heavier* the pull it feels. The angle to which the moving magnet rotates is therefore proportional to the **product of the two currents** — not their sum, not their difference, but their **multiplication**.

This is the soul of an electrodynamometer: **the instrument multiplies two electrical quantities**. When the second quantity is the supply voltage, the device becomes a **wattmeter** — it multiplies voltage and current and gives **real power**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **SI Unit of Deflecting Torque:** Newton-meter (N·m)
- **Mutual Inductance (M):** Henry (H)
- **Operating Frequency Range:** Typically **DC to 1 kHz** (air-core); reduced for iron-core
- **Accuracy Class:** Up to **±0.1% to ±0.5%** for precision air-core laboratory wattmeters
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** **20 °C** (for spring and coil resistance compensation)

### 1.4 Salient Features at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Dual-input** instrument (unlike PMMC which is single-input)
> - **True RMS** indication for AC
> - **Universal** (works on both AC and DC without recalibration)
> - Most accurate commercial **wattmeter** for power frequency
> - Square law response → non-uniform scale
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy

An electrodynamometer is built from the following subsystems:

| Subsystem | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Field system** | Two fixed coils (current coils) | Produce a uniform magnetic field proportional to load current |
| **Moving system** | One moving coil (potential/voltage coil) | Carries current proportional to voltage; mounted on a pivoted spindle |
| **Control** | Two spiral control springs (phosphor-bronze) | Provide controlling torque $T_c = K_c \cdot \theta$ |
| **Damping** | Air dashpot (piston-in-chamber) | Provides eddy-free damping |
| **Deflection Indicator** | Lightweight pointer + mirror + scale | Indicates the measured quantity |
| **Shielding** | Mumetal magnetic shield | Minimizes stray field errors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fixed coil is generally **split into two equal halves** mounted on either side of the moving coil. This creates a *near-uniform* field in the central region where the moving coil sits, and also makes the instrument more *compact* and *physically balanced*.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **Current enters the fixed (current) coil** → produces a magnetic field $B \propto I_1$.
2. **Current enters the moving (potential) coil** → the conductors of the moving coil experience a force $F = B \cdot I_2 \cdot L$ (Lorentz force on current-carrying conductors).
3. The forces create a **couple (torque)** on the moving system spindle.
4. The moving coil rotates against the **control springs**, which develop a restoring torque proportional to angular deflection.
5. **Equilibrium** is reached when the deflecting torque equals the controlling torque.
6. The pointer indicates the **steady-state deflection $\theta$**, which is proportional to the product of the two coil currents.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total magnetic energy stored | $W$ | $W = \tfrac{1}{2}L_1 I_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}L_2 I_2^{2} + M I_1 I_2$ | Joules (J) |
| 2 | **DC Deflecting Torque** | $T_d$ | $T_d = I_1 \cdot I_2 \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | N·m |
| 3 | **AC Instantaneous Torque** | $T_d(t)$ | $T_d(t) = i_1(t)\cdot i_2(t)\cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | N·m |
| 4 | **AC Average Deflecting Torque** | $T_d$ | $T_d = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | N·m; $I_1, I_2$ in RMS |
| 5 | Controlling Torque | $T_c$ | $T_c = K_c \cdot \theta$ | N·m; $K_c$ = spring constant |
| 6 | Damping Torque | $T_D$ | $T_D = D \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | N·m |
| 7 | **Equilibrium Deflection** | $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{I_1 I_2 \cos\phi}{K_c} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ | radians |
| 8 | **As Wattmeter** — Deflection | $\theta$ | $\theta \propto V \cdot I_L \cos\phi = P$ | Real Power |
| 9 | Mutual Inductance variation rate | $dM/d\theta$ | Depends on coil geometry | H/rad |
| 10 | **Multiplying Power (Wattmeter)** | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{Z_p \cdot K_c \cdot \theta}{dM/d\theta}$ | Watts (W) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention for the phase angle $\phi$:** In AC operation, $\phi$ is the **phase angle between the two coil currents** $I_1$ and $I_2$. In a wattmeter, this equals the **load power-factor angle** (assuming the potential coil is purely resistive).

### 2.4 Why "Square Law" Response?

For DC operation: $\theta \propto I_1 \cdot I_2$.
If both currents are the *same current* $I$ (e.g., an ammeter configuration): $\theta \propto I^{2}$.
This **square-law** response gives the instrument a **non-linear, cramped scale** at low values (a key disadvantage, since the pointer barely moves for small currents and "shoots up" near full-scale).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision wattmeters** in electrical calibration laboratories (reference standard).
- **Power-factor meters** and **frequency meters** (in special configurations).
- **Polyphase wattmeters** for 3-phase power measurement.
- **Transfer instruments**: calibrated on DC, then used on AC with equal accuracy.
- **Reference standard** for AC-DC comparison measurements.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the General Deflecting Torque

We start from the energy-balance principle. A current-carrying system stores magnetic energy; when the system is allowed to mechanically reconfigure (the moving coil rotates), the rate of change of stored magnetic energy with respect to angular position *equals the mechanical torque developed*.

**Step 1 — State the energy stored in the coupled-coil system.**

The two coils form a magnetically coupled system. Total magnetic field energy:

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^{2} + \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^{2} + M I_1 I_2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the deflecting torque from energy co-energy.**

For a system with constant-current excitation, the deflecting torque is the *partial derivative* of stored energy with respect to the mechanical coordinate (here, angular position $\theta$):

$$
T_d = \left. \frac{\partial W}{\partial \theta} \right|_{I_1, I_2 = \text{const}}
$$

**Step 3 — Carry out the differentiation term-by-term.**

Differentiating $W$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$
T_d = \frac{1}{2} I_1^{2} \frac{dL_1}{d\theta} + \frac{1}{2} I_2^{2} \frac{dL_2}{d\theta} + I_1 I_2 \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the geometric simplification.**

In a properly designed electrodynamometer, the **fixed coil geometry and the moving coil geometry are independent of $\theta$** — only the *mutual* coupling $M(\theta)$ changes as the moving coil rotates. Therefore:

$$
\frac{dL_1}{d\theta} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dL_2}{d\theta} = 0
$$

This leaves the elegant single-term expression:

$$
T_d = I_1 I_2 \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

This is the **fundamental torque equation** of the electrodynamometer.

**Step 5 — Realize the engineering meaning of $dM/d\theta$.**

The factor $\dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ depends only on **coil geometry, turns, and dimensions** — it is a *constant of the instrument* (let's denote it $K_d$). Hence:

$$
T_d = K_d \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2
$$

The torque is strictly **proportional to the product of the two coil currents**.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation for AC Operation (Average Torque)

**Step 1 — Express the instantaneous currents.**

Let the two coil currents vary sinusoidally with a phase difference $\phi$:

$$
i_1(t) = I_{m1}\sin(\omega t)
$$
$$
i_2(t) = I_{m2}\sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

**Step 2 — Form the instantaneous torque.**

Substituting into the torque equation:

$$
T_d(t) = i_1(t) \cdot i_2(t) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$
$$
T_d(t) = I_{m1} I_{m2} \sin(\omega t) \sin(\omega t - \phi) \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the trigonometric product-to-sum identity.**

$$
\sin A \cdot \sin B = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos(A - B) - \cos(A + B)\bigr]
$$

Applying with $A = \omega t$ and $B = \omega t - \phi$:

$$
T_d(t) = \frac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \bigl[\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\bigr]
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the two torque components.**

- **Steady (mean) component:** $\dfrac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \cos\phi$ — produces useful deflection.
- **Pulsating (double-frequency) component:** $-\dfrac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \cos(2\omega t - \phi)$ — averages to **zero** over one full cycle, and is largely absorbed by the **mechanical inertia** of the moving system.

**Step 5 — Average over one complete cycle.**

Since $\langle \cos(2\omega t - \phi) \rangle = 0$ over $0$ to $2\pi/\omega$:

$$
T_{d,\text{avg}} = \frac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

**Step 6 — Convert peak values to RMS values.**

Using $I_{m} = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{rms}$:

$$
\frac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} = \frac{(\sqrt{2} I_1)(\sqrt{2} I_2)}{2} = I_1 I_2
$$

Therefore, the **AC average deflecting torque is**:

$$
T_{d,\text{avg}} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

This is the second **key equation** of the electrodynamometer and forms the basis of its **wattmeter** application.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Wattmeter Deflection (Application)

**Step 1 — Identify the two coil currents in a wattmeter.**

- **Current (fixed) coil** carries the **load current**: $I_1 = I_L$.
- **Potential (moving) coil** has a high non-inductive resistance $R_p$ in series, so the current is:
$$
I_2 = \frac{V}{Z_p} \approx \frac{V}{R_p} \quad (\text{since } R_p \gg X_p)
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the phase relationship.**

- The phase angle between $V$ and $I_L$ is the **load power-factor angle** $\phi$.
- The current $I_2$ is essentially **in phase with $V$** (because $R_p$ dominates).
- Therefore, the **angle between $I_1$ and $I_2$ is $\phi$**.

**Step 3 — Apply the AC torque equation.**

$$
T_d = I_L \cdot \frac{V}{R_p} \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the equilibrium condition $T_d = T_c = K_c \theta$.**

$$
K_c \theta = \frac{V I_L \cos\phi}{R_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange for deflection.**

$$
\theta = \frac{1}{K_c R_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot V I_L \cos\phi
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the real power term.**

Since **real power** $P = V I_L \cos\phi$:

$$
\theta = \underbrace{\frac{1}{K_c R_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}}_{\text{instrument constant } K_w} \cdot P
$$

So:
$$
\theta = K_w \cdot P
$$

**The deflection is directly proportional to real power.** The instrument reads **true average power**, regardless of waveform distortion (provided the coils' impedances are frequency-independent) — this is the most prized property of the dynamometer wattmeter.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Operating Mode | Coil 1 | Coil 2 | Phase Angle $\phi$ | Deflection $\theta$ Proportional to |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DC Ammeter (series coils)** | $I$ | $I$ | $0$ | $I^{2}$ |
| **DC Voltmeter (series coils + multiplier R)** | $I = V/R$ | $I$ | $0$ | $V^{2}$ |
| **AC Ammeter / Voltmeter** | $I$ | $I$ | $0$ | $I^{2}$ RMS |
| **AC Wattmeter (current & potential)** | $I_L$ | $V / R_p$ | $\phi$ (load pf) | $P = V I_L \cos\phi$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete functional flow** of an electrodynamometer wattmeter — from the two input quantities (current and voltage) to the pointer deflection indicating real power.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC/DC Power Line: V and IL with phase phi] --> B1[Fixed Coil: Current Coil CC]
    A --> B2[Moving Coil: Potential Coil PC with series multiplier Rp]
    B1 --> C1[Magnetic Field B proportional to IL]
    B2 --> C2[Coil Current I2 proportional to V divided by Rp]
    C1 --> D[Force on moving coil conductors: F = B times I2 times L]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Deflecting Torque Td = I1 times I2 times cos phi times dM by d theta]
    E --> F[Moving Spindle rotates against Control Springs]
    F --> G[Controlling Torque Tc = Kc times theta]
    G --> H{Equilibrium Td equals Tc reached}
    H --> I[Pointer Deflection theta proportional to P = V IL cos phi]
    I --> J[Scaled Reading: Real Power in Watts]
```

### 4.2 Construction Cross-Section (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pointer and Scale] --> P2[Mirror for Parallax Correction]
    P2 --> P3[Moving Coil on Pivoted Spindle]
    P3 --> P4[Air Dashpot for Damping]
    P3 --> P5[Two Phosphor Bronze Control Springs]
    P5 --> P6[Terminal Block for Potential Coil]
    P3 --> P7[Two Halves of Fixed Current Coil surrounding Moving Coil]
    P7 --> P8[Terminal Block for Current Coil]
    P7 --> P9[Mumetal Magnetic Shield housing]
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Block Diagram — As a Wattmeter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vin[Voltage V at Load Terminal] --> Rmul[Multiplier Resistance Rp]
    Rmul --> Ipcc[Potential Coil Current I2 = V by Rp]
    ILoad[Load Current IL] --> Iccc[Current Coil produces field B]
    Iccc --> X[Magnetic Field Region]
    Ipcc --> X
    X --> T[Deflecting Torque Td proportional to I2 times B times cos phi]
    T --> Y[Pointer Deflection proportional to V IL cos phi = P]
    Y --> Out[Scale Reading in Watts]
```

### 4.4 Ferrodynamic (Iron-Core) Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Iron Core Dynamometer] --> B[Silicon Steel Lamination Core]
    B --> C[Higher Flux Density for same MMF]
    C --> D[Stronger Deflecting Torque]
    D --> E[Used in Switchboard Panel Instruments]
    B --> F[Drawback: Hysteresis and Eddy Current Errors]
    B --> G[Drawback: Reduced Accuracy versus Air Core]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**List the three main subsystems of an electrodynamometer wattmeter and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Current Coil (Fixed Coil):** Connected in series with the load; carries the load current $I_L$ and produces the working magnetic field.
2. **Potential Coil (Moving Coil):** Connected in parallel with the load through a high non-inductive multiplier resistance $R_p$; carries a current proportional to the supply voltage $V$.
3. **Control & Damping System:** Two phosphor-bronze springs provide the controlling torque $T_c = K_c \theta$, and an air dashpot provides critical damping to prevent pointer oscillation.

*(Marks: Definition 1M + Function 1M + Diagram/keywords 1M)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Why is the scale of an electrodynamometer voltmeter non-uniform and "cramped" at the lower end?**

**Model Answer:**

The deflecting torque is $T_d = K_d \cdot I_1 \cdot I_2 = K_d \cdot I^2$ (when the two coils are connected in series for a voltmeter configuration). Since the deflection $\theta$ is proportional to $I^2$ and the meter scale is graduated in *volts* (which is itself linear in $I$ through Ohm's law), the scale is **square-law in nature**. For small currents, a tiny change in $I$ produces an even smaller change in $I^2$ → pointer moves only slightly → **cramped lower end**. For large currents, a small change in $I$ produces a much larger change in $I^2$ → pointer moves rapidly → **expanded upper end**.

*(Marks: Square-law justification 1M + Lower end cramping 1M + Upper end expansion 1M)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Electrodynamometer**
> 1. Students frequently *omit the negative sign* in the energy co-energy step; the correct formulation is $T_d = +\partial W / \partial \theta$ under constant-current excitation.
> 2. Many students **forget to average** the double-frequency torque term in AC derivation — full marks require explicit step showing $\langle \cos(2\omega t - \phi) \rangle = 0$.
> 3. In wattmeter derivations, students often assume $\cos\phi = 1$ — this is a **gross error**; the power-factor angle must appear explicitly.
> 4. Drawing the **magnetic shield (Mumetal enclosure)** in sketches carries 1 mark — do not skip it.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** *(Module Internal Choice Option 1)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and principle of operation** of an electrodynamometer wattmeter. Clearly label the current coil, potential coil, control springs, and damping system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Starting from the expression for the energy stored in a coupled-coil system, **derive the expression for the instantaneous and average deflecting torque** of an electrodynamometer when energized by alternating currents. State clearly the role of the term $dM/d\theta$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Principle of Operation (7 marks):**

The electrodynamometer wattmeter consists of the following parts (each worth ~0.5–1 mark):

- **Fixed (Current) Coil:** Two sections of thick wire wound on non-magnetic formers, connected in series and placed coaxially with the moving coil between them. They carry the load current $I_L$.
- **Moving (Potential) Coil:** A light, fine-wire coil wound on a non-metallic former, mounted on a pivoted spindle. It is connected via a high multiplier resistance $R_p$ across the supply.
- **Control Springs:** Two helical phosphor-bronze springs, providing $T_c = K_c \theta$. They also serve as current leads to the moving coil.
- **Damping System:** Air dashpot (piston-in-cylinder) — chosen to avoid eddy-current errors that would otherwise contaminate AC accuracy.
- **Pointer & Scale:** Lightweight aluminium pointer with knife-edge for clear reading.
- **Magnetic Shield:** Mumetal enclosure to shield from stray external fields.

**Operating Principle:** When load current $I_L$ flows in the fixed coil, a magnetic field $B$ is set up. The potential coil carries a current $I_2 \approx V/R_p$ in this field. The interaction produces a deflecting torque $T_d \propto I_L \cdot I_2 \cdot \cos\phi = (V I_L \cos\phi)/R_p \propto P$. This torque rotates the moving system until balanced by the spring torque, giving a deflection proportional to **real power**.

---

**(b) Derivation of Instantaneous and Average Deflecting Torque (7 marks):**

The energy stored in the magnetically coupled system is:

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} L_1 I_1^{2} + \frac{1}{2} L_2 I_2^{2} + M I_1 I_2
$$

Since self-inductances are independent of $\theta$:

$$
T_d = \frac{\partial W}{\partial \theta} = I_1 I_2 \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

For AC, with $i_1(t) = I_{m1}\sin(\omega t)$ and $i_2(t) = I_{m2}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$:

$$
T_d(t) = I_{m1} I_{m2}\sin(\omega t)\sin(\omega t - \phi)\frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

$$
T_d(t) = \frac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot [\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)]
$$

The second term is a **double-frequency pulsating torque**, averaged to zero over a cycle and absorbed by the inertia of the moving system. The **mean (average) deflecting torque** is:

$$
T_{d,\text{avg}} = \frac{I_{m1} I_{m2}}{2} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot \cos\phi = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

where $I_1$ and $I_2$ are RMS values.

**Role of $dM/d\theta$:** It is a *geometric constant* of the instrument, determined by the **number of turns, coil dimensions, and relative positioning** of the fixed and moving coils. It governs the *sensitivity* of the instrument — a larger $|dM/d\theta|$ produces a higher deflecting torque for the same input currents.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** *(Module Internal Choice Option 2)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain why the electrodynamometer can be used on **both AC and DC** with equal accuracy, while the **PMMC instrument** is essentially restricted to DC. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a clear derivation, show that the **deflection of an electrodynamometer wattmeter is directly proportional to the real power** consumed in the load. State two important **errors** that can affect a dynamometer wattmeter. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) AC/DC Universality (7 marks):**

- **PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil):** The deflecting torque depends on the *flux density of a permanent magnet*, $B_{PM}$. The torque is $T_d = B_{PM} \cdot I \cdot N \cdot A$. On AC, the current reverses every half-cycle, causing the **average torque to be zero** (positive half cancels negative half). Hence PMMC gives a *zero* (or oscillating) reading on AC.
- **Electrodynamometer:** The magnetic field is produced by the *current itself* — there is no permanent magnet. On AC, both $I_1(t)$ and $B(t)$ reverse **simultaneously** (because $B$ is proportional to $I_1$). Therefore, the torque $T_d(t) = k \cdot I_1(t) \cdot I_2(t)$ is *always unidirectional* (the product of two reversing quantities is positive). The instrument produces a **steady mean torque** that is independent of the direction of current. Hence it works on **both AC and DC with the same calibration** — a property that makes it an excellent *transfer instrument* and *AC-DC comparator*.

Additionally, the air-core electrodynamometer is free of **hysteresis and eddy-current errors** (no iron path), giving it superior accuracy.

---

**(b) Wattmeter Deflection and Errors (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Current coil current: $I_1 = I_L$.
Potential coil current: $I_2 = V / R_p$ (since $R_p$ is highly non-inductive).
Phase angle between them: $\phi$ (load power factor angle).

Mean deflecting torque:

$$
T_{d,\text{avg}} = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} = \frac{V I_L \cos\phi}{R_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta}
$$

Equating with controlling torque $T_c = K_c \theta$:

$$
\theta = \frac{1}{K_c R_p} \cdot \frac{dM}{d\theta} \cdot V I_L \cos\phi = K_w \cdot P
$$

Since $P = V I_L \cos\phi$, the deflection $\theta$ is directly proportional to **real power**.

**Errors (2 marks — any two of the following):**

1. **Error due to Potential-Coil Inductance:** The potential coil has a small inductance $L_p$, making its impedance $Z_p \neq R_p$. At low power factors, this introduces a *phase-angle error* in the wattmeter reading.
2. **Error due to Potential-Coil Capacitance (at high frequencies):** Inter-turn capacitance causes the current in the potential coil to *lead* the voltage, again introducing a small phase error.
3. **Error due to Eddy Currents in Metal Parts:** Any nearby metallic former (if used) can host eddy currents, distorting the field. (Air-core designs eliminate this.)
4. **Error due to Stray Magnetic Fields:** Although Mumetal shielding reduces this, residual external fields can still produce an error torque.
5. **Error due to Temperature Variation:** Spring stiffness and coil resistance change with temperature, affecting calibration.
6. **Error due to Friction in Moving System:** Particularly significant in low-torque instruments.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — Electrodynamometer (Module 4)**

- **Principle:** Force between *two current-carrying coils*; torque ∝ **product of two currents**.
- **Core Equation (DC):** $T_d = I_1 I_2 \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$
- **Core Equation (AC):** $T_d = I_1 I_2 \cos\phi \cdot \dfrac{dM}{d\theta}$ *(RMS values, $\phi$ = phase between $I_1$ and $I_2$)*
- **Derivation origin:** $T_d = \partial W / \partial \theta$ at constant current, where $W = \tfrac{1}{2}L_1 I_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}L_2 I_2^2 + M I_1 I_2$.
- **Equilibrium:** $T_d = T_c = K_c \theta \;\Rightarrow\; \theta \propto I_1 I_2 \cos\phi$.
- **Wattmeter use:** Fixed coil in series with load (carries $I_L$); moving coil + multiplier $R_p$ in parallel (carries $V/R_p$). Deflection $\theta \propto P = V I_L \cos\phi$.
- **Air-core vs. Iron-core:**
  - *Air-core:* High accuracy, no eddy/hysteresis errors, weak torque, expensive, used in **laboratory standard instruments**.
  - *Iron-core:* Strong torque, robust, used in **switchboard panels**, but has iron losses.
- **Advantages:** True RMS response, AC-DC transfer capability, high accuracy, used as a **wattmeter**.
- **Disadvantages:** Low torque/weight ratio, non-uniform scale (square law), sensitive to overloads, expensive, frequency-limited.
- **Damping:** Air dashpot (NOT eddy-current — would introduce errors).
- **Control:** Two spiral phosphor-bronze springs.
- **Shielding:** Mumetal enclosure essential.
- **Double-frequency term** in AC torque is absorbed by **mechanical inertia** — never appears in the *mean* torque.
- **Common KTU mistakes to avoid:**
  1. Forgetting $\cos\phi$ in the AC equation.
  2. Mixing up peak and RMS values without the factor-of-2 conversion.
  3. Saying "PMMC works on AC" — it does *not*.
  4. Omitting the Mumetal shield in construction diagrams.
  5. Not showing that $L_1, L_2$ are independent of $\theta$ (justifies the simplified equation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
