The noise power captured within the IF filter of bandwidth RBW is:

$$P_{noise} = kT \cdot RBW$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dBm.**

$$P_{noise,\,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(kT \cdot RBW \times 10^{3}) = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW)$$

(Here $10^{3}$ converts watts to milliwatts.)

**Step 3 — Add the analyzer's internal noise figure.**

The displayed noise floor is then:

$$\boxed{\;N_{floor,\,dBm} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF\;}$$

**Step 4 — Example evaluation.**

For $RBW = 10\;\text{kHz}$ and $NF = 20\;\text{dB}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{floor,\,dBm} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(10^{4}) + 20 \\
&= -174 + 40 + 20 \\
&= -114\;\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each tenfold reduction in RBW lowers the noise floor by 10 dB (i.e., by 3.01 dB for a 2× reduction, by 6.02 dB for a 5× reduction, and by 10 dB for a 10× reduction). This is why **narrowing the RBW is the single most effective way to improve sensitivity** in a super-heterodyne spectrum analyzer.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: KTU 2024 Expected Problem Patterns

| # | Problem Pattern | Key Formulae to Apply | Mark Weight |
|---|----------------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Calculate LO frequency and image frequency for given input signal and IF | $f_{LO} = f_{signal} + f_{IF}$, $f_{image} = f_{signal} + 2 f_{IF}$ | 3–7 marks |
| 2 | Determine displayed frequency range from given LO sweep range | $f_{signal} = f_{LO} - f_{IF}$ | 7 marks |
| 3 | Compute noise floor for given RBW and noise figure | $N_{floor} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF$ | 3–7 marks |
| 4 | Explain block diagram and the role of each functional block | Super-heterodyne architecture | 7–14 marks |
| 5 | Compare spectrum analyzer with oscilloscope | Time vs. frequency domain | 3 marks |
| 6 | Effect of RBW and VBW on sweep time and noise floor | $T_{sw} \propto \dfrac{Span}{RBW \cdot VBW}$ | 7 marks |
| 7 | Real-time FFT analyzer vs. swept-tuned analyzer | FFT vs. heterodyne | 3–7 marks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Strategy:** KTU ESE questions in this module almost always combine a block diagram (for 7 marks) with a numerical sub-part (for 7 marks). Practice the LO/image calculation at least five times — it is the single most common numerical sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Super-Heterodyne (Swept-Tuned) Spectrum Analyzer

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **complete signal path** from the RF input to the display, including the sweep-ramp synchronization that drives both the local oscillator and the X-axis of the screen.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[RF Input Connector] --> ATT[Input Attenuator 0 to 70 dB]
    ATT --> PRE[Preselector Low Pass or YIG Bandpass Filter]
    PRE --> MIX[Non-Linear Mixer]
    LO[Swept Local Oscillator VCO] --> MIX
    MIX --> IFA[IF Amplifier]
    IFA --> IFF[IF Bandpass Filter sets Resolution Bandwidth RBW]
    IFF --> LOG[Logarithmic Amplifier Compressor]
    LOG --> DET[Envelope Detector]
    DET --> VBF[Video Bandwidth Low Pass Filter sets VBW]
    VBF --> YAMP[Vertical Y Axis Amplifier]
    YAMP --> CRT[Display Y axis Amplitude]
    RAMP[Sawtooth Ramp Generator] --> LO
    RAMP --> XAMP[Horizontal X Axis Amplifier]
    XAMP --> CRT
    CRT --> OUT[Display Screen Amplitude vs Frequency]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **upper signal path** carries the signal from the input port through to the vertical (amplitude) axis of the display.
- The **lower control path** originates from a single sawtooth ramp generator. This ramp performs two functions simultaneously:
  1. It tunes the local oscillator across the desired span.
  2. It deflects the CRT/LCD beam horizontally, so that the X-axis is perfectly synchronized with the LO's instantaneous frequency.

This single shared ramp generator is the engineering heart of the analyzer: it guarantees that the X-coordinate of every point on the screen corresponds exactly to the frequency currently being examined by the mixer-IF chain.

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Image Frequency Rejection

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the preselector filter prevents the image frequency from contaminating the display. This is a high-frequency KTU question theme.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SIG[Desired Signal f signal] -->|Passes through| PRESEL[Preselector Bandpass Filter]
    IMG[Image Frequency f signal plus 2 f IF] -->|Blocked by| PRESEL
    PRESEL --> MIX[Mixer]
    LO[Local Oscillator f LO] --> MIX
    MIX --> IF[IF Output at fixed f IF]
    IF --> DSP[Display Shows only Desired Signal]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The desired signal and the image frequency enter the preselector simultaneously. The preselector's passband admits the desired signal and rejects the image. Downstream of the mixer, only the desired signal reaches the display — a clean result.

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Swept-Tuned vs. Real-Time FFT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SWSYS[Swept Tuned Super Heterodyne Analyzer]
        direction TB
        S1[RF Input] --> S2[Attenuator and Preselector] --> S3[Mixer with Swept LO] --> S4[IF Filter and Log Amp] --> S5[Detector and Video Filter] --> S6[Display Swept Plot]
    end
    subgraph RTSYS[Real Time FFT Analyzer]
        direction TB
        R1[RF Input] --> R2[Wideband Anti Alias Filter] --> R3[High Speed ADC] --> R4[FFT Processor] --> R5[Display Spectrum of Entire Block]
    end
```

**Engineering takeaway:** The swept-tuned analyzer builds the spectrum **one frequency at a time** by scanning the LO; it cannot capture transient events shorter than its sweep time. The real-time FFT analyzer captures a **wide block of frequencies simultaneously** using fast analog-to-digital conversion and digital FFT, making it ideal for transient, burst, or modulated signals. For routine RF, microwave, and EMI work, the swept-tuned super-heterodyne analyzer remains the standard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Followed:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (INP, ATT, PRE, MIX, LO, IFA, IFF, LOG, DET, VBF, YAMP, CRT, RAMP, XAMP, OUT, S1–S6, R1–R5, etc.).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
> - All node labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text without bold/italics/HTML formatting.
> - Subgraphs are clearly named and use `direction TB` to avoid layout ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Spectrum Analyzer and State Two Advantages Over an Oscilloscope

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

A **spectrum analyzer** is an electronic test instrument that displays the **amplitude of an input signal as a function of frequency**, i.e., it operates in the **frequency domain**.

**Two advantages over an oscilloscope:**

1. **Frequency-domain view:** A spectrum analyzer directly reveals the **spectral content** of a complex signal — its harmonics, sidebands, intermodulation products, and noise — which is not visible on a time-domain oscilloscope. This is essential for RF, microwave, and EMI measurements.
2. **Superior measurement of small signals in the presence of large ones:** With its narrow resolution bandwidth and logarithmic amplification, a spectrum analyzer can resolve **small spectral components** sitting close to large ones (e.g., a −90 dBm harmonic next to a 0 dBm fundamental) — a task at which an oscilloscope fails.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only that "the spectrum analyzer shows frequency," without specifying that it shows **amplitude versus frequency**. Marks are specifically awarded for the "amplitude-vs-frequency" wording. Do not confuse with a wave analyzer (which only measures a single frequency at a time).

---

### Question 2: What is the Function of the Resolution Bandwidth (RBW) Filter in a Spectrum Analyzer?

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW) filter** is the **narrow-band IF bandpass filter** placed after the mixer in a super-heterodyne spectrum analyzer. Its functions are:

1. **Frequency selectivity:** It sets the smallest frequency difference between two signals that the analyzer can resolve as **distinct spectral lines**. A narrower RBW provides better frequency resolution.
2. **Noise bandwidth control:** The RBW determines the **noise bandwidth** of the analyzer. The displayed noise floor is proportional to $10 \log_{10}(RBW)$. Reducing RBW lowers the noise floor and thereby improves sensitivity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to state that RBW improves **sensitivity** without linking it to the noise bandwidth or the $10 \log_{10}$ relationship. For full marks, mention the dual role: frequency resolution **and** noise bandwidth.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A: Detailed Question on Block Diagram and Image Frequency

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Question A(a) — 7 Marks:**
With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the operation of a **super-heterodyne spectrum analyzer**. Explain the functions of the **local oscillator, mixer, IF filter, and log amplifier**.

**Model Answer:**

A super-heterodyne spectrum analyzer converts every input frequency to a **fixed intermediate frequency (IF)** using a swept local oscillator and a non-linear mixer, then measures the IF amplitude to plot amplitude-versus-frequency. The block diagram consists of the following functional blocks (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

**1. Input Attenuator (0–70 dB):**
A calibrated, switched RF attenuator at the input port protects the mixer from overload and establishes a known reference level for accurate amplitude measurements.

**2. Preselector Filter (Low-pass or YIG):**
Located after the attenuator, it rejects out-of-band signals and, critically, the **image frequency** (which is $f_{signal} + 2 f_{IF}$). It prevents the mixer from down-converting unwanted image signals to the IF and producing false displays.

**3. Mixer and Swept Local Oscillator:**
The mixer is a non-linear device that multiplies the input signal with the LO output, producing sum and difference frequencies. The **difference** is the desired IF:

$$f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{signal}$$

A **sawtooth ramp** drives the voltage-controlled local oscillator, sweeping it across the span required to convert the entire user-selected frequency range down to the fixed IF.

**4. IF Amplifier and IF Bandpass Filter (RBW filter):**
A fixed, narrow-band bandpass filter at the IF. Its bandwidth defines the **Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)**. A narrower RBW provides finer frequency resolution and a lower noise floor (sensitivity).

**5. Logarithmic Amplifier (Compressor):**
Because signals at the input can span more than 70 dB in power, the IF amplifier applies a **logarithmic compression** so that the entire dynamic range fits on the display without saturation. The display thus reads in **dBm**, not linear volts.

**6. Envelope Detector and Video Filter (VBW filter):**
The envelope detector rectifies the IF to recover its amplitude. The video filter (low-pass, bandwidth = VBW) smooths the trace, suppressing random noise variance so that weak signals are visible.

**7. Display and Ramp Synchronization:**
The same sawtooth ramp that sweeps the LO also drives the horizontal deflection of the display, ensuring a **one-to-one correspondence** between the LO's instantaneous frequency and the X-coordinate of each pixel. The vertical deflection is driven by the video-filtered envelope amplitude, producing a calibrated plot of **amplitude vs. frequency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution Tip:** A 7-mark answer should include: (i) the block diagram (3 marks), (ii) functions of at least three key blocks (3 marks), and (iii) the role of the sawtooth ramp for synchronization (1 mark). Do not exceed the time budget for diagrams — a clean text-based block diagram is acceptable.

---

**Question A(b) — 7 Marks:**
A super-heterodyne spectrum analyzer uses an **IF of 100 MHz**. If a signal of **800 MHz** is to be displayed, determine:
(i) the required local-oscillator frequency (assuming high-side injection),
(ii) the image frequency, and
(iii) the minimum LO frequency that would be needed if low-side injection were used instead.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Intermediate frequency: $f_{IF} = 100\;\text{MHz}$
- Input signal frequency: $f_{signal} = 800\;\text{MHz}$
- Mode: **High-side injection** (LO above signal) for part (i) and (ii).

**Step 1 — Compute the LO frequency for high-side injection.**

The general super-heterodyne equation is:

$$f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{signal}$$

Solving for $f_{LO}$:

$$f_{LO} = f_{signal} + f_{IF}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$f_{LO} = 800\;\text{MHz} + 100\;\text{MHz} = 900\;\text{MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the image frequency.**

The image is the mirror of the desired signal about the LO. By the formula:

$$f_{image} = f_{signal} + 2 f_{IF}$$

Substituting:

$$f_{image} = 800\;\text{MHz} + 2(100\;\text{MHz}) = 800\;\text{MHz} + 200\;\text{MHz} = 1000\;\text{MHz}$$

**Verification (mixer-output check):**

- For desired signal: $f_{LO} - f_{signal} = 900 - 800 = 100\;\text{MHz} = f_{IF}$ ✓
- For image: $f_{image} - f_{LO} = 1000 - 900 = 100\;\text{MHz} = f_{IF}$ ✓ (The image also produces the IF — this is precisely why it must be rejected by the preselector.)

**Step 3 — Compute LO frequency for low-side injection.**

For low-side injection, the LO is **below** the signal:

$$f_{IF} = f_{signal} - f_{LO}$$

Solving:

$$f_{LO} = f_{signal} - f_{IF} = 800\;\text{MHz} - 100\;\text{MHz} = 700\;\text{MHz}$$

**Summary of results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| LO frequency (high-side) | **900 MHz** |
| Image frequency | **1000 MHz** |
| LO frequency (low-side) | **700 MHz** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common mistake is to write the image formula as $f_{image} = f_{LO} + f_{IF}$ instead of $f_{image} = f_{signal} + 2 f_{IF}$. While these two expressions give the same numerical answer *if* the LO is computed correctly, only the second form is considered the canonical answer. Also, do not forget to **state the injection mode** (high-side or low-side) before computing; marks are deducted if the assumption is unstated.

---

### Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question — Noise Floor, RBW, and Real-Time FFT

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Question B(a) — 7 Marks:**
A super-heterodyne spectrum analyzer has a **noise figure of 25 dB**. Calculate its **displayed noise floor** when the resolution bandwidth is set to (i) **1 MHz**, (ii) **10 kHz**, and (iii) **100 Hz**. Comment on the engineering trade-off involved.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Noise figure: $NF = 25\;\text{dB}$
- Thermal noise floor: $kT = -174\;\text{dBm/Hz}$ (at 290 K)

**General formula:**

$$N_{floor,\,dBm} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF$$

**Part (i): RBW = 1 MHz = $10^6$ Hz**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{floor} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(10^{6}) + 25 \\
&= -174 + 60 + 25 \\
&= -89\;\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii): RBW = 10 kHz = $10^4$ Hz**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{floor} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(10^{4}) + 25 \\
&= -174 + 40 + 25 \\
&= -109\;\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (iii): RBW = 100 Hz = $10^2$ Hz**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{floor} &= -174 + 10 \log_{10}(10^{2}) + 25 \\
&= -174 + 20 + 25 \\
&= -129\;\text{dBm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary of results:**

| RBW | Computed Noise Floor |
|-----|---------------------|
| 1 MHz | **−89 dBm** |
| 10 kHz | **−109 dBm** |
| 100 Hz | **−129 dBm** |

**Engineering Trade-off Comment:**

Each **100-fold (40 dB) reduction in RBW lowers the noise floor by 20 dB**, because $10 \log_{10}(100) = 20$ dB. This demonstrates that **narrower RBW dramatically improves sensitivity**. However, the trade-off is that sweep time grows as approximately $Span / (RBW \cdot VBW)$; reducing RBW by a factor of 100 makes the analyzer about 100× slower. Also, very narrow RBW (e.g., 1 Hz) requires the analyzer's LO to have extremely stable phase noise, otherwise the displayed trace itself becomes noisy. The correct choice of RBW in practice is the **widest value that still places the noise floor below the smallest signal of interest**.

---

**Question B(b) — 7 Marks:**
Compare **real-time FFT spectrum analyzers** with **swept-tuned super-heterodyne spectrum analyzers**. State three advantages and two limitations of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Swept-Tuned (Super-Heterodyne) | Real-Time FFT |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| **Operating Principle** | Sequential — one frequency at a time via a swept LO and a fixed IF filter | Parallel — captures a wide band of frequencies simultaneously using a high-speed ADC and the Fast Fourier Transform |
| **Frequency Range** | Very wide, up to tens of GHz (with external mixers to 100s of GHz) | Limited by ADC sampling rate — typically up to a few hundred MHz |
| **Transient Capture** | Cannot capture signals shorter than sweep time | Excellent — captures single-shot transients |
| **Sweep Time** | Slow at narrow RBW over wide spans | Fast for the captured block (FFT is $O(N \log N)$) |
| **Phase Noise Sensitivity** | High (LO phase noise appears directly) | Lower — phase noise is averaged over the FFT integration |
| **Cost & Complexity** | High at microwave frequencies | Lower at audio/low-RF; very high at broadband RF |
| **Dynamic Range** | Very high (often >100 dB) | Limited by ADC resolution and spurious-free dynamic range |

**Three advantages of Swept-Tuned Analyzer:**

1. Wide frequency range — extends naturally to millimeter waves.
2. Very high dynamic range.
3. Excellent frequency resolution at narrow RBW.

**Two limitations of Swept-Tuned Analyzer:**

1. Cannot capture transient or single-shot events.
2. Sweep time becomes impractically long for narrow RBW over wide spans.

**Three advantages of Real-Time FFT Analyzer:**

1. Captures transient, pulsed, and burst signals in a single acquisition.
2. Faster display update for the same displayed span (within ADC bandwidth).
3. Phase-noise averaging through FFT integration improves trace clarity.

**Two limitations of Real-Time FFT Analyzer:**

1. Frequency range is limited by the ADC sampling rate (Nyquist).
2. Dynamic range is limited by ADC bit resolution and SFDR.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly assert that the FFT analyzer "shows the signal in time domain." The opposite is true — the FFT analyzer also operates in the **frequency domain**, but it does so by **digital computation of the Fourier transform of a captured time-domain block**, instead of the analog swept-conversion technique used by the super-heterodyne analyzer. This distinction is the crux of the comparison question and must be stated explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Spectrum Analyzer (KTU 2024)**

- A **spectrum analyzer** displays **amplitude (Y) versus frequency (X)** of an input signal — i.e., it is a **frequency-domain** instrument.
- The **super-heterodyne** architecture is the dominant design. The mixer converts every input frequency to a fixed IF using a swept local oscillator.
- **Key IF equation:** $f_{IF} = f_{LO} - f_{signal}$ (high-side injection).
- **Image frequency formula:** $f_{image} = f_{signal} + 2 f_{IF}$. The preselector filter must reject this.
- The **RBW filter** is the IF bandpass filter; it sets frequency resolution and noise bandwidth.
- The **VBW filter** is the post-detection low-pass filter; it smooths the displayed trace.
- **Sweep time relationship:** $T_{sw} \propto \dfrac{Span}{RBW \cdot VBW}$. Narrower RBW ⇒ slower sweep but lower noise floor.
- **Noise floor formula:** $N_{floor,\,dBm} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(RBW) + NF$. A 10× reduction in RBW lowers the floor by 10 dB.
- The **logarithmic amplifier** compresses the wide dynamic range (often >70 dB) onto the display.
- The **sawtooth ramp generator** simultaneously tunes the LO and drives the X-axis of the display, ensuring synchronization.
- The **real-time FFT analyzer** captures a wide band of frequencies at once using a high-speed ADC and is suited for transient signals; it is limited by ADC sampling rate (Nyquist) and bit resolution.
- **Swept vs. real-time:** Swept is wideband and high dynamic range but slow for narrow RBW; real-time is fast and captures transients but is bandwidth-limited.
- **Frequency span:** $Span = f_{stop} - f_{start} = 2 (f_{center} - f_{start})$.
- **Common KTU 2024 trap questions:**
  - Confusing **spectrum analyzer** (shows amplitude vs. frequency) with **wave analyzer** (measures amplitude at a single tunable frequency).
  - Confusing the **time domain** (oscilloscope) with the **frequency domain** (spectrum analyzer).
  - Forgetting to state the **injection mode** (high-side or low-side) in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Peak Response Voltmeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Peak Response Voltmeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Peak Response Voltmeter** (also called a **Peak-Reading Voltmeter** or **Crest Voltmeter**) is a type of electronic voltmeter that measures the **peak (crest) value** of an alternating voltage waveform, irrespective of its waveshape. It functions by using a **unilateral switching element (diode)** followed by a **holding capacitor** to capture and retain the maximum instantaneous voltage of the input signal, which is then read by a sensitive DC indicating instrument (D'Arsonval movement).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Electronic Voltmeters):**
> Peak Response Voltmeters belong to the family of **AC voltmeters with rectifiers/amplifiers**. They are distinct from *true RMS* and *average-responding* voltmeters because their response is governed by the **maximum instantaneous excursion** of the input signal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Water-Pool Peak Gauge":**
> Imagine a swimming pool where waves of water keep coming in. You want to know the **highest** water level that ever occurred. You place a **floating ball trapped inside a transparent tube** at the highest point the water ever reached. Even after the wave recedes, the ball stays at that high mark because a one-way flap only lets the ball rise, not fall. The water level marked by the ball = the **peak of the waveform**, and the tube = our **holding capacitor**; the one-way flap = our **diode**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Form Factor** $K_f$ for sinusoidal AC: $K_f = \mathbf{1.11}$ (= $\pi/2\sqrt{2}$)
- **Amplitude/Crest Factor** $K_a$ for sinusoidal AC: $K_a = \mathbf{1.414}$ (= $\sqrt{2}$)
- **Diode forward voltage drop** (Silicon): $V_D \approx \mathbf{0.6\text{–}0.7 V}$
- **Fundamental relation**: $V_{rms} = V_p / K_a$ and $V_{avg} = 2V_p / \pi$

### 1.4 Classification of Peak Voltmeters

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Peak-Responding with Capacitor (Diode-Capacitor type)** | Diode charges a capacitor to peak; meter reads DC | General lab use, audio & RF |
| **Slide-Back Peak Voltmeter** | Compares input against a DC bucking voltage | Precision measurement of high voltages |
| **Peak-to-Peak Voltmeter (Voltage Doubler)** | Two capacitors & two diodes | TV service, pulse measurements |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU Module-4 syllabus primarily covers the **diode-capacitor (peak-detector) type** and the **half-wave / full-wave (voltage-doubler)** versions. Ensure you can draw both circuits and derive the output voltage for each.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Input signal** $v_i(t)$ (sinusoidal or arbitrary) is applied to a **series diode** $D$ and **shunt capacitor** $C$.
2. On the **positive half-cycle**, diode $D$ becomes **forward-biased** and conducts. The capacitor $C$ charges rapidly (through the low forward resistance $R_f$ of the diode) toward the **positive peak** $V_p$ of the input.
3. As $v_i(t)$ begins to fall after reaching $V_p$, the diode becomes **reverse-biased** (because $v_C \approx V_p > v_i$). The diode disconnects the source from the capacitor.
4. The capacitor is left to **discharge slowly** through the **meter resistance** $R_m$ (and any leakage $R_L$) until the next positive half-cycle replenishes it.
5. The DC voltmeter (D'Arsonval) connected across $C$ therefore indicates a value **very close to $V_p$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind the design:** A moving-coil meter cannot follow AC at power-line frequencies (it would just vibrate around zero). By holding the peak on a capacitor and reading the *DC* value, we get a **steady, readable deflection proportional to the peak voltage**.

### 2.2 Half-Wave Peak Voltmeter — Circuit Description

- One diode $D_1$ in series, one capacitor $C$ in shunt.
- Diode conducts only on **positive half** → capacitor charges to $+V_p$.
- DC voltmeter reads $\approx V_p$ (minus diode drop $V_D$).

### 2.3 Full-Wave (Peak-to-Peak / Voltage-Doubler) Peak Voltmeter

- Two diodes $D_1, D_2$ and two capacitors $C_1, C_2$.
- On the **negative half-cycle**, $C_1$ charges to $-V_p$.
- On the **positive half-cycle**, $C_1$ acts as a battery in series with the source, charging $C_2$ to **$+2V_p$**.
- The DC meter reads $V_{C2} = 2V_p$.
- Hence it is called a **voltage doubler** — output = **peak-to-peak** voltage of the input.

### 2.4 Calibration of the Peak Voltmeter

The DC indicating meter has a scale calibrated directly in **RMS volts assuming a sinusoidal input**, using:

$$V_{rms,\text{indicated}} = \frac{V_p}{K_a} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}}$$

If the input is **non-sinusoidal**, the *indicated* reading is **incorrect for RMS** but correctly represents the *peak*.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations — memorize these for ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Peak voltage indicated (half-wave) | $V_m = V_p - V_D$ | Volts; subtract Si diode drop |
| 2 | Peak-to-peak (full-wave doubler) | $V_m = 2V_p - 2V_D$ | Volts |
| 3 | Discharge of capacitor | $v_C(t) = V_p e^{-t/(R_m \parallel R_L) C}$ | Exponential decay |
| 4 | Time between replenishment | $T \approx 1/f$ (for half-wave), $T/2$ (for full-wave) | Seconds |
| 5 | Required time-constant | $(R_m C) \gg T$ | To keep ripple $< 1\%$ |
| 6 | Ripple voltage (approx) | $\Delta V \approx V_p \dfrac{T}{R_m C} = \dfrac{V_p}{f R_m C}$ | Volts peak-to-peak |
| 7 | RMS of sinusoid from peak | $V_{rms} = V_p / \sqrt{2}$ | $K_a = 1.414$ |
| 8 | Average of full-wave rectified sinusoid | $V_{avg} = 2V_p / \pi$ | $V_{avg} = 0.637\,V_p$ |
| 9 | Form factor (sinusoid) | $K_f = V_{rms}/V_{avg} = 1.11$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Input impedance of voltmeter | $R_{in} \approx R_m / 2$ | Effective loading |
| 11 | Loading error | $\varepsilon\% = \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_s + R_{in}} \times 100$ | $R_s$ = source resistance |
| 12 | Slide-back equation | $V_p = V_{ref} + V_D$ | Precision peak reading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Note the use of `\parallel` (or `\Vert`) for parallel combinations inside the table above to **avoid breaking the Markdown table syntax** with vertical pipes.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Peak voltmeters are essential in:
- **Audio engineering** — measuring loudness peaks to prevent speaker damage.
- **RF and communications** — measuring modulation envelope peaks.
- **Power electronics** — measuring surge/transient peaks on DC bus.
- **High-voltage engineering** — slide-back type measures very high AC voltages safely.
- **Calibration laboratories** — transfer standard for AC voltage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacitor Charging and Discharging in a Peak Voltmeter

Consider the half-wave peak voltmeter with diode forward resistance $R_f$, meter resistance $R_m$, and capacitor $C$. During the conduction interval (very short, near the peak), the capacitor charges to $V_p$ through $R_f$. After the peak, the diode opens and the capacitor discharges through $R_m$ alone.

**Step 1 — Charging phase:**

During conduction, the time-constant is $\tau_c = R_f C \ll T$, so the capacitor charges almost instantaneously to:

$$v_C(t) \approx V_p \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_c}\right)$$

For $t = T/4$ (one quarter period of the sinusoid), since $\tau_c$ is very small, $v_C \approx V_p$.

**Step 2 — Discharging phase:**

After the diode stops conducting, the capacitor discharges through $R_m$ for almost one full period $T = 1/f$ (half-wave) before being recharged:

$$v_C(t) = V_p \cdot e^{-t/(R_m C)}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage droop (ripple) in one cycle:**

Substituting $t = T = 1/f$:

$$\Delta V = V_p - v_C(T) = V_p \left(1 - e^{-1/(f R_m C)}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Linear approximation** (valid when $f R_m C \gg 1$):

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$:

$$\Delta V \approx V_p \cdot \frac{1}{f R_m C}$$

**Step 5 — Design criterion:**

To keep droop $< 1\%$ of peak:

$$f R_m C \ge 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_m C \ge \frac{100}{f}$$

For a $50\text{ Hz}$ mains frequency: $R_m C \ge 2\text{ s}$. With $R_m = 10\text{ M}\Omega$, we need $C \ge 0.2\,\mu F$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Doubler (Peak-to-Peak) Output

Consider the two-capacitor voltage-doubler peak voltmeter.

**Step 1 — Negative half-cycle:** Diode $D_2$ conducts; $C_1$ charges to $-V_p$ (clamped to negative peak minus $V_D$).

$$V_{C1} = -(V_p - V_D)$$

**Step 2 — Positive half-cycle:** Now $D_1$ conducts. The source $v_i(t)$ is in **series** with $C_1$ (acting like a charged battery). The two voltages add and charge $C_2$ through $D_1$ to:

$$V_{C2} = v_i(t) + V_{C1} = V_p - V_D - (V_p - V_D) = 2(V_p - V_D)$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state DC reading:**

$$V_m = 2V_p - 2V_D \approx 2V_p \quad \text{(for } V_D \ll V_p\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The meter reads the **peak-to-peak** value of the input sinusoid. If the meter's scale is calibrated for RMS assuming sinusoid:

$$V_{rms,\text{indicated}} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2V_p}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Slide-Back Peak Voltmeter

A slide-back voltmeter works on the **null-deflection principle** and is much more accurate for high voltages.

**Step 1:** With input $v_i(t)$ applied, the meter (DC) reads the peak $V_p$.

**Step 2:** A known DC reference $V_{ref}$ is added in **opposition** to the input. The meter now reads the difference $|V_p - V_{ref}|$.

**Step 3:** $V_{ref}$ is slowly increased (slid back) until the meter reads **zero** (null deflection).

**Step 4:** At null:

$$V_p = V_{ref} + V_D$$

Because the meter reads zero, no current flows, and the diode drop $V_D$ no longer loads the circuit — this **eliminates the diode-drop error** that plagues ordinary peak voltmeters.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Error Sources

| Error Source | Cause | Magnitude | Mitigation |
|--------------|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **Diode forward drop** | $V_D \neq 0$ | $\approx 0.6\text{ V}$ | Slide-back or use Schottky |
| **Capacitor leakage** | $R_L$ finite | Droop $\propto 1/(R_L C)$ | Use Teflon/film caps, $R_L \to \infty$ |
| **Discharge droop** | $R_m C$ not large | $\Delta V$ per cycle | Increase $C$ or $R_m$ |
| **Input loading** | $R_{in}$ finite | $\varepsilon = R_{in}/(R_s+R_{in})$ | Buffer with op-amp follower |
| **Frequency error** | Diode junction capacitance | Effective $R_{in} \downarrow$ at high $f$ | Use video-amp detector |
| **Stray capacitance** | Wiring | Resonances | Shielded probes |

### 3.5 Derivation 4: Loading Error in Practice

If the source has internal resistance $R_s$ and the voltmeter presents $R_{in} = R_m / 2$ (effective at peak), the voltage actually reaching the meter is:

$$V_m = V_p \cdot \frac{R_{in}}{R_s + R_{in}}$$

**Percentage error:**

$$\varepsilon\% = \frac{V_p - V_m}{V_p} \times 100 = \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_{in}} \times 100$$

**Numerical example:** $R_s = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_m = 10\text{ M}\Omega$ ⇒ $R_{in} = 5\text{ M}\Omega$ ⇒

$$\varepsilon\% = \frac{10\,\text{k}}{5\,\text{M} + 10\,\text{k}} \times 100 \approx 0.2\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> For high-impedance transducers (e.g., piezoelectric sensors, $R_s$ in M$\Omega$ range), this error becomes **catastrophic**. An op-amp input buffer is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Half-Wave Peak Voltmeter — Circuit Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AC INPUT SOURCE vi(t)"] --> B["SERIES DIODE D"]
    B --> C["HOLDING CAPACITOR C"]
    C --> D["DC METER DArsonval"]
    C --> E["PARASITIC LEAKAGE RL"]
    D --> F["CALIBRATED SCALE Reads Vp in RMS"]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style F fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242
```

**Working:** Diode $D$ conducts only on positive half-cycles, charging $C$ to peak $V_p$. The DC meter holds the reading between peaks.

### 4.2 Voltage-Doubler (Peak-to-Peak) Peak Voltmeter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AC INPUT vi(t)"] --> B["DIODE D1 Positive half"]
    A --> C["DIODE D2 Negative half"]
    B --> D["CAPACITOR C2 Output node"]
    C --> E["CAPACITOR C1 Stores negative peak"]
    E --> D
    D --> F["DC METER Reads 2Vp approx Vpp"]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
```

**Working:** $C_1$ captures $-V_p$ on the negative half; on the next positive half, source + $C_1$ add in series to charge $C_2$ to $2V_p$.

### 4.3 Slide-Back Peak Voltmeter — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["AC INPUT vi(t)"] --> B["PEAK DETECTOR Capacitor C"]
    B --> C["DC METER Reads Vp minus Vref"]
    C --> D{"NULL DEFLECTION?"}
    D -- NO --> E["ADJUST DC REFERENCE Vref Increase"]
    E --> B
    D -- YES --> F["INDICATE Vp EQUALS Vref PLUS Vd"]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style F fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

**Working:** Increase $V_{ref}$ until the meter nulls. At that point, $V_p = V_{ref} + V_D$, eliminating diode-loading error.

### 4.4 Signal Waveform at the Holding Capacitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P["Input Sine Wave"]
        P1["vi t = Vp sin wt"]
    end
    subgraph Q["Capacitor Voltage vC t"]
        Q1["Charges to Vp on peak"]
        Q2["Holds near Vp between peaks"]
        Q3["Replenishes each cycle"]
    end
    P --> Q
    style P fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b
    style Q fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

> [!NOTE]
> The DC meter sees a nearly-constant $V_p$ (with small sawtooth ripple) — hence its pointer deflects to a fixed position corresponding to the **peak** of the input AC.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Define a Peak Response Voltmeter. Why is a diode-capacitor combination used in its input stage?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Peak Response Voltmeter is an electronic voltmeter that indicates the **peak (crest) value** of an AC waveform by rectifying the input and holding the maximum instantaneous voltage on a capacitor. **(1 mark)**
A **diode** acts as a unilateral switch — it allows the capacitor to charge quickly to the input peak but prevents discharge back into the source, holding the peak between cycles. **(1 mark)**
The **capacitor** stores and holds the peak voltage long enough for the DC indicating meter (D'Arsonval) to give a steady, readable deflection proportional to $V_p$. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What is a voltage-doubler (peak-to-peak) peak voltmeter? Give its main advantage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A voltage-doubler peak voltmeter uses **two diodes and two capacitors** so that the output across the meter equals $2V_p$ (the peak-to-peak value of the input). **(1.5 marks)**
**Main advantage:** It directly indicates the **peak-to-peak** value of the input — useful for measuring pulse and audio signals where both positive and negative excursions matter — and the output is referenced to ground (unlike the half-wave circuit where the meter floats). **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **half-wave peak response voltmeter**. Derive the expression for the capacitor droop voltage and state the design criterion for choosing $C$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of a **voltage-doubler peak voltmeter**. Show that its output is $2V_p$. Compare its performance with the half-wave type. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Half-Wave Peak Voltmeter (7 marks)**

**Circuit:** Input $v_i(t)$ → Diode $D$ in series → Capacitor $C$ in shunt → DC voltmeter $M$ across $C$ (with internal resistance $R_m$).

**Operation (2 marks):**
- On the **positive half-cycle** of $v_i(t)$, diode $D$ is forward-biased and conducts. Capacitor $C$ charges rapidly (through the small forward resistance $R_f$ of the diode) to the **positive peak** $V_p$.
- When $v_i(t)$ starts falling after the peak, $v_C > v_i$, so the diode becomes reverse-biased and disconnects the source.
- Capacitor $C$ then discharges slowly through $R_m$ until the next positive half-cycle recharges it.
- The DC meter therefore sees a voltage close to $V_p$.

**Derivation of droop (3 marks):**

Capacitor discharge equation:

$$v_C(t) = V_p \cdot e^{-t/(R_m C)}$$

At $t = T = 1/f$ (just before recharging):

$$v_C(T) = V_p \cdot e^{-1/(f R_m C)}$$

Droop voltage:

$$\Delta V = V_p - v_C(T) = V_p \left[1 - e^{-1/(f R_m C)}\right]$$

For $f R_m C \gg 1$ (linear approximation):

$$\Delta V \approx \frac{V_p}{f R_m C}$$

**Design criterion (1 mark):** To keep droop $\le 1\%$ of $V_p$:

$$f R_m C \ge 100 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_m C \ge \frac{100}{f}$$

**Effect of diode drop (1 mark):** Actual indicated peak is $V_m = V_p - V_D \approx V_p - 0.6\text{ V}$. For low-voltage measurements this is significant; for high-voltage signals it is negligible.

**(b) Voltage-Doubler Peak Voltmeter (7 marks)**

**Circuit:** Two diodes $D_1, D_2$ and two capacitors $C_1, C_2$. Input applied across the $D_1$–$C_1$ series leg and the $D_2$–$C_2$ leg.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- **Negative half-cycle:** Diode $D_2$ conducts. $C_1$ charges to $-(V_p - V_D)$ — it stores the negative peak.
- **Positive half-cycle:** Diode $D_1$ conducts. The source voltage $V_p$ is in **series** with the voltage on $C_1$ ($V_p - V_D$). The two add, charging $C_2$ through $D_1$ to:

$$V_{C2} = V_p - V_D + (V_p - V_D) = 2(V_p - V_D) \approx 2V_p$$

- The DC meter reads $\approx 2V_p$ — i.e., the **peak-to-peak** value.

**Derivation that output is $2V_p$ (2 marks):** As above, with $V_D \ll V_p$, the steady-state output across the meter is $2V_p$. This is called a *voltage doubler* because it doubles the peak input voltage.

**Comparison with Half-Wave type (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Half-Wave | Voltage Doubler |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Diodes / Capacitors | 1 / 1 | 2 / 2 |
| Output DC | $V_p$ | $2V_p$ |
| Measures | Peak | Peak-to-Peak |
| Recharge interval | $T$ | $T/2$ (less droop) |
| Calibration for RMS | $V_p/\sqrt{2}$ | $V_{pp}/(2\sqrt{2})$ |
| Common reference | Floating | Grounded (easier) |
| Use | General AC | Pulses, audio envelopes |

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice, KTU Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of operation of a **slide-back peak voltmeter**. Show that at null deflection, $V_p = V_{ref} + V_D$. Why is this method more accurate than the ordinary peak voltmeter? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A peak voltmeter uses a silicon diode ($V_D = 0.6$ V) and a holding capacitor of $0.01\,\mu F$ with a meter of resistance $10\text{ M}\Omega$. The input is a $1\text{ kHz}$ sinusoid of true peak $10$ V. Find (i) the droop voltage per cycle, (ii) the percentage error due to diode drop, and (iii) the indicated RMS value on a meter calibrated for sine waves. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Slide-Back Peak Voltmeter (7 marks)**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The slide-back voltmeter works on the **null-deflection method**. The unknown AC input is applied through a diode-capacitor peak detector. A known, adjustable DC reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is placed in **series-opposition** with the indicated DC voltage. $V_{ref}$ is gradually increased (slid back) until the DC meter reads **zero** (null).

**Derivation (3 marks):**
At balance, the meter's DC reading is the difference between the held peak and the reference:

$$V_m = V_p - V_{ref} - V_D = 0$$

Solving:

$$V_p = V_{ref} + V_D$$

**Why more accurate? (2 marks):**
- At null, **no current flows** through the diode. Hence the diode's I-V characteristics (non-linear, temperature-dependent) do not affect the reading.
- The diode drop $V_D$ is added algebraically, not multiplied by some non-linear function — so it is easily compensated.
- The reading depends only on the accuracy of the **DC reference standard** (a precision voltage source) — typically 0.05% or better.
- Suitable for **very high voltages** (kV range) where ordinary peak-voltmeter errors are intolerable.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $V_D = 0.6$ V, $C = 0.01\,\mu F = 10^{-8}$ F, $R_m = 10\text{ M}\Omega = 10^7\,\Omega$, $f = 1\text{ kHz}$, $V_p = 10$ V (true peak).

**(i) Droop voltage per cycle (3 marks):**

$$f R_m C = (10^3)(10^7)(10^{-8}) = 100$$

Droop:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_p}{f R_m C} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.1\text{ V}$$

**More accurate (exponential) value:**

$$\Delta V = V_p\left[1 - e^{-1/100}\right] \approx 0.0995 \text{ V} \approx 0.1 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Percentage error due to diode drop (2 marks):**

Indicated peak: $V_m = V_p - V_D = 10 - 0.6 = 9.4$ V

$$\varepsilon\% = \frac{V_p - V_m}{V_p} \times 100 = \frac{0.6}{10} \times 100 = 6\%$$

**(iii) Indicated RMS value (2 marks):**

Meter scale is calibrated assuming sinusoidal input, so:

$$V_{rms,\text{indicated}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{9.4}{1.414} = 6.647 \text{ V}$$

If we used the *true* RMS of the 10 V peak: $V_{rms,\text{true}} = 10/\sqrt{2} = 7.071$ V.

**Overall RMS error** = $(7.071 - 6.647)/7.071 \times 100 = 6\%$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Peak-Voltmeter Questions (KTU ESE 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the diode drop** $V_D \approx 0.6$ V — examiners specifically test whether you write $V_m = V_p - V_D$ rather than $V_m = V_p$. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Using full-wave formula for a half-wave circuit** (or vice-versa) — the output of a half-wave peak voltmeter is $V_p$, NOT $2V_p$. The $2V_p$ is for the **doubler**. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Calibration confusion** — the meter's scale reads RMS, but the *circuit* is detecting peak. Always write the relation: *Scale reading* = $V_p/\sqrt{2}$. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not drawing the discharge path** through $R_m$ — the droop equation $v_C(t) = V_p e^{-t/R_m C}$ requires identifying $R_m$ as the discharge resistance. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Confusing "peak" with "peak-to-peak"** — for a sinusoid, $V_{pp} = 2V_p$, not $V_p$. The voltage doubler reads $V_{pp}$; the half-wave circuit reads $V_p$. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Skipping the design criterion** $f R_m C \ge 100$ when asked for the choice of $C$. (–1 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Peak Response Voltmeter**

- **Definition:** Measures the *peak* (crest) value of an AC waveform by holding it on a capacitor via a series diode.
- **Key elements:** Series diode $D$ + shunt holding capacitor $C$ + DC indicating meter (D'Arsonval).
- **Half-wave output:** $V_m \approx V_p - V_D$ (single peak captured per cycle).
- **Full-wave / voltage-doubler output:** $V_m \approx 2V_p$ (peak-to-peak, $C_1$ charges to $-V_p$, $C_2$ to $2V_p$).
- **Discharge equation:** $v_C(t) = V_p e^{-t/(R_m C)}$.
- **Droop per cycle:** $\Delta V \approx V_p / (f R_m C)$.
- **Design rule:** $f R_m C \ge 100$ to keep droop $\le 1\%$.
- **Calibration:** Meter scale marked in RMS assuming sine: $V_{rms} = V_p / \sqrt{2}$.
- **Form factor** (sine): $K_f = 1.11$. **Crest factor** (sine): $K_a = 1.414$.
- **Slide-back method:** Null-deflection technique that **eliminates diode-drop error**; at null, $V_p = V_{ref} + V_D$.
- **Major errors:** (1) Diode forward drop, (2) capacitor droop, (3) input loading, (4) leakage, (5) frequency (junction capacitance).
- **Input impedance:** $\approx R_m/2$ — finite, so loading error must be checked: $\varepsilon\% = R_s / (R_s + R_{in}) \times 100$.
- **Best for:** High-frequency, audio envelopes, pulse and RF signals; gives correct *peak* but **incorrect RMS** for non-sinusoidal waveshapes.
- **Compared to true-RMS voltmeter:** Cheaper and simpler; less accurate for non-sinusoidal waveforms.
- **KTU-favourite exam hooks:** (1) Derive droop equation, (2) Design $C$ given $f$ and $R_m$, (3) Draw half-wave and doubler circuits and label all components, (4) Solve numerical with diode drop, (5) Compare slide-back with ordinary peak voltmeter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: True RMS Meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** True RMS Meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **True RMS Meter** is an electronic measuring instrument that accurately measures the **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value of any periodic, non-sinusoidal, or distorted alternating waveform by responding to the true heating value (thermal equivalent DC power) of the signal, rather than relying on the simple average value scaled by a fixed form factor (which is only valid for pure sinusoids).

Mathematically, the **True RMS value** of a periodic voltage $v(t)$ over a period $T$ is defined as:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A meter is called a "True RMS" meter only if it correctly measures the RMS value of **complex and distorted waveforms** such as square waves, triangular waves, SCR-controlled chopped sine waves, and noise-contaminated signals. An "Average-Responding" meter (scaled by 1.11) is NOT a true RMS meter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (The "Fire Hose" Analogy)

Imagine a firefighter trying to estimate the **average intensity** of a water jet from a fire hose that pulses on and off (mimicking an AC signal). 

- An **Average-Reading meter** would be like measuring the *average flow rate* of the water and assuming "this is the equivalent steady flow." It works perfectly if the pulsing is regular and known (like a sine wave), but it gives a **wrong reading** if the pulsing pattern changes (e.g., a fire hose pulsing in a square pattern or an irregular pulse).
- A **True RMS Meter** is like measuring the **actual kinetic energy carried by the water** (which is proportional to the *square* of the velocity). Since power dissipation is what damages real circuits and heats real resistors, only the RMS value (energy equivalent) truly represents the destructive or useful power of the waveform.

In electrical terms: **RMS = "DC equivalent heating value."** A 230 V AC RMS supply heats a resistor the same as a 230 V DC supply would — because the RMS value represents the equivalent DC power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Real-World Insight:** Power companies bill you in **kWh based on RMS values**, not peak or average values. Therefore, True RMS measurement is the only legally and technically valid method for energy metering in the presence of modern non-linear loads (computers, SMPS, LEDs, variable frequency drives).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Values

| Parameter | Symbol | Value (Pure Sine) |
|---|---|---|
| Form Factor | $K_f$ | **1.1107** |
| Crest Factor | $K_c$ | **1.4142** |
| RMS-to-Peak Ratio | $V_{rms}/V_m$ | **0.7071** |
| Average-to-Peak Ratio | $V_{avg}/V_m$ | **0.6366** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These constants apply **ONLY to pure, undistorted sinusoids**. The very reason True RMS meters exist is because these constants fail for non-sinusoidal waveforms.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Average-Responding Meters Fail — The Core Motivation

Most cheap analog multimeters (using a D'Arsonval movement with a bridge rectifier) do not measure RMS directly. Instead they:

1. Rectify the AC input to obtain $|v(t)|$.
2. Filter the rectified output to obtain the **average value** $V_{avg}$.
3. Multiply by the sine wave Form Factor $K_f = 1.11$ to display the reading.

The displayed value is therefore:
$$V_{displayed} = K_f \times V_{avg} = 1.11 \times \left(\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \vert v(t)\vert\, dt\right)$$

This is mathematically **WRONG** for any waveform other than a pure sine. For example:
- A **square wave** has a true RMS = its peak, but the meter will read **1.11 × Peak = 11% HIGH**.
- An **SCR phase-controlled waveform** (common in fan regulators, light dimmers) will read **very inaccurately** because both $V_{avg}$ and $V_{rms}$ change non-linearly with firing angle.

### 2.2 Fundamental Operating Principle of a True RMS Meter

A True RMS meter computes the integral:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

This is achieved in practice using one of three major circuit architectures:

#### Architecture A: Thermal (Heating) Method
- The unknown AC signal heats a thermistor or resistor.
- An identical DC reference heats a matched thermistor.
- A thermocouple or thermistor bridge compares the two temperatures.
- The DC reference is adjusted until both temperatures are equal.
- The DC voltage that produced the equal heating is, by definition, the **RMS value** of the AC signal.

#### Architecture B: Analog Computational (Multiplier-Based) Method
- The input signal is squared using an **analog multiplier** (e.g., AD633, MPY634).
- The squared signal is averaged using a low-pass filter (integrator).
- The average is fed to an **analog square-root circuit** to extract the RMS.
- This is the most common implementation in mid-range digital multimeters (DMMs).

#### Architecture C: Digital Sampling (DSP) Method
- The input waveform is sampled at high frequency (much greater than the Nyquist rate).
- Each sample $v_k$ is squared in firmware.
- The sum of squares is averaged and the square root is taken digitally:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_{k}^{2}}$$

This is the modern, most accurate method used in precision bench meters and energy analyzers.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the relationships below. KTU frequently asks 3-mark questions asking to derive the form factor for a given waveform.

| # | Formula | Description | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$ | Fundamental RMS definition | All periodic signals |
| 2 | $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^2}$ | Discrete digital RMS computation | Sampled waveforms |
| 3 | $K_f = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$ | Form Factor (shape factor) | All AC waveforms |
| 4 | $K_c = \dfrac{V_m}{V_{rms}}$ | Crest Factor (peak-to-RMS ratio) | All AC waveforms |
| 5 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS of pure sinusoid | Pure sine only |
| 6 | $V_{rms} = V_m$ | RMS of symmetric square wave | Symmetric square wave |
| 7 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{3}}$ | RMS of symmetric triangular wave | Symmetric triangle |
| 8 | $\%\, \text{Error} = \dfrac{V_{indicated} - V_{true\,rms}}{V_{true\,rms}} \times 100$ | Measurement error formula | Calibration problems |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Quality Analyzers:** Used in substations to measure distorted line voltages caused by harmonic injection from industrial loads.
- **Energy Meters (kWh meters):** Modern electronic energy meters for residential billing use True RMS computation to fairly bill consumers with non-linear loads (SMPS, LED drivers, computers).
- **Audio Engineering:** True RMS voltmeters measure program material (music, speech) correctly because audio signals are highly non-sinusoidal with crest factors often exceeding 3.
- **Vibration Analysis:** Accelerometer signals are pseudo-random — only True RMS gives meaningful vibration energy.
- **Noise Measurement:** Sound Level Meters (SLMs) almost universally use True RMS detection because noise is a stochastic (random) signal.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why RMS for Sinusoid Equals $V_m/\sqrt{2}$

**Step 1 — Set up the integral.**
For a pure sinusoid, the instantaneous voltage is $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ over one full period $T = 2\pi/\omega$.

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} V_{m}^{2}\sin^{2}(\omega t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity.**
Using the standard identity $\sin^{2}(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral into two parts.**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2T}\int_{0}^{T} 1\, dt \; - \; \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2T}\int_{0}^{T} \cos(2\omega t)\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each integral.**
- The first integral: $\int_{0}^{T} 1\, dt = T$.
- The second integral: $\int_{0}^{T} \cos(2\omega t)\, dt = \left[\dfrac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega}\right]_0^T = 0$, because $\sin(4\pi) = \sin(0) = 0$.

**Step 5 — Substitute back and simplify.**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2T}(T) - \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2T}(0) = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root.**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071\, V_m$$

This is the famous "0.707 rule" for sine waves. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Form Factor of a Full-Wave Rectified Sine

**Step 1 — Find the average value of $|V_m \sin(\omega t)|$ over a half-period.**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\left[-\cos(\theta)\right]_0^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + 1) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the RMS value from Section 3.1.**
$V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{V_m / \sqrt{2}}{2V_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.1107$$

This is the **1.11 multiplier** used in cheap average-responding meters. **Q.E.D.**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: True RMS for Common KTU Waveforms

| Waveform | Wave Equation | $V_m$ | $V_{avg}$ | $V_{rms}$ | $K_f$ | $K_c$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Sine | $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | $V_m$ | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | 1.11 | 1.414 |
| Square Wave | Symmetric $\pm V_m$ | $V_m$ | $V_m$ | $V_m$ | 1.00 | 1.000 |
| Triangular | Symmetric $\pm V_m$ | $V_m$ | $V_m/2$ | $V_m/\sqrt{3}$ | 1.155 | 1.732 |
| Half-Wave Rectified Sine | $V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $0<\omega t<\pi$, else 0 | $V_m$ | $V_m/\pi$ | $V_m/2$ | 1.571 | 2.000 |
| Sawtooth Wave | Linear ramp | $V_m$ | $V_m/2$ | $V_m/\sqrt{3}$ | 1.155 | 1.732 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For the **Half-Wave Rectified Sine**, note that $K_f = 1.57$. An average-responding meter would give a reading of $1.11 \times (V_m/\pi) = 0.353 V_m$, but the true RMS is $0.5 V_m$. The **error is huge (≈ 41.4%)** — this is a classic KTU numerical problem.

---

### 3.4 Error Analysis: Average Meter Reading a Square Wave

**Problem Setup (typical 7-mark KTU question):**
A D'Arsonval meter calibrated for sine waves (i.e., scaled by 1.11) is used to measure a symmetric square wave of amplitude 10 V. What does the meter read, and what is the percentage error?

**Step 1 — True RMS of the square wave.**
For a symmetric square wave of amplitude $V_m$, the signal is always $\pm V_m$, so $v^2(t) = V_m^2$ for all $t$:

$$V_{rms,true} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T V_m^2\, dt} = V_m = 10\text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — What the average meter reads.**
The meter first computes the average of $|v(t)|$, which for a symmetric square wave is simply:

$$V_{avg} = V_m = 10\text{ V}$$

Then the meter multiplies by 1.11 (sine calibration):

$$V_{indicated} = 1.11 \times 10 = 11.1\text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the percentage error.**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{V_{indicated} - V_{true\,rms}}{V_{true\,rms}} \times 100 = \frac{11.1 - 10}{10} \times 100 = +11\%$$

The meter reads **11% too high** for a square wave. **Q.E.D.**

> [!NOTE]
> This is a direct KTU-style numerical. Practice substituting different waveforms (triangle, half-wave) and computing the corresponding error to be fully prepared.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Analog Computational (Multiplier-Based) True RMS Meter

This is the most commonly drawn diagram in KTU examinations. The signal path is: Square → Average → Square Root.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Input Signal v of t"] --> SQ1["Squaring Circuit using Analog Multiplier AD633"]
    SQ1 --> LPF["Integrator and Low Pass Filter for Averaging"]
    LPF --> SQR["Square Root Extractor using Log Amplifier or Op Amp Divider"]
    SQR --> DISP["Analog or Digital Display Indicating True RMS"]
    REF["Precision DC Reference"] --> CAL["Calibration Trim"]
    CAL --> SQ1
    CAL --> LPF
    CAL --> SQR
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **DC Reference** is used during factory calibration. A known DC voltage is applied, the squarer/averager/sqrt-chain is trimmed so the display reads exactly that DC value, ensuring end-to-end accuracy.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Digital Sampling (DSP-Based) True RMS Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AIN["Analog Input Signal v of t"] --> ATT["Input Attenuator and Range Selector"]
    ATT --> AAF["Anti Aliasing Analog Low Pass Filter"]
    AAF --> ADC["High Speed Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    ADC --> MCU["Microcontroller or DSP Core"]
    MCU --> SQ2["Square Each Sample vk Squared in Firmware"]
    SQ2 --> ACC["Accumulate Sum of Squares over N Samples"]
    ACC --> AVG["Divide by N to Obtain Mean of Squares"]
    AVG --> ROOT["Compute Square Root in Software"]
    ROOT --> LCD["LCD Display Showing True RMS Value"]
    CLK["Crystal Clock Source"] --> ADC
    CLK --> MCU
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Thermal (Thermocouple-Based) True RMS Meter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SIG["Unknown AC Input"] --> R1["Heating Resistor R1"]
    R1 --> TC1["Thermocouple Junction T1"]
    DC["Adjustable DC Reference"] --> R2["Matched Heating Resistor R2"]
    R2 --> TC2["Matched Thermocouple T2"]
    TC1 --> BR["Thermocouple Bridge Comparator"]
    TC2 --> BR
    BR --> FB["Feedback Loop Adjusts DC Reference"]
    FB --> DC
    BR --> M["Meter Movement Reads the DC Reference Voltage"]
    M --> DISP["Final Display Equivalently the True RMS of AC Input"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The thermal method is the **most fundamentally accurate** (it physically implements the heating definition of RMS) but is slow, expensive, and sensitive to ambient temperature drift. It is used primarily in **laboratory standard meters** and **calibration benches**.

---

### 4.4 Comparative Functional Architecture: True RMS vs Average-Responding Meter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TRMS["TRUE RMS METER PATH"]
        direction LR
        X1["Input v of t"] --> Y1["Square v of t Squared"] --> Z1["Average over T"] --> W1["Square Root"] --> OUT1["True RMS Reading"]
    end
    subgraph AVG["AVERAGE RESPONDING METER PATH"]
        direction LR
        X2["Input v of t"] --> Y2["Full Wave Rectifier"] --> Z2["Average over T"] --> W2["Multiply by Fixed Kf = 1.11"] --> OUT2["Approximate RMS Reading"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define True RMS value. Why is a True RMS meter preferred over an average-responding meter for measuring non-sinusoidal waveforms?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **True RMS (Root Mean Square) value** of a periodic voltage $v(t)$ is the square root of the mean of the square of the signal, taken over one full period:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

It represents the **DC equivalent heating value** of the AC signal. A True RMS meter is preferred over an average-responding meter for non-sinusoidal waveforms because the latter assumes a fixed Form Factor $K_f = 1.11$, which is valid **only for pure sinusoids**. For square, triangular, or distorted (e.g., SCR-chopped) waveforms, the form factor deviates significantly from 1.11, leading to large measurement errors. A True RMS meter computes the integral directly and therefore gives correct readings for **any waveform shape**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Form Factor and Crest Factor. State their values for a pure sinusoidal waveform.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Form Factor ($K_f$):** The ratio of RMS value to the average value of the rectified waveform: $K_f = V_{rms}/V_{avg}$.
- **Crest Factor ($K_c$):** The ratio of peak (maximum) value to the RMS value: $K_c = V_m / V_{rms}$.

For a pure sinusoidal waveform:
- $K_f = \pi / (2\sqrt{2}) = \mathbf{1.1107}$
- $K_c = \sqrt{2} = \mathbf{1.4142}$

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the True RMS value of a full-wave rectified sinusoidal waveform. Compute the Form Factor and Crest Factor for the same. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an analog computational type True RMS meter. List its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The full-wave rectified sine has waveform: $v(t) = V_m \vert \sin(\omega t)\vert$ for all $t$.

**Step 1:** Because the waveform is symmetric, the integration over $T$ is twice the integration over $T/2$ (or equivalently, over $[0, \pi]$ in $\theta = \omega t$).

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi} V_m^{2}\sin^{2}(\theta)\, d\theta$$

**Step 2:** Use the identity $\sin^{2}\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_m^{2}}{\pi}\int_0^{\pi}\frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}\, d\theta = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2\pi}\left[\pi - 0\right] = \frac{V_m^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Take the square root:

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}}$$

(Note: Full-wave rectification does not change the RMS value — same as the original sine.)

**Step 4:** The average value is $V_{avg} = 2V_m/\pi$. Therefore:

$$K_f = \frac{V_m/\sqrt{2}}{2V_m/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.1107$$

$$K_c = \frac{V_m}{V_m/\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} = 1.4142$$

---

**Part (b) — Block Diagram and Explanation:**

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for the diagram.)*

**Working Principle:**
1. The input AC signal is fed to a **wideband analog squaring circuit** built using an IC multiplier (e.g., AD633). The output is $v^2(t)$.
2. The squared signal passes through an **active low-pass filter / integrator** with a time constant much greater than the signal period. This produces the time average $\overline{v^2(t)}$.
3. The averaged DC is fed to a **square-root extractor** (using logarithmic amplifiers or a divider-feedback op-amp circuit), giving $\sqrt{\overline{v^2(t)}} = V_{rms}$.
4. The final DC output drives a calibrated display.

**Advantages:**
- Accurate for any waveform shape.
- Moderate cost and complexity.
- Reasonable bandwidth (up to several hundred kHz with modern ICs).

**Limitations:**
- Limited dynamic range (typically 40–60 dB).
- Sensitive to component drift; requires periodic calibration.
- Bandwidth limited by the analog multiplier.
- Errors due to DC offset and temperature drift in the squarer.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** A symmetrical square wave has an amplitude of 15 V. An average-responding meter (calibrated for sine waves, $K_f = 1.11$) is used to measure it. Calculate: (i) the True RMS value, (ii) the indicated value on the meter, and (iii) the percentage error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the thermal type True RMS meter with a block diagram. Compare it with the analog computational type. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) True RMS of square wave:**
For a symmetric square wave of amplitude $V_m$, $v^2(t) = V_m^2$ for all $t$:

$$V_{rms,true} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T V_m^2\, dt} = V_m = 15\text{ V}$$

**(ii) Indicated value on average-responding meter:**
The average of the rectified square wave equals its peak:

$$V_{avg} = V_m = 15\text{ V}$$

The meter multiplies by 1.11:

$$V_{indicated} = 1.11 \times 15 = 16.65\text{ V}$$

**(iii) Percentage error:**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{16.65 - 15}{15}\times 100 = +11\%$$

The meter reads **11% high**.

---

**Part (b) — Thermal True RMS Meter:**

*(Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid block diagram.)*

**Working:** The unknown AC signal is applied to a heating resistor $R_1$ in thermal contact with thermocouple $T_1$. A variable DC reference is applied to a matched resistor $R_2$ and thermocouple $T_2$. A servo loop adjusts the DC reference until both thermocouples indicate the same temperature. The DC voltage that produced equal heating equals the **True RMS** of the AC by definition of RMS (heating equivalence). The DC value is read on a calibrated D'Arsonval meter.

**Comparison Table (for the answer):**

| Feature | Thermal Type | Analog Computational Type |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Heating equivalence | $v^2 \rightarrow$ avg $\rightarrow \sqrt{\cdot}$ |
| Accuracy | Very high (fundamental) | Moderate (limited by IC linearity) |
| Speed | Slow (thermal lag ~seconds) | Fast (real-time) |
| Cost | Very high | Moderate |
| Bandwidth | Wide (depends on heater) | Limited by multiplier |
| Use case | Lab standards, calibration | General-purpose DMMs |
| Temperature sensitivity | High (needs compensation) | Low |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RMS and Average definitions.** Many students write $V_{rms} = \frac{1}{T}\int v(t)\, dt$ (which is the average). Always include the **square root of the mean of the square** in your definition. Marks lost: 1–2.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the form factor depends on waveform shape.** Students write "$K_f = 1.11$" as if it were a universal constant. It is **only** valid for pure sine. For square wave it is 1.0, for triangle it is 1.155. Marks lost: 1.
>
> 3. **Numerical unit errors.** Always retain units (V, A) throughout the calculation. The final answer must include the unit. Marks lost: 0.5.
>
> 4. **Block diagram mistakes.** Do not draw an "envelope detector" and label it as RMS. The block diagram must clearly show **Squarer → Averager → Square-root extractor**. Marks lost: 1–2.
>
> 5. **Mixing up Crest Factor and Form Factor.** Recall: $K_f = V_{rms}/V_{avg}$ (RMS ÷ Average) and $K_c = V_m/V_{rms}$ (Peak ÷ RMS). Marks lost: 1.
>
> 6. **Not specifying the integration limit as a full period $T$.** Always write $\int_0^T$, not $\int_0^\infty$. Marks lost: 0.5.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **True RMS = "DC equivalent heating value"** of any periodic signal.
- [ ] Mathematical definition: $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$.
- [ ] For **pure sine**: $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071\, V_m$.
- [ ] For **square wave**: $V_{rms} = V_m$ (same as peak!).
- [ ] For **triangle wave**: $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{3}$.
- [ ] **Form Factor** $K_f = V_{rms}/V_{avg} = 1.11$ (sine only).
- [ ] **Crest Factor** $K_c = V_m/V_{rms} = \sqrt{2}$ (sine only).
- [ ] Average-responding meter **fails** for non-sinusoidal signals (square, SCR, triangle, noise).
- [ ] Three architectures: **Thermal**, **Analog Computational** (squarer/averager/√), **Digital Sampling** (DSP).
- [ ] Block diagram sequence is always: **Square → Average → Square Root**.
- [ ] Digital implementation: $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} v_k^2}$.
- [ ] Applications: power-quality analyzers, electronic kWh energy meters, audio metering, vibration analysis, sound level meters.
- [ ] Cheaper multimeters are average-responding, scaled by 1.11 — they lie on square waves.
- [ ] For a square wave of amplitude $V_m$, an average-responding meter reads $1.11 V_m$ (an 11% high error).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strip Chart Recorder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET611 - TRANSDUCERS AND MEASUREMENTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strip Chart Recorder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strip Chart Recorder (SCR):** An analog recording instrument in which one or more measured variables are recorded as a function of **time** on a paper chart that moves at a constant linear velocity past a stationary (or near-stationary) writing element. The chart is supplied in the form of a long **continuous strip** wound on a supply roll and rewound on a take-up roll after recording.

The defining KTU-level characteristics are:

- **Time-base recording:** The independent (horizontal) axis is **time (t)**, produced by the uniform motion of the paper.
- **Analog trace:** The dependent (vertical) axis is proportional to the **measured signal** (voltage, current, temperature, pressure, etc., after signal conditioning).
- **Strip length:** Typically **50 m to 100 m** of paper, allowing unattended, long-duration monitoring of slowly varying processes.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme distinguishes the Strip Chart Recorder from the **X-Y Recorder** (where the X-axis is *not* time but another variable) and from the **Circular Chart Recorder** (which uses a rotating disc instead of a strip).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Continuous ECG"

Imagine an **ECG machine** in a hospital: a long roll of graph paper slides steadily under a heated stylus. Every heartbeat deflects the stylus vertically, leaving a continuous red/black trace that a doctor later scans for irregularities.

- The **paper moving left-to-right** = the **time axis** (one full sweep per second).
- The **stylus moving up-down** = the **measured signal** (electrical potential from the heart).
- The **speed of the paper** = the **time-base setting** (25 mm/s in a standard ECG).

A Strip Chart Recorder is, in essence, a **general-purpose, industrial-strength ECG** for any physical variable you can convert into an electrical signal.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics Used in This Topic

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and units recur throughout KTU problems on this topic:
> - **Paper speed** $v$ in **mm/s** (or **mm/h** for long-duration logs).
> - **Writing width** (full-scale deflection) typically **100 mm** or **250 mm**.
> - **Accuracy class** of industrial recorders: **±0.25 %** to **±1.0 %** of full scale.
> - **Frequency response** (servo type): up to **2.5 Hz to 10 Hz** at ±1 % error.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Subsystems of a Strip Chart Recorder

Every KTU 2024-scheme block diagram of a strip chart recorder contains the following five functional blocks, regardless of the writing technology used:

1. **Input / Signal Conditioning Stage**
   - Accepts the raw mV/mA signal from a transducer.
   - Provides **amplification**, **attenuation**, **filtering**, and **cold-junction compensation** (for thermocouples) or **bridge excitation** (for strain gauges).
   - The conditioned signal drives the deflection mechanism.

2. **Deflection / Marking Mechanism (Pen Drive)**
   - Converts the electrical signal into a **mechanical displacement** of the writing tip.
   - Two dominant architectures:
     - **(a) Galvanometric (Direct-Writing) Type** — uses a **D'Arsonval moving-coil galvanometer**. Light, low-inertia pointer drags an ink pen (or hot stylus) across the paper.
     - **(b) Servo / Potentiometric (Null-Balance) Type** — uses a **dc servomotor** driven by the difference between the input signal and the feedback from a precision **slide-wire potentiometer**.

3. **Writing Element**
   - May be a **fiber-tip ink pen**, a **heated stylus on thermal paper**, an **electric stylus on electrosensitive paper**, or a **pressure pen** on pressure-sensitive paper.
   - Choice depends on sample rate, environment, and frequency of trace.

4. **Chart Drive (Time Base)**
   - A **synchronous ac motor** (or a **stepper/dc servo** in precision models) drives the paper through a **gearbox** that selects the paper speed.
   - Typical selectable speeds: **1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200 mm/s** (or equivalent mm/h).
   - A **tachogenerator** or **optical encoder** provides speed feedback for closed-loop speed control.

5. **Reference Grid & Time Markers**
   - Pre-printed or simultaneously printed horizontal lines = **signal levels**.
   - Periodic "pips" or second marks = **time ticks**.
   - Together they form the **chart's graticule**.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Why the Two Architectures Differ

> [!TIP]
> The **galvanometric** type writes the *actual* deflection of a current-sensitive coil — fast but limited by coil inertia and pen friction. The **servo (null-balance)** type continuously *corrects* the pen position via a feedback loop — slower but vastly more accurate and capable of driving heavier pens.

### 2.2.1 Galvanometric Type — The "Why"

A current $I(t)$ flows in a coil of $N$ turns, area $A$, in a radial flux density $B$. The resulting **electromagnetic torque** deflects the coil against a control spring of stiffness $K_s$ until equilibrium. The pointer (and attached pen) settles at an angular position $\theta(t)$ directly proportional to $I(t)$.

- **Strength:** Wide bandwidth (up to 100 Hz at small deflections) — good for transient capture.
- **Weakness:** Pen friction introduces **dead-band** (typically ±0.5 % of full scale) and the high-inertia pen limits accuracy.

### 2.2.2 Servo (Potentiometric) Type — The "How"

The conditioned input signal $V_{in}$ is compared to the feedback voltage $V_{fb}$ tapped from a precision slide-wire. The error $e = V_{in} - V_{fb}$ is amplified by a high-gain dc amplifier and drives a **servomotor** coupled to the pen carriage and the slide-wire wiper. Motor rotation reduces $e$ to a near-zero value, positioning the pen at the slide-wire tap that is linearly proportional to $V_{in}$.

- **Strength:** Accuracy of **±0.25 %** or better; high open-loop gain forces $V_{fb} \approx V_{in}$ (the "null" condition).
- **Weakness:** Limited by motor slew rate — typical full-scale traverse time **0.2 s to 2 s**, giving an effective bandwidth of only a few Hz.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following equations verbatim for the ESE. Units are SI throughout.

| **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deflecting torque on galvanometer coil | $T_d = N \, B \, A \, I$ | $\text{N·m}$; $B$ in T, $A$ in m², $I$ in A |
| 2 | Controlling (spring) torque | $T_c = K_s \, \theta$ | $\text{N·m}$; $K_s$ in $\text{N·m/rad}$ |
| 3 | Steady-state deflection of galvanometer | $\theta_{ss} = \dfrac{N \, B \, A \, I}{K_s}$ | rad; linear in $I$ |
| 4 | Natural (undamped) angular frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_s}{J}}$ | rad/s; $J$ = moment of inertia of moving parts |
| 5 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{K_s \, J}}$ | dimensionless; $c$ = damping coefficient |
| 6 | Paper-speed to time-axis conversion | $t = \dfrac{x}{v}$ | s; $x$ = distance along strip in m, $v$ in m/s |
| 7 | Servo loop error at equilibrium | $V_{fb} \approx V_{in}$ | V; consequence of high open-loop gain $A$ |
| 8 | Static accuracy of null-balance recorder | $\%e = \dfrac{1}{A \beta + 1} \times 100$ | %; $\beta$ = feedback factor (slide-wire ratio) |
| 9 | Number of chart divisions recorded per hour | $N_{div} = \dfrac{v \, \times 3600}{\text{div width in mm}}$ | divisions/h; for log-period calculation |
| 10 | Trace width — frequency trade-off | $f_{-3\,\text{dB}} \approx \dfrac{0.35}{\tau_{pen}}$ | Hz; $\tau_{pen}$ = pen rise time in s |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!NOTE]
> Strip Chart Recorders are not obsolete — they remain standard in:
> - **Process control rooms** for long-term temperature, pressure and level trending (paper provides a legally admissible, tamper-evident record required by FDA 21 CFR Part 11 in pharma).
> - **Power plant boiler drum** level and steam pressure logging (servo-type, 1 mA/0–100 % input).
> - **Geophysical and seismic** stations (galvanometric, ink-pen, multi-channel).
> - **Avionics and flight-test data acquisition** (thermal paper, high paper speeds up to **500 mm/s**).
> - **Medical telemetry** (ECG, EEG, EMG — modern versions use heated-stylus on thermal paper).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No "it can be shown that" shortcuts. The KTU 2024 ESE values full, transparent working.

## 3.1 Derivation I — Steady-State Deflection of the Galvanometer Strip-Chart Recorder

We start from the fundamental **torque-balance equation** of a D'Arsonval movement with viscous damping.

### Step 1 — Free-body diagram of the moving coil

The coil experiences three torques:

$$
T_d(t) \;=\; T_c(t) \;+\; T_{\text{damp}}(t) \;+\; J\,\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Newton's second law for rotation — the net electromagnetic driving torque is balanced by the spring's restoring torque, the viscous damping torque, and the inertial reaction of the rotating assembly.

### Step 2 — Substitute each torque

Using the constitutive relations $T_d = N B A \, I(t)$, $T_c = K_s \theta(t)$, and $T_{\text{damp}} = c \, \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$:

$$
N B A \, I(t) \;=\; K_s \, \theta(t) \;+\; c \, \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} \;+\; J \, \dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is a **linear, second-order ordinary differential equation** with constant coefficients.

### Step 3 — Rearrange into the standard second-order form

$$
